observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a) Require the contractors of the five on-going infrastructure projects to refund to the Municipality the retention money totaling P929,823.76 that should have been deducted to the payments for progress billing or the contractors may request to substitute the same with valid financial instruments equivalent to the retention money which may be accepted by the Municipality as long as the projects are on schedule and satisfactorily undertaken; b) Require the contractors of the seven completed infrastructure projects which had not yet been issued with Certificates of Acceptance to refund to the Municipality the retention money totaling P717,618.38 that should have been deducted to the payments for progress billing or the contractors may request to substitute the same with valid financial instruments equivalent to the retention money posted in favor of the Municipality; c) Require the contractors of the 18 completed infrastructure projects which had already been issued with Certificates of Acceptance to submit valid financial instruments equivalent to the retention money totaling P2,033,971.13 which should have a validity period of one year from the date of issuance of the Certificates of Completion; and d) Strictly impose the deduction of the 10 percent retention money to the payments to contractors or require the submission of valid financial instrument equivalent to the retention money in case the contractor has requested for its substitution which will be accepted only if the project is on schedule and satisfactorily undertaken, and to release the retention money to contractors upon final acceptance of the project, in compliance with Section 6 of the 2016 RIRR of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction: Demand letters were sent to the contractors on February 29, 2024. The amount of P6,606,882.17 was refunded and retention money as deducted from final payment of one completed project will be deducted from final payment of four completed but not yet paid infrastructure projects. Defects Liability Period had already expired for several projects.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the General Services Officer to prepare the ICS for the semi-expendable properties totaling P892,157.74 which were issued to the end-users in CY 2023 to properly establish the accountability on said assets.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer to prepare ICS on all issuances of semi-expendable properties and to prepare and maintain the RegSPI for each class of issued semi-expendable properties to ensure that the accountability on such assets is properly established and monitored, in compliance with Sections 4.1, 4.7.3, and 4.7.4 of the COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Officer prepared the ICS for issuances and submitted RegSPI. The Municipal Mayor instructed GSO to prepare the ICS and RegSPI. The items were reconciled and ICS prepared.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Inventory Committee to conduct annual physical count of all properties of the Municipality; b. Municipal General Services Officer (GSO) to maintain property cards for each class of property, plant and equipment which shall be used as one of bases for the preparation of a comprehensive and reliable RPCPPE; and c. Municipal GSO and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their respective records to come up with the reconciled balances of PPE. Should reconciliation of the RPCPPE and accounting records appeared to be improbable, consider availing the one-time cleansing of PPE in accordance with Commission on Audit Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality Not Implemented The annual physical count for PPE was not completed. The property cards maintained by the Municipal GSO were not up to date, while some PPEs still had no property cards. As of December 31, 2024, there was still a net discrepancy totaling P139,666,584.16 between the records of the Municipal Accounting Office and the submitted RPCPPE. Reiterated with modifications in Audit Observation No. 2, Page Nos. 40 to 43, Part II of this Report. For preparation of the Recommendation Tracking Sheet (RTS) for the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Local Finance Committee to recommend fiscal policies and measures to improve the financial performance of the Municipality; and b. Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to monitor the expenditures of the Municipality and ensure that they do not exceed the estimated income for the year and the expenditures did not exceed its revenues.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality realized its estimated income for the year and the expenditures did not exceed its revenues. The Municipality implemented the recommendations and the financial performance of the Municipality had improved during the year, evidenced by a surplus amounting to P7,640,638.82, as shown in the Statement of Financial Performance, compared with last year’s net loss.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Finance Committee to adopt measures to eliminate the cash overdraft by exercising diligence, prudence and proper control in the disposition of the Municipality’s funds and limit expenditures within the total income realized and collected during the year and to reduce unnecessary operating expenditures until the cash overdraft is eliminated. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Finance Committee to make a thorough analysis of the tax and non-tax revenues collected and realized for the previous year and use the result of such analysis as basis in the preparation of an attainable Annual Budget for the ensuing fiscal year.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality realized its estimated income for the year and the expenditures did not exceed its revenues. However, the cash overdraft originated from the previous years’ obligations and transactions of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. Concerned officials and employees to immediately liquidate their respective cash advances as soon as the purpose for which the advances were granted has been served by submitting the required Liquidation Reports and necessary supporting documents to the Municipal Accounting Office; settle their outstanding cash advances as of year-end due and observe strict adherence to the rules and regulations in the granting, utilization and liquidation of the cash advances; b. Municipal Accountant to ensure that additional grant of cash advances to officers and employees is processed only if their previous cash advances have already been accounted and settled, and that all must be liquidated by the end of the year; and c. Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to refrain from using cash advances to pay regular expenses and instead issue checks directly under the names of the payees, suppliers or contractors.
observations.managementAction: Reiterated with modifications in Audit Observation No. 7, Page Nos. 54 to 56, Part II of this Report. For preparation of the RTS for the recommendation. Considered closed having been reiterated with modifications.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the bank reconciliation statements and submit copies thereof, with all of the necessary supporting documents, to the Audit Team for verification within ten days after the end of each month, and to draw journal entry vouchers to take up all valid reconciling items requiring adjustments and corrections in the books, to establish the reliability and accuracy of the Cash in Bank accounts.
observations.managementAction: Implemented. The Municipality prepared bank reconciliation statements and submitted to COA Auditor on a monthly basis together with necessary supporting documents. Journal entry vouchers were drawn to record valid reconciling items and correct balances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the concerned Municipal officials expedite the implementation of the programs and projects for which the transferred funds are intended, or should it be determined that the projects are not anymore feasible or the intended programs or projects had no more use for the funds, to instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to immediately remit the P1,502,614.44 to the respective source agencies or the Bureau of Treasury.
observations.managementAction: Implemented. The unexpended balances were reverted/ remitted as appropriate. The Manila Bay Rehabilitation was completed on December 19, 2024. Construction of National Development Center was completed on May 28, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Municipal General Services Officer to maintain stock cards to monitor and control the procurement and issuance of supplies and materials, prepare the monthly SSMI at the end of every month summarizing all the approved RISs, submit them to the Municipal Accountant as the basis for recording the expense account and conduct complete physical count of all inventory items on a semestral basis and submit the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Inventory (RPCI) not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively; b. Municipal Accountant to record all deliveries of supplies and materials in the appropriate ledger cards upon the receipt of the Inspection and Acceptance Report and other supporting documents then prepare the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to take up (1) the procurement made in the books, and (2) the expenditures/issuance made based on the submitted SSMI at the end of each month.
observations.managementAction: Implemented. Municipal GSO to maintain stock cards; monthly SSMI to be prepared and submitted to Municipal Accountant; physical inventory to be conducted semestrally. Municipal Accountant to record deliveries and prepare JEVs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MHRMO, in coordination with the Department Heads of the Municipality, to: a. ensure that the contracts of the JO clearly provide their job descriptions, the duties and expected deliverables against which their performance and accomplishment would be evaluated; b. study the Municipality's manpower needs and hire JO workers based on specific requests from concerned Department Heads or officials who shall certify that his or her office needs the services of a JO worker for an identified piece of work.
observations.managementAction: The hiring of JO workers were based on the specific request of concerned Department Heads. Reiterated with modifications in Audit Observation No. 12, Page Nos. 67 to 70, Part II of this Report. For preparation of the RTS for the recommendation. Considered closed having been reiterated with modifications.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: GAD Chairperson of the Technical Working Group – GAD Focal Point Systems (TWG-GFPS) to conduct the gender analysis through the administration of HGDG tool from the planning and budgeting phase to the implementation, monitoring and evaluation phase, apply the results thereof in the preparation of the GAD Plan and Budget and the accompanying GAD Accomplishment Report and the identification of strategies to address gaps or missing elements, if any, to properly determine and gradually increase the gender-responsiveness of the GAD programs and activities and ensure that gender issues were addressed accordingly.
observations.managementAction: Implemented. Gender analysis was conducted using HGDG tool for planning and budgeting phase to the implementation, monitoring and evaluation phase of the GAD project, program and activities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the General Service Officer (GSO) to proceed with the preliminary activities on the One-Time Cleansing of PPE, particularly the listing and comparison of PPE items between the latest Report of Physical Count of PPE and PPELCs but excluding the lump-sum PPE balances in the latter report from the inventory working papers, and henceforth follow the guidelines provided under Commission on Audit (COA) Circular No. 2020-006 to properly address the unreconciled balances on PPE records.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that the reconciliation of GL, PPELC, and RCPPE is still ongoing. The Municipal Accountant also mentioned that their analysis of the cost and nature of the work done for the restoration of the Training Center’s roofing indicated that it should be capitalized. Accordingly, the JEV dated April 30, 2025, was submitted for the necessary adjustment. Additionally, she provided JEVs, all dated April 30, 2025, for the reversal of entries related to the derecognition of various PPE accounts.
observations.reason: Attended seminars on the proper conduct of the One-Time Cleansing of PPE.

observations.managementAction: Management ensured that proper and timely recording and monitoring of inventory receipts and issuances using the weighted average cost method. She also furnished the Audit Team with duly signed copy of JEVs, both dated, April 30, 2025 for adjustments to the Chemical and Filtering Supplies Inventory and Other Supplies Inventory.

Management commented that the Municipal Engineer submitted a document signed and issued by the Division Manager of NIA – Cavite-Batangas Irrigation Management Office stating no objection on the use of the subject parcel of Land in Barangay Molino, where a farm to market road was constructed. She also replied that that they will coordinate with the donor of 57 units solar streetlights for the copy of corresponding Deed of Donation. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant responded that they will promptly coordinate with the Appraisal Committee upon receipt of the Deeds of Donation for the subject properties, ensuring proper valuation and recording.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entry to restore the derecognized Operating Lease Receivable amounting to P486,000.00. Likewise, if warranted, record the amount of P117,300.00 as receivable and submit the corresponding Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to the Audit Team for review and validation. Henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 on the monitoring of receivables and de-recognition thereof;
b. Market Supervisor to provide the Municipal Accountant with the details of the uncollected receivables totaling P603,300.00 together with the pertinent documents such as contracts, demand letters, among others; and
c. Municipal Accountant, after evaluation of the pertinent documents and in coordination with the Market Supervisor, to assess the collectability of the subject receivables and to provide the corresponding impairment, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that the Municipal Accountant submitted a duly signed JEV, dated April 30, 2025, for the reversal of entries made for the unauthorized derecognition of Operating Lease Receivable amounting to P486,000.00. The Market Supervisor also responded that she will coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to discuss the correct procedures and documentary requirements required for writing off of the long outstanding receivables.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the Provincial Government to determine the origin and details of the reported discrepancies between the reciprocal accounts Due from LGUs and Due to LGUs, and make necessary adjustments to ensure that the balances of both reciprocal accounts are reconciled; and
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to furnish the Provincial Accountant with copies of the weekly summary of the JEVs showing the breakdown of amounts collected by the Municipality due to the Provincial Government, as well as a copy of the monthly Abstract of the RPT to facilitate reconciliation with the weekly summary of JEVs.
observations.managementAction: The Management explained that the discrepancy of P323,259.49 was due to an unrecorded share from SET Receivable collections by the Provincial Treasurer's Office (PTO). They had communicated with the Provincial Accounting Office before the finalization of the CY 2024 Financial Statements, however, the latter was unable to provide the necessary details as they were not yet available at that time. She further stated that a journal entry had already been made to account for the discrepancy upon receipt of the share from the PTO on March 31, 2025, as evidenced by JEV No. 221-2025-03-01590.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all supporting documents are in her possession and properly evaluated for validity of the claim, particularly if goods/services are actually delivered/rendered, before recognizing and recording these transactions as AP.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant admitted that there was negligence on their part due to bulk transactions during year end to which some documents were not thoroughly checked. However, they have assured to be careful in evaluating supporting documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
1. Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
• Validate the noted discrepancies on the issued semi-expendable properties, supplies and PPE totaling P1,068,613.95 that were recorded under Semi-Expendable Property as of December 31, 2024. Once validated, make the necessary adjusting entries and submit the duly signed copy of Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to the Audit Team for review and validation;
• Properly prepare the SPLCs for all purchased semi-expendable properties, including among others, details pertaining to receipts and issuance thereof; and
2. Direct the General Services Officer to prepare and maintain complete SPCs and RegSPI for each class of issued semi-expendable properties in accordance with the pertinent provisions of COA Circular Nos. 2022-004 and 2024-006 to ensure proper monitoring of all issued Semi-Expendable Properties.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that the Municipal Accountant acknowledged that they overlooked checking the status and condition of the semi-expendable properties. Two JEVs, both dated April 30, 2025, for the necessary adjustments were submitted to the Audit Team and were found in order. Moreover, the General Services Officer acknowledged that they have recently begun reconciling records involving SPC and RegSPI and assured that proper monitoring of materials is being carried out under her close supervision, as evidenced by the existing ICS on file and the monitoring logbook, both readily available for review. She further stated that all necessary actions are being taken to ensure that backlogs are fully reconciled at the earliest possible time.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to: i) refrain from processing and granting additional cash advance/s to Accountable Officers (AOs) unless their previous cash advances have been fully accounted for or liquidated; ii) refrain from issuing certification that the previous cash advance of the concerned AO have been liquidated, unless the same have been verified; and iii) ensure that all cash advances, except petty cash fund, are liquidated at year-end;
b. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to provide the names of personnel who are qualified and could be designated as additional AOs to serve as Special Disbursing Officers (SDOs); and
c. Require every AO who will receive any cash advance to strictly observe the prescribed liquidation period of his/her cash advance consistent with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant responded that certification that no outstanding cash advance were given to Accountable Officers on the basis that his/her cash advance for specific program was liquidated and just learned that the same was not in accordance with the guidelines. However, she agreed with the audit recommendations and ensure compliance thereto. She further mentioned that constant reminders were given to the AOs for timely liquidation of cash advances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal HRMO to:
a. Submit written justification on why the three individuals were hired as consultants of the Municipality to render services which can be rendered by its regular plantilla personnel or which were similar with the duties and functions of vacant plantilla positions, to avoid the issuance of audit disallowance;
b. Evaluate the Municipality’s Plantilla of Personnel and to submit recommendations before hiring an individual under Consultancy Service Contract to avoid the incurrence of additional cost to the Municipality due to hiring of consultants for services which could be rendered by its pool of plantilla personnel, pursuant to Section 2 of Annex “B” of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and
c. Evaluate the necessity of filling the vacancies, abolishment, and/or creation of plantilla positions to address the lack of available manpower for services which are needed by the Municipality and its constituents.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that the Municipal HRMO acknowledged that they overlooked checking the status and condition of the semi-expendable properties. Two JEVs, both dated April 30, 2025, for the necessary adjustments were submitted to the Audit Team and were found in order. Moreover, the General Services Officer acknowledged that they have recently begun reconciling records involving SPC and RegSPI and assured that proper monitoring of materials is being carried out under her close supervision, as evidenced by the existing ICS on file and the monitoring logbook, both readily available for review. She further stated that all necessary actions are being taken to ensure that backlogs are fully reconciled at the earliest possible time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to check the relevant records pertaining to the Due to GSIS balance of P201,343.56 and if necessary, to make coordination with the GSIS for the proper settlement of any balance determined to be overdue unremitted premiums.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that the Municipal Government had previously constituted an Inventory and Disposal Committee but some of its members are no longer associated with the Municipality. To address this, they are in the process of issuing a new Executive Order (EO) indicating the new members of disposal committee and dividing the existing Inventory and Disposal Committee. He also mentioned that the Local Government expresses its commitment to willfully and faithfully comply with the provisions and requirements of the DBM-COA JMC No. 2024-1 or the Revised Manual on the Disposal of Government Properties.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Market Supervisor to:
a. Prepare and issue Contracts of Lease to the remaining 106 market stallholders and 17 Grand Central Terminal stallholders to secure the interest of the Municipality and to categorically inform the stallholders of their rights and obligations including the revocation of right for those with more than three months unpaid stall fees;
b. Investigate and require the stallholders who sub-leased their stalls to explain their action and submit the report thereon to the Audit Team and henceforth, strictly enforce the provisions of the Market Code on the restriction on sub-leasing of market stalls and revoke the lease of non-complying stallholders; and
c. Advertise and offer to the public all stalls which had been idle and abandoned for more than 30 days and strictly follow the process of awarding of stalls as stated in Section 40 of the Revised Municipal Market Code.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that the Market Supervisor prepared and submitted Contracts of Lease to the remaining stallholders and that investigations and coordination with stallholders who sub-leased were undertaken. The Market Supervisor also stated that she had talked to stallholders who have been sub-leasing their stall to explain their action and that vacant stalls had been advertised to the public to entice occupancy.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to ensure that projects under the 20 percent DF are backed up with available funds to guarantee their implementation; b. Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Municipal Engineer and other concerned officials to continue their efforts to fully create and establish the GAD M&E Team and System; c. Municipal Engineer and other concerned officials to conduct detailed engineering and in-depth planning for each project to ensure that the priority development projects are feasible for implementation.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer opened a separate depository account with the Landbank of the Philippines (LBP)-Cavite City Branch in the latter part of CY 2024 exclusively for the 20 percent DF to ensure the availability of funds for such purpose. The Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant agreed to comply and continue efforts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: a. Municipal Accountant, Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and other concerned officials to submit to the COA Audit Team all lacking documents relative to the disbursements for the Noveleta Information Technology Building and Purchase of Heavy Equipment (Backhoe), otherwise a Notice of Suspension will be issued; b. BAC to use the prescribed Philippine Bidding Documents (PBD) and other standard forms issued by the Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB) whenever necessary; and c. BAC to explain in writing to the COA Audit Team, for further evaluation, the inability to invite three observers in all stages of the procurement process, and their failure to conduct pre-bid conference, bid evaluation, and post-qualification as required under the pertinent provisions of Revised IRR of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction: The BAC and concerned officials submitted the required documents to the COA Audit Team on May 23 and June 6, 2024. The required justifications and forms were provided and verified during subsequent review.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Municipal Accountant and other responsible officials to submit to the COA Audit Team for evaluation all lacking documents and justifications on why the procurement and distribution was charged out of the 20 percent DF, otherwise a Notice of Disallowance will be issued accordingly; and b. Municipal Accountant to cause the submission to the COA Audit Team for evaluation the required justification and documentation such as the approved program design containing the rationale, objective and target beneficiaries, list of recipients with names, addresses and signatures, and the description and quantity of the items received and pictures during distribution.
observations.managementAction: The Local Finance Committee, Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and Municipal Accountant submitted the required justifications and documentation to the COA Audit Team on June 6, 2024. The BAC explained the procurement charged to 20% DF and provided program design and recipient lists.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Enjoin the Municipal Budget Officer, the Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to provide valid explanation or justification on the circumstances behind the disbursements beyond the appropriations amounting to P4,090,584.58 for evaluation of the Audit Team, otherwise a Notice of Disallowance will be issued accordingly; b. Direct the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to ensure that the actual expenditures do not exceed the budget for each class/fund and that they are utilized solely for the purpose for which they were created/intended; and c. Require the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to closely monitor the balances of appropriations and to advise the concerned officials and employees to stop the incurrence of obligations when appropriations are no longer available for the purpose.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and concerned officials provided explanations and supporting documentation. Close monitoring of obligations and appropriations was adopted going forward.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Health Officer, and other responsible officials to: a. Cause the immediate liquidation of the HEA benefits in compliance with the pertinent provisions of the MOA; and b. Submit to the COA Audit Team the lacking documents relative to the total payment of HEA to eligible health care workers (HCWs) and non-HCWs for the period May 1, 2022 to October 31, 2022, otherwise a Notice of Suspension will be issued accordingly.
observations.managementAction: Lacking documents were submitted to the Audit Team on June 27, 2024. The liquidation of HEA was already submitted to CHD 4A Calabarzon on May 30, 2024. The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Health Officer coordinated with CHD and provided required documentation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant or the authorized representative to submit all the succeeding cash advance vouchers and supporting documents for CF to the Office of the Audit Team Leader within seven days after the release of the check for timely post-audit thereof and ensure strict compliance with the said Circular.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal offices submitted the outstanding CF liquidation vouchers and supporting documents to the COA Audit Team on June 6, 2024. The Municipal Accountant agreed to ensure timely submission of CF liquidations in the future.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Explain in writing to the COA Audit Team, for further evaluation, why the requirement of posting of performance security was not imposed in the procurement; and b. Ensure that henceforth, the posting of performance security and submission of the same, if warranted, in the procurement activity, is adhered to by the winning bidders as required under Section 39 of the 2016 RIRR of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction: The required written justification was submitted to the Audit Team on May 23, 2024. Verification showed that performance security posting and submissions were adhered to thereafter in July to December 2024 procurements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to review and verify records pertaining to outstanding balances of Due to GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth and thereafter remit the premiums withheld to avoid the incurrence of penalties and surcharges.
observations.managementAction: Verification showed that the premium contributions withheld during the year and prior year’s overdue balances were remitted; summary of amounts withheld and remitted during CY 2024 were provided and the remaining balances were remitted on January 10, 2025.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. Cause the liquidation of the total disbursements charged to LDRRMF and submit all lacking supporting documents relative to the total disbursements charged to LDRRMF, otherwise a Notice of Suspension will be issued; and b. Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant should ensure that all payments to suppliers are supported with complete documentation.
observations.managementAction: The required lacking documents were submitted to the Audit Team on June 6, 2024. The Municipal officials agreed to ensure that payments are supported with complete documentation thereafter.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all cash advances and liquidation reports are supported with complete and properly accomplished documentation, cash advances for travel expenses are given directly to the concerned individual and not to a representative and only to a duly authorized and bonded Disbursing Officer of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal offices submitted the lacking supporting documents and reconciliations to the COA Audit Team on June 6, 2024. The Municipal Accountant agreed to ensure compliance with COA Circulars going forward.

In the audit of the CY 2024 accounts of the Municipality, Part II of this Report informed the Audit Team that the Municipality was on the preliminary activities and inventory taking of the one-time cleansing of PPE balances.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 1, Part II of this Report

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Instruct the Municipal Accounting Office to submit immediately to the Audit Team the 5,725 DVs and its supporting documents amounting to P1,508,802,258.73, otherwise, the transactions shall be disallowed in audit; and
b. Henceforth, strictly adhere with the provisions of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No. 2009-006 and the MNGAS regarding the timely submission of financial transaction documents and reports to COA.
observations.managementAction: The Management submitted all 5,725 DVs and its supporting documents to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit Team the corresponding JEVs, approved DVs, perfected contract and AIR pertaining to the recorded AP amounting to P148,776,199.27, otherwise, make the necessary adjusting entries to revert or derecognize the AP to ensure that subject account and other affected accounts are fairly presented in the financial statements; and
b. Refrain from recognizing AP unless the required documents to establish the validity of the claims are complete and henceforth, strictly comply with the submission of JEV and supporting documentation to the Audit Team pursuant to Section 31, Chapter 2 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II and COA Circular No. 2009-006.
observations.managementAction: The documents to substantiate the AP amounting to P148,776,199.27 were submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Submit immediately the following to the Audit Team: i. the JEVs and supporting documents pertaining to the deposits in transit amounting to P28,166,169.84; and ii. the DVs and supporting documents representing the 174 outstanding checks totaling P20,919,012.25; and
b. Prepare the necessary accounting entries to record the bank account and the online collections totaling P3,058,522.98 and submit the same to the Audit Team for verification and audit; and
c. Henceforth, strictly adhere with the provisions of PD No. 1445, COA Circular Nos. 96-011 and 2009-006, and the Manual regarding the preparation of Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) and the timely submission of financial transaction documents and reports to COA.
observations.managementAction: A JEV was passed to record the bank account and online collections in CY 2023. The CY 2024 monthly BRS were timely submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor stop the practice of processing of payment of claims under a contract awarded not strictly in accordance with the procedures prescribed under RA No. 9184 and its 2016 Revised IRR.
observations.managementAction: The Management implemented corrective action. Closed due to issuance of Notice of Disallowance (ND) Nos. 24-002-SILANG-RO4A-CAVITE and 24-003-SILANG-RO4A-CAVITE dated July 8, 2024 amounting to P63,700.00 and P93,100.00, respectively.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. subject all deliveries of all items for acceptance first by the MGSO before inspection to ensure proper and accurate recording, inventory and monitoring; and
b. duly notify the COA Audit Team of the time and scheduled delivery date of all purchases of the Municipality within 24 hours from such acceptance and submit the corresponding report of inspection thereafter; and henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 92-386 and 95-006 and the Manual relative to the acceptance and inspection of the purchases of the Municipality and the notification of delivery and submission of the report of inspection to COA.
observations.managementAction: Management now notifies the Audit Team of the time and scheduled delivery date of purchases and submits the corresponding report of inspection. Management implemented the recommendation in CY 2024.

Management submitted a written explanation regarding the procurement activities totaling P251,272,722.34, and the Municipality submitted a supplemental Procurement Plan (SPP) covering the said amount. The Municipality submitted the Report of Bid Results and uploaded in the FDPP.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council to:
a. Ensure that the LDRRMFIP in the succeeding years includes under a separate caption the PPAs to be charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years, otherwise any disbursement out of the STF will be suspended or disallowed in audit; and
b. Ensure 100 percent accomplishment of LDRRMF PPAs considering funds are available thereto, in order to enhance the preparedness and response capabilities of the Municipal and its constituents.
observations.managementAction: The CY 2024 LDRRMFIP of the Municipality includes the previous years’ balances in the CY 2025 LDRRMFIP and the Municipality is in the process of realigning/reprogramming funds and finalizing the LDRRMF Accomplishment Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to:
a. In coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer, submit a copy of the supplemental portion of the budget covering the excess charges, if any; and
b. Ensure 100 percent utilization of the GAD Fund considering funds are available thereto.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality has substantially accomplished its GAD PPAs in CY 2024 and the GAD Focal Person submitted the supplemental document covering the excess charges and the MDC Resolution realigning the funds.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Through the Human Resource Management Office (HRMO), expedite the hiring and/or promotion of qualified personnel to reduce, if not totally eliminate, the need to appoint casual workers, thus ensuring a more efficient and effective government service thru strategic management of human resources; and
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the HRMO to ensure that the figures reported in the Quarterly Report on Human Resource Complement (QRHRC) submitted and uploaded in the Full Disclosure Policy Portal (FDPP) reconcile with the figures appearing in the financial statements and related reports.
observations.managementAction: The HRMO is gradually reducing the need to appoint casual workers (with crowdsourcing) and is promoting qualified personnel in the Municipality. The Municipal Accountant and HRMO will ensure the figures submitted in the QRHRC reconcile with the FS; the Municipality submitted QRHRCs for CY 2024 to the FDPP.
No action taken by the Management as of audit; the Municipality was informed previously and no updated PCs or RPCPPE supporting documents were submitted to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 1, Page Nos. 30 to 33, Part II of this Report

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Legal Officer and/or other concerned Municipal Officials to:
a. Initiate the termination of the subject contract in accordance with Annex I of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and the blacklisting of the Contractor pursuant to his Performance Securing Declaration form dated March 24, 2022;
b. Demand from the Contractor the return of the balance of the advance payment made by the Municipality amounting to P484,261.26 and to pay necessary damages as a result of the abandonment of the project; and
c. File appropriate legal action against the Contractor and other responsible officials and employees, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: The Legal team of the Municipality is currently reviewing the process of terminating the subject contract. No document initiating the termination of the subject contract was submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned BAC-TWG to provide written explanation on why the involved contractor was not disqualified despite non-submission of a valid business permit and henceforth, direct the TWG to strictly adhere to the requirements, rules and guidelines on the post-qualification evaluation of bidders as provided under Section 34 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, otherwise the relevant procurement and payments to the bidder, who was supposedly disqualified, will be disallowed in audit, with the participating BAC members included as liable persons.
observations.managementAction: The BAC-TWG has provided written explanation to the Audit Team and the TWG has been directed to strictly adhere to the post-qualification requirements. The valid business permit of the contractor was forwarded to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Legal Officer and/or other concerned Municipal Officials to:
a. Instruct the Municipal Engineer to determine the actual work accomplished by the Contractor and demand from the latter the return of the overpayment and payment of necessary damages as a result of the non-completion and abandonment of the project; and
b. File appropriate legal action against the Contractor and other responsible officials and employees, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: Review by the Legal team is on-going and the Municipal Engineer is determining the actual work accomplished by the Contractor and demanding the return of the overpayment. No document showing the formal demand was submitted to the Audit Team as of audit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineering Office to conduct preliminary inspection and submit punch-lists to contractors on infrastructure projects when they reach an accomplishment of 95 percent of the total contract amount to determine defects and thus, make necessary corrections, before proper turnover and acceptance of the projects to the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineering Office now practices the submission of punch-list when any of the CY 2024 projects reached 95 percent accomplishment and submits the punch-list to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a. The OIC-GSO to prepare and submit the monthly report of fuel consumption to the COA Audit Team;
b. Municipal Administrator and the OIC-GSO to formulate additional guidelines on the use of motor vehicles and/or revisit existing policies of the Municipality on fuel utilization, if there is any; and
c. Municipal Administrator and other concerned officials to study the advantages and disadvantages of sourcing the gasoline need of the Municipality thru the use of fleet cards being provided by some gasoline stations as what other government agencies are doing for better control and monitoring of its fuel consumption.
observations.managementAction: The MGSO submitted monthly reports of fuel consumption and the Municipality entered into a retail purchase agreement with an accredited fuel provider. The MGSO will submit the monthly fuel consumption report to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Market Administrator and the Municipal Market Board to provide him with complete information on the status of lease and occupancy of all market stall holders and to initiate the process of revoking the lease agreement/certificate of award of Stall Holders who subleased or sold the market stalls, including the 105 initially established in audit, and cause the execution of lease agreement between the Municipal Government and present occupants.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality conducted orientations and provided information sheets for the issuance of IDs for stall owners. The Municipality conducted meetings and conveyed the observations and awareness of the policy; reported on 101 out of 105 subject stalls that violated the 2010 Municipal Market Code by subleasing or selling their stall rights.

The Municipality sent notices to stallholders for unsettled balances. The Municipality provided meetings and conveyed the observation; submitted eviction notices to some stallholders. The Municipality presented collection efforts and reported collections; however, the required list/schedule of lessees with delinquent accounts and with current unpaid rental fees as of December 31, 2022 was not submitted to the Audit Team as of date.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the General Services Officer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer in securing the necessary documents like contracts and disbursement payments of land acquisition and construction of infrastructure assets as basis in recording and updating their respective PPE Property Cards (PC);
b. Require the General Services Officer to update all PCs and records based on the latest RPCPPE; and
c. Require the General Services Officer and Municipal Accountant to exert their best efforts to reconcile their respective PPE records to come up with the reconciled balances of the PPE accounts to be presented in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Officer coordinated with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer and updated certain records; updated PCs were not fully submitted at the time of audit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (LDRRMO), in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant, to closely monitor the Implementation of projects with funding from the capital outlays for Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (LDRRM) or consider a thorough review thereof during the budget preparation to determine whether there is a need to revert and/or reprogram the funds, subject to the approval of the Sanggunian Bayan upon the Mayor’s recommendation and compliance with other pertinent rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMO prepared the LDRRMFIP and the Municipality is considering reprogramming funds and conducting a review during budget preparation. The LDRRMFIP for CY 2025 was prepared and is to be submitted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Instruct the OIC-GSO to submit the SSMI to the Municipal Accountant together with the original copies of the RIS which will be the basis of the latter to record in the books the expended portions of supplies and materials; and
b. Instruct all Inventory Custodians to ensure that all issuances of supplies and materials are covered by RIS and the General Services to use the RIS as basis for the preparation of the SSMI.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-GSO submitted the SSMIs and RISs and the Municipal Accountant recorded the necessary entries. Revised/restated entries were made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LDRRMO, in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant, to closely monitor the Implementation of projects with funding from the capital outlays for Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (LDRRM) or consider a thorough review thereof during the budget preparation to determine whether there is a need to revert and/or reprogram the funds, subject to the approval of the Sanggunian Bayan upon the Mayor’s recommendation and compliance with other pertinent rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMO and concerned offices are coordinating to monitor LDRRM projects and reprogram funds where necessary; the LDRRMFIP for CY 2025 was prepared.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the General Services Officer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer in securing the necessary documents like land titles, contracts and disbursement payments of land acquisition and construction of infrastructure assets as basis in recording and updating their respective PPE Property Cards (PC);
b. Require the General Services Officer to update all PCs and records based on the latest RPCPPE; and
c. Require the General Services Officer and Municipal Accountant to exert their best efforts to reconcile their respective PPE records to come up with the reconciled balances of the PPE accounts to be presented in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality implemented actions to reconcile PPE accounts; coordination among GSO, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer was initiated. Updated RPCPPE reconciliation has been pursued.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LDRRMO, in coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant, to closely monitor the Implementation of projects with funding from the capital outlays for Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (LDRRM) or consider a thorough review thereof during the budget preparation to determine whether there is a need to revert and/or reprogram the funds, subject to the approval of the Sanggunian Bayan upon the Mayor’s recommendation and compliance with other pertinent rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMO prepared the CY 2025 LDRRMFIP and is reviewing/realigning PPAs; some PPAs were reprogrammed and utilized in CY 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Instruct the OIC-GSO to submit the SSMI to the Municipal Accountant together with the original copies of the RIS which will be the basis of the latter to record in the books the expended portions of supplies and materials; and
b. Instruct all Inventory Custodians to ensure that all issuances of supplies and materials are covered by RIS and the General Services to use the RIS as basis for the preparation of the SSMI.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-GSO and Inventory Custodians submitted SSMIs and RISs; the Municipal Accountant recorded the expenditures based on the submitted SSMIs.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office (MSWDO) and other concerned Offices to take proactive measures to collect the outstanding receivables such as sending demand letters to the debtors to notify them that payments are overdue and legal action may be pursued, if necessary, otherwise, analyze the effectiveness of the Self-Employment Assistance (SEA) program if non-payment has become a pattern among these beneficiaries;
b. Municipal Accountant to provide allowance for impairment on expected losses from uncollectible or long overdue receivables pursuant to Paragraph 67 of the IPSAS 29; and
c. Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to communicate with Postema Gasoline Station and Aqua Fojas Water Refilling Station on the overpayments made and immediately settle these accounts; and
d. Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entry to correct the error that resulted to the negative balance amounting to P14,740.65 in the Other Receivables account.
observations.managementAction:
Letters were sent to the creditors and/or employees to inform them of their collection, and/or an adjusting entry was posted to immediately revert the dormant claims not receiving any response from collecting on the deadline given, or revert these amounts to the unappropriated surplus in accordance with COA Circular No. 76-45.
JEV No. 12-35917 dated December 31, 2024 was drawn by the Municipal Accountant to record an allowance for impairment on Other Receivable account; Postema Gasoline Station and Aqua Fojas refunded the overpaid amount.
The Municipal Accountant has posted an adjusting entry with JEV No. 12-35910 dated December 31, 2024, to correct the errors in recording.
The Municipal Accountant has posted an adjusting entry with JEV no. 12-35920 dated December 31, 2024, to correct the errors in recording.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:
a. submit to the Audit Team all necessary supporting documents such as Delivery Receipts, Sales Invoice, Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) and other pertinent documents to prove the validity of the recorded Accounts Payable totaling P1,100,132.29 in CY 2022, otherwise revert all unsupported Accounts Payable to the GF of the Municipality to fairly present the balances of the affected accounts in the financial statements; and
b. cause the immediate reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund of the long outstanding payables totaling P2,956,745.20 and P17,500.00for the GF and SEF, respectively.
observations.managementAction:
Necessary supporting documents were submitted to the Audit Team.
Adjusting Entry was posted with JEV No. 12-35947 dated December 31, 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with concerned offices, to send letters to creditors and/or employees and/or inform them of their collectibles from the Municipality and if no response is received upon the deadline given, immediately revert these amounts to the unappropriated surplus in accordance with COA Circular No. 76-45;
b. Municipal Treasurer to obtain explanation from the Accountable Officer (AO) with overages in collection, and if the overages cannot be satisfactorily explained by the AO, forfeit the amount in favor of the Municipality by issuing an Official Receipts (ORs) and record the overage as “Miscellaneous Income”; and
c. Municipal Treasurer to demand from the AO the immediate settlement of their unremitted collections, otherwise deduct the amount from their salaries, and thereafter make sure that overages and/or shortages are settled immediately once discovered.
observations.managementAction:
Letters were sent to the creditors and/or employees to inform them of collection, and if no response is received upon the deadline given, immediately revert these amounts to the unappropriated surplus in accordance with COA Circular No. 76-45.
Municipal Treasurer obtained explanations and AOs have settled their unremitted collections, with details provided: Leilani Torres – OR No. 8735499 dated 05/21/2024 amounting to P400.23; Aubrey Tamio – OR No. 8735488 dated 05/21/2024 amounting to P10.00; Juanita Cerrero - OR No. 8735489 dated 05/21/2024 amounting to P0.55.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal GSO to update all the PAR for all motor vehicles and transfer them to the personnel who are actually using the service vehicles to establish proper accountability;
b. Municipal GSO and the concerned officials to observe the allowed number of vehicles provided in Item 4.2 of DBM Budget Circular 2022-1, unless assignment of more than one vehicle is justified as necessary in the performance of their official functions; and
c. Municipal GSO and the concerned officials to limit the assignment of motor vehicles to the LGU officials enumerated under Item 4.4 of DBM Budget Circular No. 2022-1, and ensure full adherence to the guidelines on the common use of motor vehicles by other officials and employees in the exercise of their core function; and
d. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal GSO, to compute the amount that must be deducted from the Transportation Allowance (TA) of personnel based on the actual days they use government vehicles.
observations.managementAction:
PARs for motor vehicles were updated. Vehicles were transferred to the personnel who are actually using the service vehicles to establish proper accountability. Justification that the need for assignment of more than one vehicle as it is necessary in the performance of their official functions was submitted.
The Management monitored and limited the assignment of motor vehicles to the LGU officials and ensured full adherence to the guidelines on the common use of motor vehicles by other officials and employees in the exercise of their core function. The Municipal GSO and concerned officials monitored and limited the assignment of more than one vehicle and ensured full adherence to the guidelines on the common use of motor vehicles by other officials and employees.
The Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal GSO, computed the amount to be deducted from TA of personnel based on the actual days they use government vehicles.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to ensure that the procedural requirements in the hiring of consultants, as provided under the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 are strictly complied with; 
b. Human Resource and Management Officer (HRMO) and other concerned officials to analyze and identify the consultants whose services or functions could be performed by existing plantilla personnel and ensure that in the hiring of consultants for the completion of a specific project/program/activity (PPAs), the services to be rendered are highly technical or special in nature that require skilled experts in their respective fields to avoid unnecessary payments of Consultancy Fees; and
c. Municipal Accountant to ensure that complete and proper documentations are attached to the disbursement vouchers before payment of consultancy fee.
observations.managementAction:
The BAC only hired one consultant under procedural competitive bidding requirements in CY 2025.
HRMO analyzed and identified the consultants whose services and functions could be performed by existing plantilla personnel, hence, contracts of other five consultants were not renewed in CY 2025.
The Municipal Accountant ensured complete and proper documentations before payment of consultancy fee.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MPDC and other concerned officials to ensure that all issues that may cause delay in the implementation of 20 percent development projects are reasonably identified and addressed prior to their inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) and procurement so that the intended benefits from the projects may be timely enjoyed by the intended users.
observations.managementAction: The Management ensured full adherence with the Planning and audit recommendations given.
observations.reason: Changes in the PPAs in the AIP for the LDRRMF are supported by SB resolutions. Implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and submit an action plan that will outline and discuss in details the legal measures and/or strategies the Municipal Treasurer’s Office will adopt or undertake to improve the collection of delinquent RPT and SET of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer has already sent statements of account to delinquent taxpayers and conducted house-to-house visits to collect RPT and SET.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Chairperson to furnish the COA with copies of the approved contracts and all the documents forming part thereof within five days from their execution as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001 to facilitate prompt review and evaluation of the contracts and early communication to Management of the deficiencies, if any.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer and BAC have been furnishing the COA with copies of the approved contracts and purchase orders, along with other required supporting documents, within five days upon their execution/perfection.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and submit the necessary requirements to enable the Municipality to include in its automated payroll system the payment of the wages of its JO workers.
observations.managementAction: The job order workers have already been included in the automated payroll system.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the Municipal Accountant and the BAC Secretariat to immediately submit the lacking documentary requirements for the procurement of heavy equipment subject to verification of the Audit Team, to avoid audit suspension and/or audit disallowance; and
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the provision of warranty security for the procurement of goods is imposed by retaining an amount to the supplier or requiring the latter to submit a special bank guarantee equivalent to at least one percent of the total contract price, as required under Section 62.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and BAC submitted the lacking documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to ensure the timely release of the barangays’ shares from RPT collections in compliance with Section 271(d) of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: The transfer of the barangay shares in the RPT collection was already made within the reglementary period by the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to increase the amount of fidelity bond of the subject DO to cover the large amount of her accountability of CAs pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 to protect the interest of the Municipal Government, or otherwise, limit the amount of their cash advances up to the maximum cash accountability of their fidelity bond.
observations.managementAction: The bond was increased to P1,500,000.00 with a corresponding Accountant’s accountability amounting to P5,000,000.00 upon renewal.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous recommendations that:
a. Municipal Mayor require the concerned Municipal officers/employees to immediately liquidate the outstanding CAs, especially at year-end and to implement internal control procedures to prevent the incurrence of the outstanding CAs; and
b. Municipal Mayor and Municipal Accountant to stop the practice of advancing cash except for Confidential Fund and official travels and refrain from granting additional CAs unless previous CAs are liquidated.
observations.managementAction:
Demand letters have already been issued by the AOs, however they were still unable to liquidate their outstanding CAs. For those who are no longer in service or are deceased, the MAO will request a write-off.
Elected officials are no longer allowed to request CAs, unless the request is for official travel.
Purchases of similar common-use supplies and equipment have been consolidated and undertaken through competitive bidding. Distributions of supplies under LDRRMF are now supported by recipients' names, addresses and signatures. The 10 percent retention money is already being implemented for projects involving construction.
observations.reason: Despite the issuance of demand letters to the accountable officers (AOs), they were still unable to liquidate their CAs. Given the length of time that has passed, the AOs are now having difficulty collating the necessary documents for liquidation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, during the preparation of the Annual Procurement Plan (APP), instruct the BAC to consolidate similar CSE and procure the items in bulk thru public bidding except when shopping or other alternative mode of procurement is necessary and warranted under the circumstances set forth under the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: Purchases of CSE have been consolidated and have been undertaken through competitive bidding.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (DRRM) Officer to ensure that distribution of various supplies and relief goods are supported by original copy of the list of recipients with their names, addresses, contact numbers, and signatures, and the description and quantity of the items they had received to facilitate validation by the COA Audit Team and other stakeholders, otherwise relevant disbursements may be suspended or disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRM Office has implemented the distribution of goods, supported by recipients' names, addresses, contact numbers, and signatures, ensuring that all distributions are properly documented and supported by recipient lists.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Require the contractors of the seven completed infrastructure projects to submit valid financial instruments equivalent to the retention money totaling P452,274.84 which should have a validity period of one year from the date of issuance of the Certificates of Completion; and
b. Strictly impose the deduction of the 10 percent retention money to the payments to contractors or require the submission of valid financial instrument equivalent to the retention money in case the contractor has requested for substitution which will be accepted only if the project is on schedule and satisfactorily undertaken, and to release the retention money to contractors upon final acceptance of the project, in compliance with Section 6 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: The contractors were required to submit valid financial instruments equivalent to the retention money. For CY 2025 construction projects, the 10 percent retention deduction is already being implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with each other to facilitate the immediate return of the unexpended fund transfers totaling P152,012.53 to the respective source agencies or National Treasury.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant is still coordinating with the Municipal Treasurer for the return of the unexpended balances to the respective source agencies or the National Treasury. The Municipal Accountant is collating the documents related to the fund transfers received to facilitate the return of unexpended balances to the source agencies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and the Municipal Engineer to prepare a catch-up plan to fast-track the completion of all delayed projects and henceforth ensure the optimal and timely utilization of 20 percent DF in accordance with the approved time-frame in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP).
observations.managementAction: The implementation of delayed Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs) commenced in the third quarter and thereafter. Additionally, appropriations for some development projects were realigned to other PPAs.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal DRRM Officer and Municipal Accountant to submit the monthly report on Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF for the months of January to September of CY 2023. Henceforth, ensure that monthly report is submitted on or before the 15th day after the end of each month to facilitate timely review and verification of the Municipality’s LDRRMF transactions, thus corrective measures can be recommended, if necessary.
We also recommended that the LDRRM Council decide on the use of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF, and review and update annually the LDRRMF Investment Plan to incorporate the PPAs to be funded from the unexpended Quick Response Fund (QRF) and LDRRMF-MOOE of the previous years.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRM has implemented the monthly reports on Sources and Utilization and has included the unexpended balances in the LDRRMF Investment Plan.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipality GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) and the Technical Working Group (TWG) to undergo training on HGDG to build their competence in the conduct of gender analysis of programs and projects for the purpose of GAD budget attribution.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Person and the TWG will undergo training on HGDG and other capacity-building programs. The GAD focal person and other employees have attended training and orientation sessions on the proper application of the HGDG tool, and have conducted gender analysis of the Municipality's PPAs using the said tool.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to remit all taxes withheld within the prescribed period to avoid any liability equivalent to the surcharges that may be imposed by the BIR against the latter and other responsible officials in their personal capacity.
observations.managementAction: All taxes withheld during CY 2024 by the Municipality were remitted to the BIR within the prescribed period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a) Instruct the CGSO to immediately submit the RPCPPE for the physical count of PPEs conducted in CY 2022, and in coordination with the City Accountant, reconcile any discrepancy with the General Ledger; b) Direct the City Accountant to exert extra effort to locate records pertaining to PPEs acquired prior to CY 2006, which are also necessary for the conduct of one-time cleaning of PPEs; and c) Enjoin the members of the Inventory Committee to speed up the preparation and actual conduct of physical inventory activities for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE to lessen, if not totally eliminate, the discrepancy between actually existing and recorded PPEs.
observations.managementAction: The CGSO submitted the RPCPPE for the physical count of PPEs conducted in CY 2022. The City Accountant and Municipal General Services Office were still in the process of reconciling the Inventory Report and the General Ledger.
observations.reason: No record pertaining to PPEs acquired prior to CY 2006 submitted to the Audit Team to account for the unsupported item of PPE in the accounting records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to adopt the Perpetual Inventory Method where purchases of inventories not for immediate distribution shall first be recorded under proper Inventory account, and to recognize expense only upon issuances thereof to end-users and submission of necessary supporting documents.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the City Accountant already made an adjusting entry to recognize that the items are still in the custody of various offices in the City per JEV No. 100-23-02-1249 dated February 28, 2023.
observations.reason: The City Accountant already made the necessary adjustment for the items covered by the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued by the Ospital ng Imus-Property and Supply Department but has yet to record all procurement-related expenses.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a) Facilitate the completion of the documents needed to file the request for write-off to the COA of the receivable due from the deceased former Municipal Mayor amounting to P42,650.55 which had been dormant for 36 years; and b) Follow-up the status of the case filed at Branch 16 Cavite City Trial Court against the two Accountable Officers and include the amounts due them in the request for write-off only if the cases against them are already dismissed as evidenced by certification or certified copies of finality of decision from the said courts.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant has exerted effort in knowing the whereabouts of the former Cashier through seeking the necessary assistance of the City Legal Office to issue notice of demand to the former cashier. The City Accountant has not yet requested for the write-off to the COA.
observations.reason: The CGSO and the City Accountant were still in the process of locating the records; the case filed at the trial court was still ongoing and required certified copies of finality of decision for write-off.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the: a) CGSO to exert effort to locate the records of the 12 undocumented unserviceable motor vehicles and/or conduct inquiry to establish the ownership of the said vehicles and submit the results thereof to the Audit Team for verification and evaluation; b) Concerned accountable officers to submit the duly accomplished forms required for disposal to facilitate the determination by the Disposal Committee of their condition; and c) Members of Disposal Committee to immediately conduct the necessary inspection of the unserviceable motor vehicles and proceed with the disposal thereof. Thereafter, prepare and submit the duly accomplished IIRUP supported with complete disposal documents to the City Accountant for review and dropping of the disposed properties from the accounting books. Should efforts to locate the missing vehicles proved futile, file a request for relief from property accountability to the Commission on Audit.
observations.managementAction: The CGSO has located the records of the 12 undocumented unserviceable motor vehicles. The concerned accountable officers have submitted the forms required for disposal of the remaining 18 motor vehicles. The Members of Disposal Committee has conducted inspection of the motor vehicles and proceeded with the disposal. Preparation and submission of duly accomplished IIRUP with complete disposal documents is ongoing.
observations.reason: The CGSO was still in the process of locating the records of the remaining 18 unserviceable motor vehicles and submission of complete disposal documents for the remaining units; disposal and drop from accounting not yet completed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a) Schools Division Superintendent, BAC and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC)-Technical Working Group (TWG) to provide valid explanation or justification in writing on the excessive Approved Budget of the Contract (ABC) and unit price of the DSLR cameras, for evaluation of the Audit Team, otherwise, the amount of P4,267,569.95 will be disallowed in audit and filing of appropriate charges against accountable officials will be recommended.
observations.managementAction: The Schools Division Superintendent, BAC and Bids and Awards Committee - Technical Working Group submitted written justification on the excessive ABC. However, upon evaluation of the justifications submitted by the responsible persons, the recommendations were not duly complied with; thus Notice of Disallowance (ND) No. 2023-003(2022) dated July 17, 2023 was issued.
observations.reason: No satisfactory explanation was provided or the justifications did not fully address the excessive pricing; ND issued.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the GFPS to ensure that all PPAs included in the GPB have well-defined gender issues or GAD mandate to be addressed.
observations.managementAction: All the required documents were submitted to Audit Team. The Municipality reiterated commitment to comply.
observations.reason: No management action was taken to fully define gender issues in the PPAs; justifications submitted on February 28, 2023 were evaluated and found not satisfactory.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the City Mayor direct the CDRRMC to ensure that all projects and programs in the LDRRMFIP are implemented to enhance the preparedness and response capabilities of the City and its constituents.
observations.managementAction: The CDRRMC justified that the use of LDRRMF was strictly regulated or halted anticipating the possibility that it would be used for future COVID-19 Response. While the PPAs for Disaster Mitigation were not implemented to enhance preparedness and response due to prohibition on face-to-face training. The City committed to implement the programs and projects intended for mitigation once conditions allow.
observations.reason: While PPAs for the training were not implemented due to prohibitions on face-to-face training. For CY2023, some funds were utilized and some activities were halted given the context of response needs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Inventory Committee, through the Municipal General Services Officer, to avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances and observe the guidelines and procedures in the conduct of physical count of property, plant and equipment, recognition of PPE items found at the station and disposition for non-existing/missing PPE items, as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006, and maintain PCs per category of property, plant and equipment; and b. Municipal Accountant to maintain PPELC for each category of assets, which shall be kept for each class of property, plant and equipment to record the acquisition, description, custody, estimated life, depreciation, disposal and other information about the PPE.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee was still unable to submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE), as well as the preparation of the PCs. They did not avail the one-time cleansing of PPE as of Calendar Year (CY) 2023.
