observations.reason: Only subsidiary ledgers were prepared by the Municipal Accountant instead of PPELCs for each category of assets. Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 1, Page Nos. 31 to 32

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a) the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with all the concerned officials and employees to facilitate the immediate liquidation of their respective cash advances to date or as soon as the purpose for which the advances were granted has been served by submitting the required Liquidation Reports and necessary supporting documents to the Municipal Accounting Office; settle their outstanding cash advances as of year-end and observe strict adherence to the rules and regulations in the granting, utilization and liquidation of the cash advances; b) the Municipal Accountant to ensure that additional grant of cash advances to officers and employees is processed only if their previous cash advances have already been accounted and settled, and that all must be liquidated by the end of the year; and c) all concerned officials and employees to strictly comply with COA Circular No. 97-002 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.
observations.reason: Cash advances of P569,265.00 remained unliquidated as of year-end. Various officers and employees were still granted additional cash advances despite non-settlement of their previous cash advances. COA Circular No. 97-002 was not strictly complied with in the granting, utilization and liquidation of the cash advances. Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 4, Page Nos. 36 to 37

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accounting Office with the duly certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year 2023 to enable the latter Office to set up at the beginning of the year the RPT and SET Receivables for the current year correctly; and b. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to analyze and investigate the cause/s of the net difference of P7,610.49 between the balances of the RPT and SET Receivables and between the aggregate amount of these two accounts and the List of Unpaid Taxes as of December 31, 2022, and make the necessary adjustments in their records.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented The duly certified list of taxpayers was not provided to the Accounting Office causing the latter to not set up the RPT and SET Receivables for the current year.
observations.reason: There were unreconciled net differences of P13,866,974.79 between the balances of the RPT and SET Receivables and P27,765,662.37 between the Accounting and Treasury records for CY 2023. Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 3, Page Nos. 34 to 35

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous years’ audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to file a request for authority to write off the dormant and/or unliquidated receivables amounting to P404,467.50 to the COA, following the guidelines and procedures provided by COA Circular No. 2016-005.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented The Municipal Accountant still failed to file a request for authority to write off the dormant receivable of P404,467.50.
observations.reason: Office has been preparing the necessary documents required under COA Circular No. 2016-005 in order to file for a request for authority to write-off the dormant receivables. Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 6, Page Nos. 39 to 41

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned Municipal officials to expedite the implementation of the programs and projects for which the transferred funds are intended, or should it be determined that the projects are not anymore feasible or there had been no more use for the funds, to instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to immediately remit the P350,058.19 to the respective source agencies or the Bureau of Treasury.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented The DILG-LGSF – Other Financial Assistance for LGU Priority Programs and Projects amounting to P5,000.00 was already remitted last January 30, 2024. The rest are for transfer or remittance to the Bureau of Treasury.
observations.reason: Unexpended balances totaling P350,058.19 of funds received by the Municipality were not returned to the source agencies or the Bureau of Treasury after the completion of the intended programs or projects. Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 5, Page Nos. 37 to 38

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a) Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (MDRRMO) and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to provide accurate information on the timetables or schedules of implementation of all the projects and activities in the Local Disaster Risk Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) for proper guidance and monitoring of their implementation; and b) review the programs with unexpended balances and determine whether these are still feasible, otherwise, realign/reprogram the funds allotted for such projects.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented The unexpended balances were re-aligned/re-programmed for other projects. The MDRRMC will abide by the recommendation as to the prescribed treatment of the LDRRMF under COA rules from this year on.
observations.reason: Only P16,504,978.02 or 80.35 percent of the appropriation was utilized due to inadequate planning/scheduling of programs/projects implementation. Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 9, Page Nos. 46 to 48

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a) Inventory Committee to conduct annual physical count of all properties of the Municipality and submit a RPCPPE to the COA Audit Team on or before January 31 of the following year; b) Property Custodian to maintain property cards for all properties of the Municipality and reconcile these balances with the PPE Ledger Cards of the Accounting Office and the Property Acknowledgement Receipts (PARs); and We also recommended that should reconciliation of the RPCPPE and accounting records appeared to be improbable, the Municipality shall consider availing the one-time cleansing of PPE in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant commented through a letter dated May 2, 2024 that upon the creation of the General Services Office and full assumption of functions of its personnel, the Municipality will conduct the necessary periodic inventory and maintenance of property records. He added that the Municipality also considers the availment of one-time cleansing of PPE to resolve issues on unreconciled discrepancies.
observations.reason: The Municipality had just created the position of General Services Office (GSO), hence, actual physical count was not conducted and the RPCPEE was not prepared and/or submitted for CY 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of all inventory items semi-annually, and prepare and submit the corresponding RPCI to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year.
observations.managementAction: Reiterated in Observation No. 4, pages 40 to 43.
observations.reason: The Municipality had just created the position of GSO, hence, actual physical count was not conducted for the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer or the designated Property Custodian to cause the registration of the five motor vehicles and the renewal of registration of the five other motor vehicles with the LTO and thereafter ensure that all vehicles of the Municipality are insured with the GSIS.
observations.managementAction: The five unregistered motor vehicles were already unserviceable and disposal thereof will be conducted in CY 2024.
observations.reason: The five motor vehicles were unserviceable; disposal procedures to be conducted in CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC Secretariat to furnish the COA Auditors with copies of all PO within five days from their execution pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001, and ensure that POs are properly accomplished containing all the basic data or information required under COA Circular No. 96-010. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the officer/s responsible for accepting deliveries to notify the Auditors of incoming deliveries of goods within 24 hours from the scheduled acceptance so that inspection could be timely conducted by the Auditors, if warranted, and regularity of the deliveries could be effectively established.
observations.managementAction: The GSO and BAC Secretariat regret the failure to submit the POs to the Auditors within five days and that Auditors were not notified of time and date of deliveries. For proper flow of procurement process, all recommendations will be implemented on subsequent transactions.
observations.reason: Non-submission of POs within five days remained a practice; Municipality committed to implement recommendations for subsequent transactions.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the following:
a. the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to specifically identify and provide details of the property totaling P78,397,105.23 in the RPCPPE taken from damaged old municipality record and adjust the accounting records accordingly; and
b. the Municipal Accountant to derecognize the disposed property amounting to P2,502,362.39 in the books of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Management committed that the Municipal Accountant and the GSO, in coordination with the Inventory Committee, shall proceed in its activities towards the reconciliation of balances of PPEs and assured that the One-Time Cleansing Activity be given top priority.
observations.reason: The Municipality was unable to effectively follow and complete the procedures for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE. Of the six procedural guidelines, only the initial/preliminary activities and the conduct of inventory taking had been carried out.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. identify the total amount of accumulated depreciation which should have been posted under Land Improvement, Aquaculture Structures accounts but were posted under different account and adjust both the accounts accordingly; and
b. coordinate with the accounting system provider to review the system and fix the issue in the posting of depreciation of PPE accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Management coordinated the problem with the system provider to review the system and fix the issue in the posting of depreciation of PPE accounts.
observations.reason: Lack of supporting documents showed that the amount of P 51,268,391.15 pertains to Infrastructure project of prior years (2005 and below) which is under the Land Improvement account. OIC-GSO accounting systems, our account. This can be considered as fully depreciated and shall be derecognized from books of account. Corresponding Journal Entries for the derecognition of various PPE items has been prepared and shall be submitted for proper approval of COA. The amount of P 1,995,000.00 pertains to the procurement of Robotics materials under the Special Education Fund. The students shall be educated on said Robotics program within the school year and the materials shall be used within one year. This amount must be reclassified as MOOE and not Capital Outlay. Proper journal entries have been recorded. Re JEV# 2024-06-04929 dtd 06/30/24. The remaining balance has been evaluated and has been reclassified as MOOE. The individual items are below the threshold amount of P50,000.00.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the LFC to analyze the trend in the collection of tax revenues in the previous years and determine the amount of income which has reasonable assurance of collectivity and use the data as inputs or benchmark in the preparation of Annual Budget for the ensuing fiscal year.
observations.managementAction:
The management promised to comply with the recommendation. In addition, the management commenced the following initiatives to increase its collection of revenues:
a. The LFC conducted meetings to implement measures of expanding its collection through modernization of the Treasury System. They have contracted the services of a reputable company that supplies, delivers and installs a Cloud based e-Governance Smart LGU System. The purpose of which is to enhance the delivery of government services to the citizens as well as businesses of the Municipality.
b. The Municipal Assessor’s Office has implemented the new assessed value for real properties. Although the revision is not yet 100 percent complete, the said project has brought a tremendous impact on the increase in collections especially during the first quarter of CY 2023. The LFC made a forecast on Tax Revenues amounting to P585,825,725.61 mainly due to the expected increase collections on RPT. However, there were some lapses due to the failure in the timely submission of tax revision and assessment of newly constructed buildings at the Philippine Offshore Gaming Operator (POGO) area at Barangay Pulvorista, Binakayan, Kawit Cavite which should have been conducted by the Office of the Provincial Assessor. Thus, it resulted to a chain reaction where Business Income from POGO was not generated as expected.
c. The Business Permit and Licensing Office (BPLO) has continuously been exerting effort to locate new business establishments and collect payments for the unpaid renewal of old business establishments.
d. The Municipal Legal Office has worked on various transactions that would demand for the collection of penalties and charges, thus giving additional gains for the Municipality.
observations.reason: No actions were taken by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Finance Committee (LFC) to adopt measures to eliminate the cash overdraft by exercising diligence, prudence, and proper control in the disposition of the Municipality’s funds and limit expenditures within the total income realized and collected during the year and reduce unnecessary operating expenditures until the cash overdraft is eliminated.
We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Finance Committee to make a thorough analysis of the tax and non-tax revenues collected and realized for the previous years and use the results of such analysis as basis in the preparation of an attainable Annual Budget for the ensuing fiscal year.
observations.managementAction: The management replied that it is a continuing effect on the unrealized target revenue/income. Various expenses were incurred based on the appropriations in the Annual Municipal Budget. However, not all were realized and as such cash was not sufficient to cover the said liabilities. Nevertheless, the management committed to comply with the aforementioned recommendations.
observations.reason: Despite the efforts of the concerned officials, the Municipality’s collection was still insufficient to cover its current liabilities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Inventory Committee, through the Municipal General Services Officer, to avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances and observe the guidelines and procedures in the conduct of physical count of property, plant and equipment, recognition of PPE items found at the station and disposition for non-existing/missing PPE items, as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006, and maintain Property Cards per category of property, plant and equipment; and b. Municipal Accountant to maintain Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards for each category of assets, which shall be kept for each class of property, plant and equipment to record the acquisition, description, custody, estimated life, depreciation, disposal and other information about the PPE.
observations.reason: Although preliminary steps were undertaken during the year, the Municipality was still unable to avail the one-time cleansing of PPE. Furthermore, the Inventory report submitted was incomplete.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned Municipal Officials to exercise diligence, prudence and proper control in the disposition of government funds and to implement concrete and effective cost-cutting measures to avoid the incurrence of cash overdraft.
observations.reason: The Municipality's available cash for General Fund and Trust Fund were insufficient to cover their current liabilities as of December 31, 2023, resulting in a cash deficit/overdraft of P16,598,499.23.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct: a. the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with all the concerned officials and employees to facilitate the immediate liquidation of their respective cash advances; b. the Municipal Accountant to ensure that additional grant of cash advances to officers and employees is processed only if their previous cash advances have been accounted and settled; and c. all concerned officials and employees to strictly comply with COA Circular No. 97-002 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.
observations.reason: Most of the cash advances granted during the year were held for a significant amount of time. Although partial liquidations were made, it took seven days to five months before the same were fully liquidated. Moreover, of the P4,891,480.00 cash advances granted during the year, only P42,073.03 remained unliquidated as of year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to record the goods procured for consumption upon receipt and to recognize expense only when the goods are issued to end-users or beneficiaries, in accordance with IPSAS 12, Section 114 of the MNGAS for LGUs and COA Circular No. 2015-09.
observations.reason: Inventory accounts showed that the accounts were only debited to record their procurement and the total amount of P4,923,205.51 were credited in lump sum to recognize the issuance of these inventories at year-end rather than accounting for the beginning balances as of January 1, 2023, as well as all subsequent procurement and utilization on a timely manner.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to immediately remit the unexpended balance of the transferred funds totaling to P73,157.29 to their respective source agencies or the National Treasury, in accordance with COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.
observations.reason: Unexpended balances totaling P1,502,614.44 of funds received by the Municipality were not returned to the source agencies or the Bureau of Treasury after the completion of the intended programs or projects.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor design and install appropriate and effective control measures to monitor the stallholders’ accounts and other income derived from the Naic Public Market and Grand Terminal to ensure that reports and records are correct and collections and receivables are properly accounted, such as:

a. require the Market Supervisor, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to maintain separate records of stallholders’ accounts which should be regularly reconciled with the accounting records to prevent one Office from having absolute control over the records; and

b. require the Market Supervisor and Municipal Treasurer to prepare monthly financial statements or records of income and expenses to be submitted to the Municipal Mayor and Sangguniang Bayan; and

c. require the Municipal Accountant to maintain a separate financial statements for special accounts for proper monitoring of all properties of the Market, Slaughter and Terminal, and to determine whether income generated by these accounts were sufficient to meet their operating costs.
observations.managementAction:
The Office of Market Supervisor had already prepared schedule of operating lease receivables as of December 31, 2023 while the Municipal Accountant had started the preparation of their own records in January 2024 only.

The previously received amount was deducted from the amount due for the 15 days mandatory closure of all stalls selling non- essential commodities in April 2021 during the COVID-19 pandemic.

No action was taken by the concerned Municipal officials.
observations.reason: Inability of the previous market administration to provide the schedule of outstanding receivables from stallholders.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. GSO and the Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records of PPE and ensure that differences between the Report on the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) and the PPE balances presented in the Financial Statements are addressed;
b. Municipal Accountant to determine the causes of the differences between the general ledger balances of PPE and the PPE Ledger Cards (PPELCs) maintained by their Office and make the necessary adjustments to ensure that both records are reconciled; and
c. Municipal Accountant and all other concerned Offices to evaluate the repairs and/or improvements done or to be done to the Municipality’s assets to determine whether they extend the useful life of the original property and add these costs to the carrying value of the PPE, once recognition criteria per IPSAS 17 are met, otherwise, record these repairs/improvements in the appropriate Repairs and Maintenance account.
observations.managementAction:
Reconciliation of records for CY 2023 acquisitions were made. The Municipal Engineering Office had difficulty in locating the records.
The GSO had communicated with the Accounting Office to commence with the preliminary activities on the One-Time Cleansing of PPE accounts and that they will start the actual conduct of said activity upon the availability of the accounting personnel in charge.
observations.reason: Reconciliation and verification of prior year acquisitions is still on-going due to difficulty in locating the records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to conscientiously carry out detailed engineering activities before awarding contracts for infrastructure projects to ensure that issues are properly addressed prior to project implementation to avoid delays, suspension and/or termination of projects.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer/BAC Chairperson commented that they will coordinate with the concerned Home Owners Association (HOA) and prepare the facilities needed for the proper implementation of the 78 commercial stalls. Moreover, she commented that the BAC had overlooked to check the lot ownership and that they will try their best that the future projects will not have an issue regarding this matter. And for the occupancy and usage of the subject property, she said that the Barangay Government of Calubcob had applied last March 2024 for the electrical connection to Meralco and that they are preparing to move to the new building. Furthermore, she added that they will comply with the recommendations of the Audit Team in their future projects to avoid deficiencies/errors and wastage of government funds and property.
observations.reason: The project was completed on April 5, 2023 only, or a delay of 206 calendar days from its target date of completion on September 11, 2022. Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 8, Page Nos. 55 to 59, Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: a) require the General Services Office (GSO) and Municipal Accounting Office to reconcile their respective records of PPE accounts; b) ensure that the RPCPPE includes all the necessary information and shall include only serviceable PPE which are physically counted; and c) enjoin the members of the Inventory Committee to speed up the preparation and actual conduct of physical inventory activities for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 to lessen, if not totally eliminate, the discrepancy between actually existing and recorded PPEs.
observations.managementAction: General Services Office and Municipal Accounting Office is yet to reconcile their respective records for PPE to come up with the correct balances for PPE accounts. The Inventory Committee has conducted partial physical inventory per office.
observations.reason: Management has insufficient manpower to speed up the physical inventory activities for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE.

observations.recommendation: a. direct the Local Finance Committee to submit a specific, measurable, attainable, relevant, and time-bound action plan that will outline their recommended fiscal policies and strategies to address the problem of cash overdraft that the Municipality had been experiencing over the years; b. direct the Municipal Budget Officer, Accountant and Treasurer to adopt or implement measures to eliminate the cash overdraft by exercising diligence, prudence, and proper control in the disposition of the Municipality’s funds and limit expenditures within the total income realized and collected during the year and reduce unnecessary operating expenditures until the cash overdraft is eliminated; and c. instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and Treasurer to refrain from using the Special Education and Trust Funds for purposes other than those for which these funds are intended, otherwise, necessary charges should be filed against them.
observations.managementAction: Management will submit a specific, measurable, attainable, relevant, and time-bound action plan that will outline the recommended fiscal policies and strategies to address the problem of cash overdraft. Management committed to adhere to other audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Due to cash overdraft, the Municipality still resorted to borrow from Special Education and Trust Fund.

observations.recommendation: a. enjoin the Local Finance Committee and other responsible officials to submit to the Audit Team the basis used in the computation of the target collectible income for CY 2023 and explain in writing, for further evaluation of the Audit Team, the justification for the continuous failure of the Municipality to realize said target; b. direct the Local Finance Committee, Committee on Finance, Budget and Appropriations, Municipal Accountant and other responsible officials to submit a specific, measurable, attainable, relevant, and time-bound action plan that will outline their recommended fiscal policies and strategies to address the problem of unrealized income that the Municipality had been experiencing over the years; c. direct the Committee on Finance, Budget and Appropriations to judiciously and meticulously review the annual budget of the Municipality in order to prevent the inclusion of items of income that are not collectible; and d. direct the Local Finance Committee to strictly set a more realistic budget based on previous year’s actual performance in terms of revenues and expenses in accordance with the existing guidelines on budget preparation.
observations.managementAction: Management will submit to the Audit Team the basis used in the computation of the target collectible income for CY 2023 and explain in writing, for further evaluation, the justification for the continuous failure of the Municipality to realize said target. Specific, measurable, attainable, relevant, and time-bound action plan that will outline their recommended fiscal policies and strategies to address the problem of unrealized income that the Municipality had been experiencing over the years will be submitted. Management will review the annual budget and will exclude items of income that are not collectible.
observations.reason: The Municipality still fail to realize the target; included unrealistic estimates (e.g. environmental fee not imposed in the year).

observations.recommendation: a. Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to ensure that projects under the 20 percent DF are backed up with available funds to guarantee their timely implementation; b. Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Engineer and other concerned officials to ensure the optimal and timely utilization of 20 percent DF in the succeeding period by including development projects that are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready pursuant to DBM, DOF and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2020-1; and c. Municipal Engineer and other concerned officials to conduct detailed engineering and in-depth planning for each project to ensure that the priority development projects are feasible for implementation.
observations.managementAction: Management will ensure to back up the 20 percent Development Fund with available funds to guarantee timely implementation of the projects. It will ensure the optimal and timely utilization of 20 percent DF in the succeeding period by including development projects that are well-planned and procurement and implementation ready. For a feasible implementation of the projects, Management will conduct detailed engineering and thorough planning for each project.
observations.reason: Due to cash overdraft, the Municipality was not able to utilize fully its 20 percent Development Fund since no cash is available to pay such obligations. Also, several projects were bidded late (Dec 21, 2023 to Jan 3, 2024) and thus scheduled for CY 2024 implementation.

observations.recommendation: a. Municipal Accountant, BAC and other concerned officials to submit to the COA Audit Team for evaluation all lacking documents relative to the disbursements for the Noveleta Information Technology Building at Poblacion, Noveleta, Cavite and the Purchase of Heavy Equipment (Backhoe), otherwise a Notice of Suspension will be issued, accordingly. Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant should ensure that all payments to suppliers and contractors are supported with complete documentation; b. BAC to use in their procurements the prescribed Philippine Bidding Documents, and other standard forms issued by the GPPB whenever necessary; and c. BAC to explain in writing to the COA Audit Team, for further evaluation the inability to invite three observers in all stages of the procurement process, and their failure to conduct pre-bid conference, bid evaluation, and post-qualification as required under the pertinent provisions of RIRR of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction: Management has currently compiling and completing the necessary documentation for the payments totaling P3,994,950.00 and will ensure that all missing documents are obtained and submitted promptly. The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) has been instructed to strictly use the prescribed Philippine Bidding Documents (PBDs) and other standard forms issued by the Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB) in all future procurements. Regarding the absence of three observers in the procurement processes, Management recognized that there were shortcomings in the procedures. The BAC faced challenges in securing observers due to the scheduling conflicts and the unavailability of representatives from private groups and non-governmental organizations (NGOs).
observations.reason: BAC did not comply with required documentary and procedural requirements (e.g., invitation of observers, pre-bid minutes, bid evaluation report, post-qualification report); some documents still lacking.

observations.recommendation: a. Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Municipal Accountant and other responsible officials to explain in writing to the COA Audit Team, for further evaluation, on why the subject procurement was charged out of the 20 percent Development Fund, otherwise a Notice of Disallowance will be issued accordingly; and b. Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate submission to the COA Audit Team for evaluation the approved program design containing the rationale, objective and target beneficiaries, list of recipients with names, addresses and signatures, and the description and quantity of the items received and pictures during distribution.
observations.managementAction: Management will submit to COA Audit Team for evaluation the approved program design containing the rationale, objective and target beneficiaries, list of recipients with names, addresses and signatures, and the description and quantity of the items received and pictures during distribution.
observations.reason: The procurement was charged to 20% DF; lacking program design, list of recipients with signatures and pictures during distribution; justification submitted but evaluation pending.

observations.recommendation: Direct the GFPS to maximize the utilization of the GAD funds through the full implementation of GAD-related programs and projects in order to fully attain the objectives of the appropriated funds. Likewise direct the GFPS TWG and other concerned officials to continue their efforts to fully create and establish the GAD Monitoring and Evaluation Team and System of the Municipality as required under Sections 3.4, 5.1 and 5.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 and 2016-01.
observations.managementAction: Management was unable to fully utilize the GAD fund to its maximum potential because of financial constraints, which hampered the complete implementation of GAD related programs and projects. Management promised to create GAD Monitoring & Evaluation Team and System and will be functionally implemented for the effectiveness and efficiency of the municipality ‘s gander mainstreaming effort could be fully established.
observations.reason: Due to cash overdraft and financial constraints the Municipality was unable to maximize the utilization of GAD funds; GAD M&E Team/System not yet created.

observations.recommendation: Direct the Municipal Accountant or the authorized representative to submit all the succeeding cash advance vouchers and supporting documents for Confidential Funds to the Office of the Audit Team Leader within seven days after the release of the check for timely post-audit thereof and to ensure strict compliance with the said Circular.
observations.managementAction: Management will submit all the succeeding cash advance vouchers and supporting documents for Confidential Funds to the Office of the Audit Team Leader within seven days after the release of the check for timely post-audit thereof and to ensure strict compliance with the said Circular.
observations.reason: Delays observed: first and second quarter submissions delayed by 87 and 69 days, respectively.

observations.recommendation: Instruct the Municipal Accountant to trace and determine the details of the Due to BIR account amounting to P822,443.39 and cause its immediate remittance and/or make the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts as the case may be. Henceforth, instruct the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with BIR Memorandum Circular No. 23-2007 by ensuring that the correct amount of taxes being withheld and that these taxes are properly remitted within the prescribed period to prevent the incurrence of surcharges and penalties.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to remit immediately the said overdue balance and will ensure that correct amount of all taxes withheld be properly remitted within the prescribed period to avoid any liability in terms of surcharges that may be imposed by the BIR.
observations.reason: Remittance to BIR is ongoing; part of balance remitted on January 10, 2024 leaving P822,443.39 unremitted as of Dec 31, 2023.

observations.recommendation: Instruct the Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to review and verify records pertaining to outstanding balances of Due to GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth and thereafter, remit immediately all the premiums withheld to avoid the incurrence of penalties and surcharges.
observations.managementAction: Management promised to remit immediately to the said National agencies the outstanding balances of premiums withheld to avoid the incurrence of penalties and charges.
observations.reason: Remittances to GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG were made partially on January 10, 2024; outstanding balances remained as of Dec 31, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. reconstitute the Inventory Committee and require them to conduct complete physical count of the Municipality’s PPE according to their prepared Physical Inventory Plan and prepare the corresponding RPCPPE following the guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006; b. direct the OIC - General Services Officer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Engineer in securing the necessary documents like land titles, contracts and disbursement payments of land acquisition and construction of infrastructure assets as basis in recording and updating their respective PPE Property Cards (PC); c. require the OIC - General Services Officer to update all PCs and records based on the latest RPCPPE; and d. require the OIC - General Services Officer and the Municipal Accountant to exert their best efforts to reconcile their respective PPE records to come up with the reconciled balances of the PPE accounts to be presented in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by Management.
observations.reason: Comparison of the accounting and the property records of the Municipality as of December 31, 2023 disclosed a difference of P3,299,802,109.36 Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 1, Page Nos. 31-33.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Legal Officer and/or other concerned Municipal Officials to: a. initiate the termination of the subject contract in accordance with Annex I of the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184 and the blacklisting of the Contractor pursuant to his Performance Securing Declaration form dated March 24, 2022; b. demand from the Contractor the return of the balance of the advance payment made by the Municipality amounting to P484,261.26 and to pay necessary damages as a result of the abandonment of the project; and c. file appropriate legal action against the Contractor and other responsible officials and employees, if warranted. Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC and the Municipal Accountant to: d. ensure the submission to and acceptance by the Municipality of an surety bond before release of advance payment to contractor; and e. strictly comply with pertinent provisions of the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184 on deduction of retention money from progress billings and the conditions for their release to the contractor.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by Management.
observations.reason: The Legal team of the Municipality is currently reviewing the process of terminating the contract.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Legal Officer and/or other concerned Municipal Officials to: a. instruct the Municipal Engineer to determine the actual work accomplished by the Contractor and demand from the latter the return of the overpayment and payment of necessary damages as a result of the non-completion and abandonment of the project; and b. file appropriate legal action against the Contractor and other responsible officials and employees, if warranted. Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC and the Municipal Accountant to: c. ensure the posting and submission by the contractors of the required Performance Security or Performance Securing Declaration, before award of contracts to them; and d. strictly comply with pertinent provisions of the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184 on deduction of retention money from progress billings and the conditions for their release to the contractor.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by Management.
observations.reason: Review by the Legal team of the Engineering Office is on-going. No formal demand from the contractor was made as of date.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineering Office to conduct the preliminary inspection and submit punchlists to contractors on infrastructure projects when they reach an accomplishment of 95 percent of the total contract amount to determine defects and thus, make necessary corrections, before proper turnover and acceptance of the projects to the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by Management.
observations.reason: No punch-list/s was submitted to the Audit Team as of date.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct: a. all concerned officials to strictly comply with the COA guidelines on proper filling out and approval of Driver’s Trip Ticket except when the travel is proven to be confidential in nature, in which case a certification to that effect should be submitted; b. the OIC – Head of the General Services Office to prepare and submit the monthly report of fuel consumption to the COA Audit Team; c. the Municipal Accountant to ensure completeness of driver’s trip tickets, purchase orders and charge invoices before processing the payments for gasoline consumptions, otherwise the transactions will be suspended or disallowed in audit; d. Municipal Administrator and the OIC – Head of the General Services Office to formulate additional guidelines on the use of motor vehicles and/or revisit existing policies of the Municipality on fuel utilization, if there is any; and e. Municipal Administrator and other concerned officials to study the advantages and disadvantages of sourcing the gasoline need of the Municipality thru the use of fleet cards being provided by some gasoline stations as what other government agencies are doing for better control and monitoring of its fuel consumption.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by Management.
observations.reason: The monthly reports are not being submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Market Administrator and the Municipal Market Board to provide him with complete information on the status of lease and occupancy of all market stall holders and to initiate the process of revoking the lease agreement/certificate of award of Stall Holders who subleased or sold the market stalls, including the 105 initially established in audit, and cause the evaluation of the current agreement between the Municipal Government and present occupants.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by Management.
observations.reason: No complete information on the status of lease and occupancy of all market stall holders as of December 31, 2023 was submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. instruct the Market Administrator to strictly enforce the provisions of the Municipal Market Code and the contract of lease of market stalls and cause the revocation or cancellation of lease agreement/certificate of award of stallholders in case of failure of the delinquent stallholders to immediately comply with the demand of the Municipal Treasurer to settle their liabilities after observing due process of law; b. require the Market Administrator, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer to submit the List/Schedule of Lessees with delinquent accounts and with current unpaid rental fees as of December 31, 2022 to the Municipal Accountant for recording as Accounts Receivable; c. instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) based on the Schedule of Lessees with delinquent accounts and with current unpaid rental fees as of December 31, 2022; and d. instruct the Market Administrator and Municipal Treasurer to consistently send demand/collection letters to delinquent Tenants/Lessees, and to recommend to the Market Board the termination of contracts of those who continuously refuse to comply with their demand for payments, otherwise Notice of Charge will be issued by the Audit Team that will include all Municipal Officials who must but did not exercise due diligence or take necessary action to collect the amount due the Municipal Government.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by Management.
observations.reason: The required list/schedule of lessees was not submitted to the Audit Team as of date.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. direct the General Services Officer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer in securing the necessary documents like contracts and disbursement payments of land acquisition and construction of infrastructure assets as basis in recording and updating their respective PPE PC; b. require the General Services Officer to update all PCs and records based on the latest RPCPPE; and c. require the General Services Officer and Municipal Accountant to exert their best efforts to reconcile their respective PPE records to come up with the reconciled balances of the PPE accounts to be presented in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by Management.
observations.reason: Comparison of the accounting and the property records of the Municipality as of December 31, 2023 disclosed a difference of P3,299,802,109.36 Reiterated in Part II Observation No. 1, Page Nos. 31-33.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. instruct the General Services Officer (GSO) to submit the SSMI to the Municipal Accountant together with the original copies of the RIS which will be the basis of the latter to record in the books the expended portions of supplies and materials; and b. instruct all Inventory Custodians to ensure that all issuances of supplies and materials are covered by Requisition and Issue Slips (RIS) and the General Services to use the RIS as basis for the preparation of the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI).
observations.managementAction: No action taken by Management.
observations.reason: The GSO has yet to submit the SSMI and corresponding RIS to the Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and other concerned officials to ensure that all issues that may cause delay in the implementation of 20 percent development projects are reasonably identified and addressed prior to their inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) and procurement so that the intended benefits from the projects may be timely enjoyed by the intended users.
observations.managementAction: Fully Implemented
observations.reason: The Municipal Mayor directed the MPDC to ensure that all issues that may cause delay in the implementation of 20 percent development projects were reasonably identified and addressed prior to their inclusion in the AIP and procurement.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (MDRRMO) and all other concerned personnel to ensure all changes in the Annual Budget and AIP be implemented in accordance with Article 417 and 454(b) of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 7160, otherwise, affected transactions may be disallowed in audit; and b. Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office to ensure the implementation of projects, programs, and activities allocated in the Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) to enhance the disaster capabilities of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: The Municipal Mayor instructed the Municipal Budget Officer and the MDRRMO to ensure that all changes in the PPAs in the AIP for the LDRRMF were supported by SB resolutions and all PPAs allocated in the LDRRMFIP are implemented to enhance the disaster capabilities of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
1. a. Inventory Committee to immediately convene to plan or strategize the implementation
of the one-time cleansing of all PPEs owned by the City in order to resolve the recurring
issues on the discrepancy between the property records and accounting records and
to come up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition
and accountability; and
b. City Accountant to draw Journal Entry Voucher to record the unrecognized
depreciation on depreciable PPEs totaling P828,950,213.00 by debiting the
Government Equity account and crediting the corresponding Accumulated
Depreciation accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a. Gather and present to the Audit Team the necessary documents that will
substantiate the claims totaling P49,210,396.60, otherwise make the necessary
adjustments in the books of accounts to fairly present the balances of the
affected accounts in the financial statements; and
b. Strictly ensure that succeeding recording of Accounts Payable will be based on
the relevant supporting documents attached to the claims instead of the
Obligation Request submitted to the City Budget Office.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a. Prepare the necessary journal entry voucher to record in the books the noted
book reconciling items totaling P733,526.38;
b. Revert to the Cash in Bank - LCCA and record to the corresponding liability
accounts the stale checks totaling P876,617.01; and
c. Henceforth, ensure that valid reconciling items and stale checks included in the
BRS are immediately adjusted in the books in order to fairly present the affected
accounts in the financial statements for the succeeding years.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and Acting City
Treasurer to review their records to ascertain the whereabouts and status of the
unsubmitted DVs totaling P1,652,942.94 and immediately submit the same to the
Audit Team, otherwise pertinent disbursements will be suspended or disallowed in
audit.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and City Budget Officer to submit a written
explanation to the COA Audit Team regarding the non-submission of APP, for
the latter’s evaluation;
b. City Budget Officer to strictly enforce to the end-user or implementing units the
preparation of PPMPs for their different PAPs in order to facilitate the
preparation of the City’s APP;
c. BAC to ensure that:
• All procurements that will be undertaken are in accordance with the
approved APP, including approved changes thereto, pursuant to Section 7.2
of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and
• Similar items requested for the same PAP will be consolidated to avoid the
splitting of procurements, which is prohibited under Section 54.1 of the 2016
Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, otherwise the subject transactions will be
disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee to:
a. Provide written justification to the Audit Team on why the subject contracts were
awarded to suppliers who were not PhilGEPS-registered, for our evaluation,
otherwise the subject transactions will be disallowed in audit;
b. Ensure that subsequent procurements of goods through Shopping and Small
Value Procurement will be awarded to suppliers registered with the PhilGEPS
pursuant to Section 54.6 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; and
c. Scrutinize and validate the eligibility documents submitted by the bidders in
order to ensure that a contract will be awarded to a technically, legally and
financially capable supplier, contractor or consultant.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee and
City Engineer to coordinate with each other with regard the formulation of an
improved procurement plan which will ensure that all priority development projects
under the 20 percent Development Fund will be implemented during the year in
compliance with Section 3.2.3 of the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 and in order that
the City’s constituents will immediately benefit from the timely implementation of
the projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Officer to refrain from charging against the LDRRMF the subject
disbursements and to strictly utilize the fund for programs and projects which are
supportive of disaster risk reduction and management activities as provided in
Section 3 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and Item 5 of NDRRMC- DBM-DILG JMC
No. 2013-1.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. Ensure that only one disbursing officer is designated for each specific purpose
cash advance unless additional disbursing officers are fully justified by him,
pursuant to Section 108(A)(6) of the LTOM 2nd Edition, 2019, Book III;
b. Direct the City Treasurer to require all the designated disbursing officers to
maintain cashbooks; and
c. Require the City Treasurer and City Accountant to ensure that the cash
advances granted to the disbursing officers do not exceed the maximum cash
accountabilities covered by fidelity bond and that all the designated disbursing
officers are properly bonded as such.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a. Ensure that all cash advances, except for petty cash fund, are fully liquidated at
year-end and any unexpended balance shall be refunded by the accountable
officer;
b. Require every City official who will receive cash advance to strictly observe the
prescribed period of liquidation of his/her cash advance to safeguard the cash
from the risk of misuse or loss; and
c. Record in the books the liquidation of cash advances only upon verification of
the completeness of the supporting documents, which includes the official
receipt issued for the excess cash advance returned by the accountable officer,
if any.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to:
a. Require the concerned City officials to submit immediately to the COA Audit
Team the pertinent documents that would support the validity of the reported
expenses totaling P666,118.86, otherwise the subject payments will be
suspended in audit;
b. Ensure that all subsequent grant of cash advance for financial assistance will be
supported with duly approved payroll or list of payees indicating the amount to be
paid; and
c. Henceforth ensure that every City official who will receive cash advance is well-
oriented in the documentary requirements needed in the liquidation of his/her
cash advance, as prescribed in the pertinent sections of COA Circular Nos.
2012-001 and 2023-004, and ensure their completeness and validity prior to
liquidation to avoid audit suspension or disallowance.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the OIC - General Services Officer to:
a. Submit the duly accomplished PIF to the Office of the Supervising Auditor or
Audit Team Leader and the GIF of GSIS on or before April 30 of each year to be
used as basis for the assessment of the general insurance coverage of all the
City’s insurable properties; and
b. Insure with the GIF of GSIS all the City’s insurable assets and properties with
the appropriate insurance coverage to protect the City thru indemnification in
cases of damage or loss due to fortuitous events.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer and City
Accountant to exert utmost effort to identify the details of the reconciling items
with a net amount of P44,524,457.31 so that appropriate journal entries could be
drawn by the latter in the books.

Management’s Comment:
The City Accountant and City Treasurer committed to exert utmost effort to identify the
details of the reconciling items so that appropriate journal entries could be drawn.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. CAO and the CGSO to update and complete the PPELCs and PCs, respectively,
and to compare them with the RPCPPE and submit the same to the Inventory
Committee to be used as inventory working papers and on this basis, to revisit
and adjust the timeline of inventory-taking and other activities in the PIP;

b. Inventory Committee, together with the CGSO and CAO, to revisit the COA
Circular No. 2020-006, particularly the guidelines on how to conduct the
inventory taking that would likely resolve the issues on the whereabouts and
condition of the PPE, and on how to assign or establish cost/value based on
the market/fair value of PPE or by appraisal, in instances when the unit values
or costs of the PPE, including the subject land properties, were lacking or not
available from property records; and henceforth

c. CGSO and CAO to proceed with the following activities of One-Time Cleansing
of PPE using the procedural guidelines prescribed in subject circular,
esspecially to address the issue on unaccounted PPE and those which were only
presented in lump-sum amounts:
   i. Section 6.3 - Reconcilation of inventory count per RPCPPE with property and
   accounting records;
   ii. Section 7.0 - Disposition for non-existing/missing PPEs;
   iii. Section 8.0 - Derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs without available
   record of accountability; and
   iv. Section 9.0 - Accounting entries and disclosure in the notes to the Financial
   Statements

The City should take note of the sunset provision in COA Circular No. 2020-006
emphasizing that the procedures could no longer be used to further derecognize
missing properties and/or cleanse subsequent discrepancies or unreconciled
balances in PPE accounts.

Management’s Comment:
The City Accountant and the CGSO committed to update and complete the PPELC and
the PCs, respectively, and submit the same to the Inventory Committee. They also
promised to revisit and adjust the timeline of inventory-taking and other activities in the
PIP.

Moreover, the Inventory Committee committed to address all the problems encountered
in the preliminary activities and proceed in the cleansing of the City’s PPE accounts in
in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to reclassify the
aforementioned completed infrastructure projects to their appropriate PPE
accounts and provide the same with corresponding depreciation expense.

Management’s Comment:
The City Accountant committed to review the details of the CIP account. Completed
infrastructure projects will be reclassified to their appropriate PPE accounts and
depreciation expenses will also be provided.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to:

a. thoroughly review the long and/or unsubstantiated outstanding payables
totaling P8,295,693.53 and prepare appropriate adjusting entries to revert them
to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund, unless sufficient evidence is
submitted to the Audit Team to establish that the payables are founded on
valid claims; and

b. inform in writing the concerned suppliers and contractors about their
unclaimed retention money with the advice that the amount will be reverted to
the unappropriated surplus of the general fund of the City should proper claim
be not made within reasonable period.

Management’s Comment:
The City Accountant committed to review the long outstanding payables of the City and
prepare the necessary adjusting entries to revert the same to the unappropriated surplus
of the general fund. The City Accountant also committed to send letters/notification to
the concerned suppliers and contractors about their unclaimed retention money.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to draw Journal
Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to adjust the aforementioned deficiency by debiting the
account Other Payables and crediting the account Guaranty/Security Deposits
Payable both by P273,709,549.17.

In addition, ensure that appropriate accounts are used in recording warranty
security and retention money, in accordance with the RCA for LGUs, as prescribed
by COA Circular No. 2015-009.

Management’s Comment:
The noted deficiency was adjusted by the City Accountant thru JEV No. 101-2404-3149
dated April 1, 2024. In addition, the City Accountant also ensured that appropriate
accounts will be used in recording warranty securities and retention money.

a. We recommended that the City Mayor direct the accountable officers to
immediately liquidate their outstanding CAs and henceforth ensure that all CAs
are fully liquidated at the end of each year pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002.

b. We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to refrain from
granting additional CAs to any official or employee without liquidating first
his/her prior cash advances, and to instead designate additional SDO for the
grant of additional cash advances for special activities/projects.

c. We recommended that the City Mayor direct all AOs and employees to ensure
liquidation of CAs within the prescribed period as required by COA Circular
No. 97-002.

Moreover, instruct the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to propose
specific and achievable measures to resolve delays in the liquidation of CAs,
which include, among others, the City Accountant’s regular sending of demand
letters to AOs with a reminder that their salaries could be withheld should they
still fail to liquidate after demand.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to properly record
the purchases of welfare goods under the account Welfare Goods for Distribution
and recognize distributed portion to Welfare Goods Expenses only upon receipt of
report/proof of distribution by the CGSO or other concerned departments to reflect
the actual asset and expenses incurred during the year.

Moreover, require the City Accountant to maintain SLCs for all welfare goods as
prescribed under COA Circular No. 2014-002.

Management’s Comment:
The City Accountant explained that Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) No. 101-2404-3148 dated
April 1, 2024 was drawn to properly record the purchases of welfare goods under the
account Welfare Goods for Distribution. She also committed to maintain SLCs for all
welfare goods.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer and the City
Accountant to jointly evaluate the impairment of the receivables and to comply with
the documentary requirements and guidelines prescribed under COA Circular
No. 2016-005 to facilitate the submission to the COA of the request for authority to
write-off the dormant receivables amounting to P859,749.99 or to avail of the one-
time cleansing of dormant receivables within one year from the effectivity of COA
Circular No. 2023-008.

Management’s Comment:
The City Treasurer and City Accountant were amenable with the Audit Team’s
recommendation.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer to terminate the
time deposits under SEF and appropriate and use the funds for the projects
enumerated in Section 100 (c) of RA No. 7160 among the different schools of the
City as may be determined by the Local School Board (LSB).

Management’s Comment:
The City Treasurer explained that time deposit with the LBP was already transferred to
the SEF Current Account on April 4, 2024 as part of the compliance with the Audit Team’s
recommendation. The remaining time deposit with the DBP will be transferred to the
City’s SEF Current Account after its maturity date on May 3, 2024.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. City Engineer to formulate and implement an action plan to fast-track the
completion of the six projects amounting to P95,924,748.39 and thereafter,
ensure strict compliance with the guidelines on the issuance of suspension
order provided under the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to avoid
unnecessary delay in the implementation of infrastructure projects;

b. City Engineer and City Planning and Development Coordinator to ensure that
necessary detailed engineering activities are conducted prior to procurement
of infrastructure projects; and

c. BAC to require the submission of certification from the CEO that detailed
engineering surveys and designs have been conducted by the latter.

Management’s Comment:
The City Engineer submitted Action Plans to ensure that the aforementioned projects will
be completed accordingly.

Moreover, the BAC Chairperson committed to require the City Engineer to submit
certification that detailed engineering surveys and designs have been conducted.

a. We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Disaster Risk Reduction
and Management (DRRM) Officer and City Accountant to ensure that the
monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF is submitted on or
before the 15th day after the end of each month to facilitate timely review and
verification of the City’s DRRMF transactions, such that corrective measures
can be recommended, if necessary.

b. We recommended that the City Mayor direct the LDRRM Council to decide on
the use of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF, and review and update
annually the LDRRMFIP to incorporate the PPAs to be funded from the
unexpended Quick Response Fund (QRF) and DRRMF-MOOE of the previous
years to enable the City to further enhance its ability to address disaster risks,
disaster preparedness and response capabilities.

c. We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to maintain
the subsidiary ledgers of the unexpended balances of LDDRMF by year of
transfer to facilitate the monitoring of the five-year period in which the funds
shall remain in the Special Trust Fund and the reversion of any remaining
balance thereof to the General Fund after said period.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City GFPS and the TWG to
undergo trainings on HGDG to build their competence in the conduct of gender
analysis of programs and projects for purpose of GAD budget attribution.

Management’s Comment:
The Management was amenable with the Audit Team’s recommendation.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to:

a. immediately remit to the BIR the unremitted taxes totaling P2,414,547.36 to
avoid incurrence of penalties and surcharges due to late remittances of taxes;
and

b. henceforth, remit all taxes withheld by the City within the prescribed period of
10 days following the end of the month as required under Section 5(A)
paragraph 3 of BIR Regulation No. 11-2018. Otherwise, all penalties and
surcharges that may be incurred due to late remittances of taxes will be the
personal liability of the City Treasurer in accordance with Section 251 of
National Internal Revenue Code of the Philippines.

Management’s Comment:
The City Accountant explained that the City Accounting Office will verify the details of the
Due to BIR account of the City. Any amount actually due to the BIR will be remitted
accordingly and/or necessary adjusting entries will be drawn as needed.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CGSO and City Accounting Office
to reassess and adjust the PIP since the original timeline of the activities did not
proceed as planned, then proceed with the enforcement of One-Time Cleansing of
PPE using the procedural guidelines prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006,
specifically to address the issues on unreconciled balances of PPE in their records
and the unaccounted PPE or those which were only presented in lump-sum
amounts, with the following activities:

i. Section 6.3 - Reconcilation of inventory count per RPCPPE with property and
accounting records;
ii. Section 7.0 - Disposition for non-existing/missing PPEs;
iii. Section 8.0 - Derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs without available
record of accountability; and
iv. Section 9.0 - Accounting entries and disclosure in the notes to the Financial
Statements.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to prepare the BRS
and submit copies, along with all the necessary supporting documents, to the Audit
Team for verification within ten days after the end of each month. Likewise, draw
JEVs to take up all valid reconciling items requiring adjustments and corrections in
the books, to ensure the reliability and accuracy of the Cash in Bank – Local
Currency, Current Account.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to adopt the
Perpetual Inventory Method, whereby purchases of inventories shall first be
recorded under the proper Inventory account, and to recognize the expenses only
upon issuance thereof to end-users and submission of the necessary supporting
documents.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:

a. CGSO to prepare and maintain the SPC for each class of Semi-Expendable
Property, the RegSPI for the issuance, return, reissue and disposal of these
items and the RSPI to summarize all issued properties, and submit the same to
the City Accounting Office to be used as the basis for preparing the JEV to
recognize the property issued; and

b. City Accountant to prepare the SPLC, which includes the acquisition,
description, custody, impairment, issue/transfer/disposal, repair history and
other information about the property to establish the validity and reliability of
the recorded amounts in the books.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to ensure that all taxes
withheld by the City Government as withholding agent of the BIR are properly and
promptly remitted in full amount in accordance with BIR Revenue Memorandum Order
No. 23-2014 and BIR Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 23-2012.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and the City Accountant
to make representation with source agencies and/or the Department of Budget
Management (DBM) to determine the programs, activities and projects (PAPs) that
could be funded out of the P715,584.00 and to ensure the implementation of the
pertinent PAPs during the year. Further, immediately remit the unexpended balance of
the transferred funds totaling P44,305.18 to their respective source agencies or the
BTr.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. City General Services Officer to properly maintain and regularly update the SCs
to monitor and control all procurements and issuances of supplies and materials, to
prepare the monthly SSMI at the end of every month summarizing all the
approved RIS, and to submit these records and reports to the City Accountant as
the basis for recording the corresponding expenses;

b. City Accountant to record all deliveries of supplies and materials in the
appropriate SLCs upon the receipt of Inspection and Acceptance Report (IAR)
and other supporting documents, then prepare the JEVs to take up in the books
the: (1) procurement made and (2) issuances made based on the submitted SSMI
at the end of each month; and

c. Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical count of all inventory items
on a semi-annual basis and submit the corresponding RPCI not later than July
31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters,
respectively.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City General Services Officer
(CGSO) to prepare the PIF listing all the insurable properties of the City and submit
the same to the Office of the Auditor and GIF of the GSIS not later than April 30 of
each year, and insure them with the GSIS in compliance with the foregoing laws,
rules and regulations.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the CHRDMO, in coordination with
the Department Heads of the City, to:

a. ensure that the contracts of the JO clearly provide their job descriptions, duties
and expected deliverables against which their performance and
accomplishments, and the necessity of their hiring and regularity of payments
to them would be evaluated; and

b. study the City's manpower needs and hire JO workers based on specific
requests from concerned Department Heads or Officials who shall certify that
his or her office needs the services of a JO worker for an identified piece of
work.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to increase the amount of
bond of the AOs, who have been receiving large amount of cash advances, pursuant to
Section 101 of PD No. 1445 and BTr Circular No. 02-2019 dated April 25, 2019, in
order to protect the interest of the City Government, otherwise, limit the amount of
their cash advances up to the extent of the maximum cash accountability of their
fidelity bonds.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Development Council, the City
Planning and Development Coordinator and the OIC-City Engineer to:

a. immediately address the issues and concerns on the four projects and proceed
with their implementation so that the benefits therefrom could be enjoyed by the
City’s constituents in a timely manner; and

b. henceforth, thoroughly review all development projects to ensure that all issues
and concerns relating to the projects are addressed and resolved prior to their
inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan, ensuring proper facilitation of project
completion on time.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Council (CDRRMC) to:

a. identify and incorporate in the LDRRMFIP, under a separate caption, the projects
and activities to be charged out of the previous years’ unexpended LDRRMF and
the continuing appropriations, if any, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002; and

b. validate whether the PPAs funded by the continuing appropriations for CY 2021
and 2022 will still be implemented; otherwise, transfer the funds to the STF.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and the Officer-In-
Charge, City Public Market Administration Office to:

a. execute formal COLs with all the qualified market stallholders, making sure that
they are prepared in accordance with the stipulations of the 2015 Market Code
of General Trias;

b. update and complete the names reported in the list submitted by the CTO, and
reconcile them with those of the City Public Market Administration Office, as
well as with the registered business owners per BPLO reports, to ensure
uniformity of reports among the three offices and reliable identification of
leaseholders;

c. require all market stallholders to obtain business or Mayor’s permits from the
BPLO, or face penalties and the closure of their operations under relevant City
Ordinances;

d. investigate the practice of selling and sub-leasing of market stalls not in
accordance with Section 15 of the Market Code and cause the revocation of the
lease, if warranted;

e. investigate why numerous market stallholders were permitted to occupy two
stalls that were not adjacent to each other, to lease more than two market stalls,
and to lease stalls from different sections of the market;

f. send demand letters to current market stallholders with unsettled accounts,
consisting of market stall rental fees and/or electricity bills, totaling
P4,048,554.74, including surcharge, informing them that failure to settle their
accounts within 30 days from the due date shall be sufficient grounds for the
automatic cancellation of the lease rights and eviction; and

g. ensure that the provisions of the 2015 Market Code of General Trias,
particularly on the issuance of lease contracts, the disallowance of sub-leasing
and sale of privilege to occupy stalls, the prohibition of leasing more than two
market stalls to an individual, and the collection of market fees and electricity
bills and imposition of surcharge on unsettled balances, among others, are
strictly enforced by the City.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Chairperson of the Technical Working
Group (TWG) of the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to conduct gender analysis
through the administration of HGDG tool from the planning and budgeting phase to the
implementation, monitoring and evaluation phase, apply the results thereof in the
preparation of the GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) and the accompanying GAD
Accomplishment Report (AR) and the identification of strategies to address gaps or
missing elements, if any, to properly determine and gradually increase the gender-
responsiveness of the GAD programs and activities and ensure that gender issues were
addressed accordingly.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and General
Service Officer to first update and compare their respective latest records, that is
the RPCPPE and the PPELCs, prepare the inventory working papers for
submission to the Inventory Committee and the latter, to revisit, re-assess and
accordingly adjust the PIP, before proceeding with the inventory taking of the
City and succeeding activities for the one-time cleansing of PPE, including the
reconciliation of the inventory count with the property and accounting records
and disposition of missing properties, among others, in accordance with the
guidelines and procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to prepare and
maintain the lapsing schedule for depreciation expense for all the PPEs of the
City, complete with all necessary information to arrive at an accurate and correct
balances of PPE accounts, and prepare the necessary adjusting entry to reflect
the correct balance of the PPE, expense, and Retained Earnings accounts.

We recommend that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City General Services Officer and City Accountant to reconcile their records
and to reclassify as semi-expendable property the PPE with acquisition cost of
less than P50,000.00 per unit in compliance with COA Circular No. 2022-004;
and
b. City Accountant to prepare the adjusting entries to reclassify the Semi-
Expendable items to appropriate inventory accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Accountant and the City Treasurer to conduct reconciliation of
records/reports and make the necessary adjustments to establish the correct
balances of the subject receivables and their deferred credits;
b. City Treasurer to prepare and provide the City Accounting Office with the List
of Certified List of RPT Taxes Due and Collectible for the year, based on the
City Assessor’s Assessment Roll; and
c. City Accountant to set-up the RPT Receivable and SET Receivable at the
beginning of every year based on the Certified List of Real Property Taxes Due
and Collectible and reconcile any discrepancy with the Certified List of all Real
Property Tax Delinquencies to ensure the accuracy of the said receivable
accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to prepare and
furnish the City Accountant of the Schedule of Unreleased Checks as of year-end
for the restoration/reversion of Cash in Bank-LCCA and recognition of
payable/liability.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer to ensure that
the Liquidating Officer strictly deposit intact all daily collections with the
Authorized Government Depository Bank (AGDB) within the day or not later than
the next banking day as required under the pertinent provisions of PD No. 1445
