and MNGAS for LGUs. Likewise, it is recommended that the City Mayor direct the
City Treasurer to strictly establish strong internal controls in the handling of cash
to prevent further incurrence of abnormal/negative balances of the Cash Local
Treasury account.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer to terminate
time deposits under SEF and make the same readily available to support
programs and projects under the SEF.

We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the City Accountant and City
Engineer to coordinate with each other to facilitate the full settlement and proper
liquidation of the fund, in compliance with COA Circular No. 94-013 dated
December 13, 1994, and Memorandum of Agreement dated April 26, 2021.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to require the
contractor to submit an irrevocable standby letter of credit of equivalent value
from a commercial bank, a bank guarantee or a surety bond callable upon
demand, issued by a surety or insurance company duly licensed by the Insurance
Commission and confirmed by the City. Otherwise, any amount of advance
payments that may not be recovered by the City shall be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to ensure that all
CAs and liquidation reports are supported with proper documentation, in
compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-001, and stop the granting of CAs for travel
under one representative, in compliance with Items 4.1.5 and 4.1.6 of COA Circular
No. 97-002.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the City Mayor require the
GFPS Technical Working Group (TWG) to ensure that all PPAs included in the
GPB have well-defined gender issues or GAD mandate to be addressed.

We likewise recommended that the City Mayor direct the M&E Team as created by
EO No. 32 dated September 9, 2022 and other concerned officials to fully comply
with the establishment of a GAD M&E System as required under Sections 3.4 and
5.1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC Nos. 2013-01 and 2016-01.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to cause the
immediate reversion to the unappropriated surplus in the GF the AP that have
been outstanding for at least two years and which could not be supported with
complete documentation.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to submit to the
Audit Team all necessary documents to prove the validity of the recorded AP,
otherwise make the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to fairly
present the balances of the affected accounts in the financial statements.

Furthermore, we recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to
ensure that the projects are already 100 percent complete prior to issuing
certification to that effect to prevent unsubstantiated or premature recording of
AP by the City Accounting Office.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer, City Accountant
and other concerned officials to submit, for evaluation of the Audit Team, a written
explanation and all the necessary documents required under COA Circular No. 89-
296 and the Manual on Disposal of Government Property to justify and support the
disposal/demolition of various infrastructure assets, buildings and other
structures and the dropping from the books of the corresponding PPE accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC – GSO and City Accounting
Office to proceed with the following activities of One-Time Cleansing of PPE using
the procedural guidelines prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006, to finally
dispose or derecognize the missing PPE amounting to P46,596,823.81 and thereby
establish a reliable balance of PPE accounts, to wit:

a. Section 7.0 - Disposition for non-existing/missing PPEs;

b. Section 8.0 - Derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs without available
record of accountability; and

c. Section 9.0 - Accounting entries and disclosure in the notes to the
Financial Statements

The City should take note of the sunset provision therein emphasizing that the
procedures could no longer be used to further derecognize missing properties
and/or cleanse subsequent discrepancies or unreconciled balances in PPE
accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:

a. CGSO and City Accountant to reconcile the balances of their respective
records of inventories; and

b. City Accountant to adjust in the books the semi-expendable properties
already issued to end-users/beneficiaries using the appropriate expense
accounts based on the reports submitted by the GSO.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. Concerned accountable officers to submit to the GSO the accomplished
forms as required under Section C of Part II of the Manual on Disposal of
Government Property; and

b. Disposal committee to facilitate the immediate disposal of the
unserviceable properties, including the determination of the appropriate
mode of disposal, appraisal of the same to prevent further deterioration and
reduction of the properties’ economic value.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and the City
Accountant to ensure the timely release of the barangays’ shares from RPT
collections in compliance with Section 271(d) of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to require every
Accountable Officer who will receive a cash advance to strictly observe the
prescribed due date for the liquidation of his/her cash advance, in compliance with
COA Circular No. 97-002 and LTOM 2nd Edition, 2019, Book III, to safeguard cash
from the risk of misuse or loss.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a. Require the City Health Officer to strictly require from the suppliers the
CPR from the BFAD; and

b. Require the inspection and acceptance committee to submit the AIR to
COA within 24 hours from receipt of the deliveries as prescribed in COA
Circular Nos. 92-386.

We recommended that the City Mayor enforce timely project implementation by
requiring the City Engineer, in coordination with City Planning and Development
Officer, to:

a. Conscientiously carry out detailed engineering to address any issues on the
viability of the project sites to ensure that projects are implementation-ready
before they are posted for bidding;

b. Thoroughly review and evaluate the Program of Works to ensure that project
designs and estimates are properly prepared and suit the needs of end-users
and that all phases of the projects are covered to minimize time extensions; and

c. Strictly monitor and supervise the status/progress of projects being
implemented.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to compute,
withhold and remit to the BIR the taxes due on the honoraria of chairman,
secretariat and members of the BAC and TWG whose total annual taxable income
exceed the P250,000.00 exemption threshold, in accordance with pertinent BIR
laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the CDRRMC, through the
CDRRMO, to include under separate caption the unexpended balances of
LDRRMF in previous years and the programs or activities which will be charged to
the funds as determined by the LDRRMC and approved by the SP in the Annual
LDRRMF Investment Plan to facilitate the monitoring and evaluation of the
utilization of the funds.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City GSO and City Accounting Office
to proceed with the following activities of One-Time Cleansing of PPE using the
procedural guidelines prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006, to establish a reliable
balance of PPE accounts:
(a) Section 6.3 - Reconcilation of inventory count per RPCPPE with property and
accounting records;
(b) Section 7.0 - Disposition for non-existing/missing PPEs;
(c) Section 8.0 - Derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs without available
record of accountability; and
(d) Section 9.0 - Accounting entries and disclosure in the Notes to the Financial
Statements
Also, we recommended that the City take note of the sunset provisions therein
emphasizing that the procedures could no longer be used to further derecognize
missing properties and/or cleanse subsequent discrepancies or unreconciled
balances in PPE accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Accountant, in coordination with the City Assessor, the City GSO and
other concerned officials, to identify all the land owned by the City and ensure
that all properties owned by the City are accounted in the books;
b. City Accountant, in coordination with the City GSO, to analyze and identify to a
specific parcel of land the survey cost and other expenses totaling
P183,223.65, otherwise make the necessary adjustments by charging these
amounts to the Prior Years’ Adjustment account;
c. City Accountant to reclassify the road lots totaling P1,745,600.00 from the
Land account to the Road Networks account to achieve consistency in the
presentation of accounts;
d. City Accountant and/or the City GSO to submit to the Audit Team copies of
proof of ownership of 11 parcels of lot recorded in the books and inventory
records;
e. City GSO or the Office concerned/assigned to facilitate the registration and
transfer of the titles of the parcels of lot/land determined to be owned by the
City Government of Trece Martires from the previous owner/s to the City
government; and
f. City Accounting Office to maintain RPLC and the City GSO maintain PCs for all
lands owned by the City.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a. Property/Supplies custodian to furnish the City GSO copies of documents and
reports on the receipts and issuances of donated medicines and supplies for
their recording to reconcile the discrepancies noted above with the records of
the Accounting Office; and
b. OIC-TMMP Officer to issue RIS or other related document for every issued/sold
item to be summarized weekly in the SSMI, certified correct by the City GSO and
submit the same to the City Accounting Office for preparation of Journal Entry
Voucher (JEV) to record cost of goods/items sold or issued, to reflect the
correct balance of the Merchandise Inventory account for that period.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accounting Office, City GSO and
the City Agriculturist to coordinate and come up with an updated report on the status,
condition, location, accountability and/or other relevant information needed for
proper recording and/or derecognizing of biological assets in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a. City Accountant to prepare an adjusting entry to record the unrecorded
Accounts Payable account of P45,757,044.16, the related PPE accounts of
P11,338,659.93, and the Construction in Progress account of P34,418,384.23; and
b. City Accountant to coordinate with the City Engineering Office for the
submission of the Status of Completion of Projects as of year-end to ensure
that all unpaid completed and on-going projects are recorded in the books of
accounts to reflect the correct balances of Accounts Payable and the
counterpart asset accounts at year-end.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to prepare and
draw the necessary adjusting entries to correctly classify the liabilities to their
respective accounts, and observe the proper classification of accounts in accordance
with the RCALGU.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant:
a. In coordination with other Offices, inform in writing the concerned personnel
and/or contractors about their unclaimed money with the advice that the amount
will be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund of the City
should proper claim be not made within reasonable period;
b. To thereafter cause the immediate reversion of the dormant Other Payables to
the unappropriated surplus of the general fund aged more than two years; and
c. To prepare and draw the necessary adjusting entries to correctly classify the
liabilities to their respective accounts, and observe the proper classification of
accounts in accordance with the RCALGU.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to:
a. Conduct an in-depth review/analysis of the accounts with negative balances
and make the necessary adjusting entries in the accounting system to reflect
the correct subsidiary ledger balances for all accounts; and
b. Ensure that all entries made in the accounting system are accurate and
produce reliable records, in particular the financial statements for special
accounts in the General Fund, with minimal human intervention.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned implementing office or
department to coordinate with the source agency or if not known, with the DBM, on
the proper utilization or liquidation of the amount granted as well as the refund of
excess project funds to the trustor, if so provided in the terms of the grant and/or to
seek approval from the trustor to revert the balances of these long outstanding
dormant accounts, to the City’s unappropriated surplus of the General Fund.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Engineer, City Planning and Development Coordinator, and Bids and Awards
Committee to ensure the conscientiously carrying out of detailed engineering
activities before procuring and awarding contracts for infrastructure projects to
ensure that issues on right of way, soil, relocation plan, and plan approvals are
properly addressed prior to project implementation to avoid delays and/or
suspension of projects, otherwise they will be jointly and severally liable with the
contractor for liquidated damages;
b. City Engineer or any concerned officials to strictly observe the grounds on the
issuance of suspension orders or time extensions as provided under Sections 10.1
and 11.2 of Annex E of the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184 so that liquidated damages
are imposed to contractors/supplier incurring delays; and
c. City Engineer or any concerned officials to submit draft suspension orders or time
extensions the City Mayor for her signature and approval prior to its release to the
suppliers/contractors to ensure its validity.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Engineer and the City Accountant to submit written justification to the
COA Audit Team on why the Extra Work Order exceeding 10 percent of the
contract was not bid out and submit to the COA Audit Team the noted lacking
documents to support the variation orders on the subject eight projects,
otherwise pertinent disbursements may be suspended or disallowed in audit;
b. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and the City Engineer to strictly adhere to
the provisions of Annex E, RIRR of RA 9184 relative to the issuance of
variation orders and make sure that any cumulated positive change/extra work
orders beyond 10 percent of the original contract price shall be bid out in
another contract; and
c. City Engineer to make sure that all documentary requirements enumerated in
Item 9.1.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 for changes in the contracts were
prepared before proceeding the works and attached to the disbursement
vouchers submitted to the COA Auditor to support the changes made.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a. City GSO to prepare the RSPI for issued semi-expendable properties totaling
P2,150,573.00 and the Accounting Office to prepare a JEV to record the same
as expense using the Prior Period Adjustment account to reflect the correct
balance of PED account; and
b. City GSO to prepare and maintain SPCs and the Accounting Office to prepare
and maintain RegSPI and SPLCs for all semi-expendable properties in
compliance with COA Circular No. 2022-004.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Offices involved in the
payment/disbursement process to monitor, possibly through the use of a routing slip,
the flow of documents to identify the cause of delays and/or areas that require
improvement so that the City may comply with the aforementioned Manual and
conduct its operations efficiently.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City General Services Officer to
cause the registration of the 11 unregistered motor vehicles and the renewal of
registration of the 36 motor vehicles with the Land Transportation Office in
compliance with Section 5 of Batas Pambansa Bilang 74.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a. City Treasurer to obtain explanation from the Accountable Officer (AO) with
overages in collection, and if the overages cannot be satisfactorily explained
by the AO, forfeit the amount in favor of the City by issuing an Official Receipt
and record the overage as “Miscellaneous Income”;
b. City Treasurer to demand from the AO the immediate settlement of their
unremitted collections, otherwise deduct the amount from their salaries, and
thereafter make sure that overages and/or shortages are settled immediately
once discovered;
c. City Treasurer to institute control measures to minimize, if not totally
eliminate, errors in the recording of collections, and thereby overages and
shortages of Collecting Officers;
d. City Accountant, in coordination with concerned offices, to send letters to
creditors-employees at their last known address to inform them of their
collectibles from the City and if no response is received on the deadline given,
immediately revert these amounts to the unappropriated surplus in
accordance with COA Circular No. 76-45; and
e. City Accountant to prepare and draw the necessary adjusting entries to
correctly classify the property tax share amounting to P101,814.50 to their
proper accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to conduct annual physical count of all properties of the Municipality
and prepare and submit the RPCPPE to the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year;
b. Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to maintain property cards based on the
RPCPPE;
c. Municipal Accountant to maintain PPE ledger cards for each group of PPEs to facilitate
the reconciliation and verification of the year-end balances; and
d. MGSO and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their respective property records to come
up with the reconciled balances of PPE. Should reconciliation of the RPCPPE and
accounting records appeared to be improbable, consider availing the one-time cleansing
of PPE in accordance with Commission on Audit Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31,
2020.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
compute the depreciation expense of the infrastructure asset, prepare the lapsing
schedule, and draw necessary journal entry to take up the depreciation expense for
the current year, establish the accumulated depreciation for the Property, Plant and
Equipment (PPE) account and record the adjustment in the government equity
account for the depreciation expenses that should have been recorded in prior
years, to present fairly the financial statements in accordance with IPSAS 17 and
pertinent provisions of Item IV of COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accounting Office with the duly
certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible
for the year 2024 to enable the latter Office to set up at the beginning of the year
the RPT and SET Receivables for the current year correctly; and
b. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to analyze and investigate the
cause/s of the net difference of P13,866,974.79 between the balances of the RPT
and SET Receivables and the P27,765,662.37 discrepancy between the aggregate
amount of these two accounts and the List of Unpaid Taxes as of December 31,
2023, and make the necessary adjustments in their records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Concerned officials and employees to immediately liquidate their respective
cash advances as soon as the purpose for which the advances were granted
has been served by submitting the required Liquidation Reports and necessary
supporting documents to the Municipal Accounting Office; settle their
outstanding cash advances as of year-end and observe strict adherence to the
rules and regulations in the granting, utilization and liquidation of the cash
advances; and
a. Municipal Accountant to ensure that additional grant of cash advances to
officers and employees is processed only if their previous cash advances have
already been accounted and settled, and that all must be liquidated by the end
of the year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned Municipal officials
to expedite the implementation of the programs and projects for which the
transferred funds are intended, or should it be determined that the projects are not
anymore feasible or there had been no more use for the funds, to instruct the
Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to immediately remit the
P350,058.19 to the respective source agencies or the Bureau of Treasury.

We reiterated our previous years’ audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
instruct the Municipal Accountant to file a request for authority to write off the
dormant and/or unliquidated receivables amounting to P404,467.50 to the COA,
following the guidelines and procedures provided by COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal General Services Officer to maintain stock cards to monitor and
control the procurement and issuance of supplies and materials, prepare the
monthly SSMI at the end of every month summarizing all the approved RIS,
submit them to the Municipal Accountant as the basis for recording the expense
account and conduct complete physical count of all inventory items on a
semestral basis and submit the corresponding RPCI not later than July 31 and
January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively; and
b. Municipal Accountant to record all deliveries of supplies and materials in the
appropriate ledger cards upon the receipt of Inspection and Acceptance Report
and other supporting documents and prepare the JEVs to take up the
procurement made in the books, and the expenditures/issuance made based on
the submitted SSMI at the end of each month.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Development
Council, the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, the Municipal
Engineer and the Bid and Awards Committee (BAC) Chairperson to:
a. thoroughly plan and review all development projects to ensure that all issues
and concerns relating to the projects are addressed and resolved prior to their
inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan, ensuring proper facilitation of project
completion on time;
b. program the appropriated funds to development programs that are well-
planned, procurement-ready, necessary, suitable and essential to promote the
effective and efficient local governance and the general welfare of the
constituents; and
c. strictly adhere to the provisions provided by DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1, s. 2020
to achieve greater autonomy, transparency, and accountability in the City’s
appropriation and utilization of its 20 percent DF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (MDRRMC) to identify
and incorporate, under a separate caption, in the LDRRMFIP the projects and
activities to be charged out of the unexpended previous years’ LDRRMF and the
continuing appropriations, if any, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002; and
b. Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers of the unexpended
LDRRMF by year of transfer to facilitate the monitoring of the movements in the
account and of the five-year period within which they shall remain in the Special
Trust Fund, and consequently, the reversion of any unexpended balance to the
General Fund after said period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction Management Council (MDRRMC) to ensure that all projects and
programs in the LDRRMFIP are well-planned and properly executed to enhance the
preparedness and response capabilities of the Municipality and its constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MHRMO, in coordination
with the Department Heads of the Municipality, to:
a. ensure that the contracts of the JO clearly provide their job descriptions, duties and
expected deliverables against which their performance and accomplishments, and the
necessity of their hiring and regularity of payments to them would be evaluated; and
b. study the Municipality's manpower needs and hire JO workers based on specific
requests from concerned Department Heads or officials who shall certify that his or
her office needs the services of a JO worker for an identified piece of work.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and other concerned officials to facilitate the
review of the technical aspects of the contracts, detailing the necessary information in
the determination of the reasonableness of the contract amount, the scope of work of
the Contractors, and the supporting documents to enable the Audit Team to conduct
a systematic and effective auditorial review and technical evaluation of the contracts; and
b. Municipal Accountant to ensure completeness and propriety of relevant supporting
documents pertaining to garbage hauling and disposal.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Technical Working Group and
the GAD Focal Point System (TWG-GFPS) to undergo trainings on HGDG to build
their competence in the conduct of gender analysis of programs and projects for
purpose of GAD budget attribution.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairperson of the
TWG-GFPS to conduct gender analysis through the administration of HGDG tool
from the planning and budgeting phase to the implementation, monitoring and
evaluation phase, apply the results thereof in the preparation of the GAD Plan and
Budget and the accompanying GAD Accomplishment Report and the identification
of strategies to address gaps or missing elements, if any, to properly determine and
gradually increase the gender-responsiveness of the GAD programs and activities
and ensure that gender issues were addressed accordingly.

We reiterated the prior years’ recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a) Inventory Committee to conduct annual physical count of all properties of the Municipality and submit a RPCPPE to the Commission on Audit (COA) Audit Team on or before January 31 of the following year;
b) Property Custodian to maintain property cards for all properties of the Municipality and reconcile these balances with the PPE Ledger Cards of the Accounting Office and the Property Acknowledgement Receipts (PARs); and
We also recommended that should reconciliation of the RPCPPE and accounting records appeared to be improbable, the Municipality shall consider availing the one-time cleansing of PPE in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, and the Municipal Treasurer or the designated property custodian to identify all the land owned by the Municipality and ensure that all properties owned by the Municipality are properly accounted in the books to avoid discrepancies between the records;
b. Municipal Treasurer, the designated property custodian or the Office concerned/assigned to facilitate the registration and transfer of the titles of the procured parcels of lot/land from the previous owner/s to the Municipal government; and
c. Municipal Accounting Office to maintain RPLC and the Municipal Treasurer or the designated property custodian to maintain PCs for all lands owned by the Municipality.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant, MENRO and the Municipal Agriculturist to coordinate and come up with an updated report on the status, condition, location, accountability and/or other relevant information needed for proper recording and/or derecognizing of biological assets in the books of accounts;
b. Municipal Agriculturist to prepare a MOA duly signed by the Municipality and the current farmer-beneficiaries of the Municipal’s Cattle Dispersal Program; and thereafter ensure that perfected MOAs are prepared on every transfer or turn-over to establish obligation and accountability for the cattle; and
c. Municipal Agriculturist to submit to the Audit Team the supporting documents on the two deceased cows as required in Item 4 of the MOA; and thereafter ensure compliance of both parties with the requirements written in the MOA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to stop the practice of granting additional cash advances to any official, officer or employee with existing unliquidated cash advances.
Additionally, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider designating additional Special Disbursing Officer/s to handle cash advances for special activities/projects of the Municipality, if necessary.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and other concerned officials to:
a. ensure that PPAs charged against the 20 percent Development Plan are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready by conducting extensive planning and coordination with other concerned government agencies prior to its inclusion in the AIP to ensure that the projects to be incorporated therein are not among those projects to be implemented by other agencies; and
b. ensure the timely implementation and procurement of the projects in accordance with the schedules on the approved Annual Procurement Plan.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office to ensure the implementation of projects, programs, and activities allocated in the LDRRMFIP to enhance the disaster capabilities of the Municipality; and
b. Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (MDRRMC) to identify and incorporate, under a separate caption, in the LDRRMFIP the projects and activities to be charged out of the unexpended previous years’ LDRRMF pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal LDRRM Officer to refrain from obligating and charging the LDRRMF for uniforms, which should instead be charged against the annual clothing allowance and/or other budgetary account under the General Fund as may be allowed under existing laws, rules and regulations; otherwise such transaction will be disallowed in audit; and
b. Municipal Budget Officer to revert the amount of P136,800.00 from the General Fund to the Trust Liability - DRRMF representing improperly charged expenditures to the LDRRM Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant or his authorized representative to:
a. submit all the lacking supporting documents on the grant of Confidential/Intelligence Funds in CY 2023 enumerated above to the COA Audit Team Leader; and
b. ensure that succeeding releases of advances for CF/IF are compliant with the requirements of Section 6.1 of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND JMC 2015-01, otherwise, this will not be stamped as “received” by the COA ATL or the authorized representative.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. through the Acting Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO), hire and/or promote qualified personnel to fill out vacant plantilla positions first before resorting to the hiring of JO workers so that deserving personnel may enjoy the benefits associated with plantilla positions;
b. require the Acting HRMO and other concerned officials to come up with internal policy that will govern the recruitment of JO, including the terms and conditions of their hiring and other working arrangements, and the expected deliverables or accomplishments, taking into account existing laws, and civil service rules and regulations; and
c. require the Acting HRMO and the Municipal Accountant to make sure that proper documentations, such accomplishment reports, authority to render additional work hours and claim travelling allowances, among others, duly signed by the immediate supervisor and approved by the Municipal Mayor are attached to the claims.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer or the designated Property Custodian to cause the registration of the five motor vehicles and the renewal of registration of the five other motor vehicles with the Land Transportation Office in compliance with Section 5 of Batas Pambansa Bilang 74, and thereafter ensure that all vehicles of the Municipality are insured with the GSIS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, together with his Co-Chairman and other members of the Local School Board:
a. regularly monitor and evaluate the balance of the SEF to determine the actual unexpended balance thereof and ensure its inclusion in the SEF budget in the ensuing year; and
b. review and approve the SEF Budget for the ensuing year using the prescribed format of the SEF Budget Preparation Form No. 1 on or before October 31 of the current year; and
c. ensure that the LSB Resolution and other pertinent documents are duly signed by the required numbers of LSB members for validity, otherwise all disbursements made out of SEF budget will be suspended or disallowed in audit for lack of legal basis.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC Secretariat to furnish the COA Auditors with copies of all POs within five days from their execution pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001, and ensure that POs are properly accomplished containing all the basic data or information required under COA Circular No. 96-010.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the officer/s responsible for accepting deliveries to notify the Auditors of incoming deliveries of goods within 24 hours from the scheduled acceptance so that inspection could be timely conducted by the Auditors, if warranted, and regularity of the deliveries could be effectively established.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) require the Municipal Accountant, as the one certifying the completeness and propriety of supporting documents, to submit written explanation on why there are erasures/alterations on the above documents; and thereafter ensure that all attachments to the disbursement vouchers are properly accomplished, without erasures, to avoid doubts on their regularity; and
b) require the Municipal Engineer to make sure that the NOA are properly received by the supplier with dates of receipt written in the document itself.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the LFC to analyze the trend in the collection of tax revenues in the previous years and determine the amount of income which has reasonable assurance of collectivity and use the data as inputs or benchmark in the preparation of Annual Budget for the ensuing fiscal year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Finance Committee to adopt measures to eliminate the cash overdraft by exercising diligence, prudence, and proper control in the disposition of the Municipality’s funds and limit expenditures within the total income realized and collected during the year and reduce unnecessary operating expenditures until the cash overdraft is eliminated.

We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Finance Committee to make a thorough analysis of the tax and non-tax revenues collected and realized for the previous years and use the results of such analysis as basis in the preparation of an attainable Annual Budget for the ensuing fiscal year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, Municipal Engineer, Municipal Budget Officer and other concerned officials to ensure the optimal and timely utilization of 20 percent DF in the succeeding period by including development projects that are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to ensure the timely release of the barangays’ shares from RPT collections in compliance with Section 271(d) of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to increase the amount of fidelity bond of the subject AOs to cover the amount of their respective cash advances pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 to protect the interest of the Municipal Government, otherwise, limit the amount of their cash advances up to the maximum cash accountability of their fidelity bond.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. require the contractors of the seven completed infrastructure projects to submit valid financial instruments equivalent to the retention money totaling P904,549.68 which should have a validity period of one year from the date of issuance of the Certificates of Completion; and
b. strictly impose the deduction of the 10 percent retention money to the payments to contractors or require the submission of valid financial instrument equivalent to the retention money in case the contractor has requested for its substitution which will be accepted only if the project is on schedule and satisfactorily undertaken, and to release the retention money to contractors upon final acceptance of the project, in compliance with Section 6 of the 2016 RIRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (MDRRMC) to identify and incorporate, under a separate caption, in the LDRRMFIP the projects and activities to be charged out of the unexpended previous years’ LDRRMF pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer to ensure implementation of projects and programs in accordance with the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) to enhance the preparedness and response capabilities of the Municipality and its constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the General Services Officer to cause the insurance of the government motor vehicles against any insurable risk and pay the premiums consistent with the provisions of RA No. 656.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
1. a. direct the Municipal Accountant and the General Service Officer (GSO) to proceed
with the preliminary activities on the one-time cleansing of PPE, particularly the
listing and comparison of PPE items between the latest Report of Physical Count of
PPE (RPCPPE) and PPELCs but excluding the lump-sum PPE balances in the latter
report from the inventory working papers, and henceforth follow the guidelines
provided under COA Circular No. 2020-006 and if applicable, take up the necessary
accounting entries/drop from the books of accounts the remaining balances of
amounts lumped under the “Unreconciled SL,” “Reconciling SL” for PPEs, and the
like pursuant to Item 6.3.2(e) thereof or to immediately refer to the COA issues and
concerns that the Municipality may encounter during the conduct of one-time
cleansing of PPE, if any; and
b. direct the Municipal Accountant to record in the books of accounts of the Municipality
the appropriate adjusting entry pertaining to the payment for the acquisition of
auxiliary customized play equipment costing P999,500.00 in CY 2023 which was
subsequently donated to Barangay Poblacion I-A, Maragondon, and to provide the
Audit Team with the copy of the corresponding Journal of Entry Voucher for review
and validation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to record
all check disbursements based on the Report of Checks Issued prepared by the Office
of the Municipal Treasurer and not on the approved disbursement vouchers with
prepared checks alone, pursuant to Section 43 of the MNGAS, Volume 1, to ensure
the fair presentation of the Cash in Bank – Local Currency, Current Account and
liability accounts.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Acting Municipal
Treasurer to prepare and submit to the Accounting Office the RCI together with the
disbursement vouchers with issued checks on daily basis to facilitate the timely
recording of the check disbursements by the latter.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
take-up the necessary adjusting entry for the liquidation of the cash advances for
Confidential Funds of the former Municipal Mayors totaling P6,220,000.00 in the
books of accounts and to submit to the Audit Team the corresponding Journal Entry
Voucher/s supported with the copy of the duly received liquidation reports for review
and validation.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
require the three elected municipal officials to immediately submit to the latter the
required liquidation reports on their cash advances totaling P130,000.00 and/or
refund immediately to the Municipality’s cashier/collecting officer the unutilized cash
advance, if there was any. Otherwise, direct the Municipal Treasurer to withhold the
payment of their salary due to their failure to liquidate their cash advance within the
prescribed period, pursuant to Section 5.1.3 of the COA Circular No. 97-002 dated
February 10, 1997.

Further we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to send a demand letter to the former Sangguniang Bayan Member who received cash
advance amounting to P50,000.00 requiring him to immediately submit the required
liquidation report and/or refund immediately to the Municipality’s
cashier/collecting officer the unutilized cash advance, if there was any. Otherwise,
direct both the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to deduct the amount
equivalent to his unliquidated cash advance to any payment that was still due to him.

Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to properly orient all Municipal officials and employees who will receive
cash advances on the prescribed periods to be observed in the liquidation thereof, and
the possible consequences in case of their failure to observe the same, as provided
in Sections 5.1 and 9.3 of the COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a) fully adopt the Perpetual Inventory Method by recording all the acquisitions of
supplies and materials, except those purchased out of the Petty Cash Fund, to an
Inventory account;

b) prepare and maintain SLCs for each type of supplies and materials for the proper
recording of the receipts and issuances of the same and monitoring of their balances
by the Accounting Office;

c) record the issuances of supplies and materials based on the SSMI to be prepared
and submitted by the General Services Office; and

d) record the appropriate adjusting entry for the acquisition of PPE amounting to
P126,684.00 which was improperly recorded to Other Supplies and Materials
Expense and to provide the Audit Team with the corresponding Journal of Entry
Voucher for review and validation. Henceforth, ensure that all transactions are
recorded in the proper accounts to avoid similar misstatements in the succeeding
periods.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the General Services Officer to:

a) prepare and maintain SCs for each type of supplies and materials for the proper
recording of the receipts and issuances of the same and monitoring of their
balances by the General Services Office; and

b) prepare and submit to the Accounting Office the SSMI together with the original
copy of the RIS to enable the latter to properly record the issuances of supplies and
materials in the books of accounts.

Further we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee
to conduct the complete physical count of all the supplies and materials every
semester and to submit the corresponding RPCI to the Accounting Office and General
Services Office not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and
second semesters, respectively, to facilitate reconciliation of their respective
subsidiary records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Assessor and other
concerned Municipal officials to:

a) take the necessary actions to expedite the registration of the titles on the five fully
paid parcels of land totaling P20,456,000.00 under the name of the Municipality to
safeguard its interest over these properties against possible third-party claims;

b) submit proof of payment of CGT and DST for the acquisition of land totaling
P2,400,000.00; and

c) submit written explanation on why the total consideration per Deed of Sale and
selling price per CAR for the partially paid land with check number 1033538 dated
April 26, 2023, had a discrepancy amounting to P134,998.20, for review and
validation of the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
require the Municipal Engineer and the concerned contractors to submit the lacking
documentary requirements for the disbursements for progress billings totaling
P6,947,288.93 on four partially completed infrastructure projects and for the
disbursements for first and final progress billing totaling P23,125,297.21 on
25 completed infrastructure projects for evaluation of the Audit Team, otherwise
these transactions will be suspended in audit.

We also recommended that henceforth, the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to ensure that all the documentary requirements for payments of
progress billing and final progress billing/first and final progress billing prescribed
by the COA Circular No. 2023-004 dated June 14, 2023 are submitted to her by the
Municipal Engineer and concerned contractors before certifying in the DV that the
documentary requirements are complete, to avoid suspension of transaction in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to strictly
impose the defects liability period against the contractors by issuing the Certificate
of Acceptance for completed infrastructure projects one year after the date of the
issuance of the Certificate of Completion, in compliance with the pertinent provisions
of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 and the Local Government Units Procurement
Manual.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to require
the concerned contractors to immediately install the project signboards of the four
awarded and ongoing infrastructure projects totaling P7,960,635.82 within the
vicinity of the project location considering that cost for the same was included in
their total contract cost.

We also recommended that henceforth, the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Engineer to strictly observe the pertinent provisions of the COA Circular No. 2013-004
dated January 30, 2013 relative to the posting of the project signboard to ensure full
transparency and accountability in the implementation of the infrastructure
projects of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a) LDRRMC, through the MDRRMO, to include under separate caption the
unexpended balances of LDRRMF in previous years and the programs or
activities which will be charge to the funds as determined by the LDRRMC and
approved by the Sangguniang Bayan in the Annual LDRRMF Investment Plan to
facilitate the monitoring and evaluation of the utilization of the funds;

b) MDRRMO to submit to the Audit Team the LDRRMC Resolution, or any equivalent
document, showing the approval and recommendation of the Council to re-align
the unexpended funds in CY 2022 amounting to P1,500,000.00 which were utilized
on various expenses for CBMS; and

c) Municipal Accountant to maintain the subsidiary ledgers of the unexpended
balances of LDDRMF by year of transfer to facilitate the monitoring of the
five-year period in which the funds shall remain in the Special Trust Fund and the
reversion of any remaining balance to the General Fund after said period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Administrator and all
other concerned Municipal officials to have a consultation meeting with the RTF-
ELCAC and barangay officials of the Barangay Sta. Mercedes (Patungan) to properly
evaluate the probability of implementation of the two projects chargeable against the
LGSF - SBDP of the NTF-ELCAC within the validity period for the use of the funds or
until December 31, 2024, and if determined not probable, the Municipality should
immediately refund the amount received totaling P6,606,882.17 to the National
Treasury, pursuant to Section 4.9 of the LBC No. 151 dated March 30, 2023.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a) require the contractors of the five on-going infrastructure projects to refund to the
Municipality the retention money totaling P929,823.76 that should have been
Page 36 of 27
deducted to the payments for progress billing or the contractors may request to
substitute the same with valid financial instruments equivalent to the retention
money which may be accepted by the Municipality as long as the projects are on
schedule and satisfactorily undertaken;

b) require the contractors of the seven completed infrastructure projects which had
not yet been issued with Certificates of Acceptance to refund to the Municipality
the retention money totaling P717,618.38 that should have been deducted to the
payments to contractors or they may request to substitute the same with valid
financial instruments equivalent to the retention money posted in favor of the
Municipality;

c) require the contractors of the 18 completed infrastructure projects which had
already been issued with Certificates of Acceptance to submit valid financial
instruments equivalent to the retention money totaling P2,033,971.13 which
should have a validity period of one year from the date of issuance of the
Certificates of Completion; and

d) strictly impose the deduction of the 10 percent retention money to the payments
to contractors or require the submission of valid financial instrument equivalent
to the retention money in case the contractor has requested for its substitution
which will be accepted only if the project is on schedule and satisfactorily
undertaken, and to release the retention money to contractors upon final
acceptance of the project, in compliance with Section 6 of the 2016 Revised IRR
of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the General Services Officer to
prepare the ICS for the semi-expendable properties totaling P892,157.74 which were
issued to the end-users in CY 2023 to properly establish the accountability on said
assets.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer
to prepare ICS on all issuances of semi-expendable properties and to prepare and
maintain the RegSPI for each class of issued semi-expendable properties to ensure
that the accountability on such assets is properly established and monitored, in
compliance with Sections 4.1, 4.7.3, and 4.7.4 of the COA Circular No. 2022-004
dated May 31, 2022.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to conduct annual physical count of all properties of the Municipality;
b. Municipal General Services Officer to maintain property cards for each class of property, plant and equipment which shall be used as one of bases for the preparation of a comprehensive and reliable RPCPPE; and
c. Municipal General Services Officer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their respective property records to come up with the reconciled balances of PPE. Should reconciliation of the RPCPPE and accounting records appeared to be improbable, consider availing the one-time cleansing of PPE in accordance with Commission on Audit Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Local Finance Committee to recommend fiscal policies and measures to improve the financial performance of the Municipality; and
b. Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to monitor the expenditures of the Municipality and ensure that they do not exceed the target revenue and/or total income realized during the year to avoid incurrence of net loss.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Finance Committee to adopt measures to eliminate the cash overdraft by exercising diligence, prudence and proper control in the disposition of the Municipality’s funds and limit expenditures within the total income realized and collected during the year and reduce unnecessary operating expenditures until the cash overdraft is eliminated.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Finance Committee to make a thorough analysis of the tax and non-tax revenues collected and realized for the previous year and use the result of such analysis as basis in the preparation of an attainable Annual Budget for the ensuing fiscal year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Concerned officials and employees to immediately liquidate their respective cash advances as soon as the purpose for which the advances were granted has been served by submitting the required Liquidation Reports and necessary supporting documents to the Municipal Accounting Office; settle their outstanding cash advances as of year-end and observe strict adherence to the rules and regulations in the granting, utilization and liquidation of the cash advances;
b. Municipal Accountant to ensure that additional grant of cash advances to officers and employees is processed only if their previous cash advances have already been accounted and settled, and that all must be liquidated by the end of the year; and
c. Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to refrain from using cash advances to pay regular expenses and instead issue checks directly under the names of the payees, suppliers or contractors.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the bank reconciliation statements and submit copies thereof, with all of the necessary supporting documents, to the Audit Team for verification within ten days after the end of each month, and to draw journal entry vouchers to take up all valid reconciling items requiring adjustments and corrections in the books, to establish the reliability and accuracy of the Cash in Bank accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned Municipal officials to expedite the implementation of the programs and projects for which the transferred funds are intended, or should it be determined that the projects are not anymore feasible or there had been no more use for the funds, to instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to immediately remit the P1,502,614.44 to the respective source agencies or the Bureau of Treasury.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal General Services Officer to maintain stock cards to monitor and control the procurement and issuance of supplies and materials, prepare the monthly SSMI at the end of every month summarizing all the approved RISs, submit them to the Municipal Accountant as the basis for recording the expense account and conduct complete physical count of all inventory items on a semestral basis and submit the corresponding RPCI not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively; and
b. Municipal Accountant to record all deliveries of supplies and materials in the appropriate ledger cards upon the receipt of Inspection and Acceptance Report and other supporting documents then prepare the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) to take up (1) the procurement made in the books, and (2) the expenditures/issuance made based on the submitted SSMI at the end of each month.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MHRDMO, in coordination with the Department Heads of the Municipality, to:
a. ensure that the contracts of the JO clearly provide their job descriptions, duties and expected deliverables against which their performance and accomplishments, and the necessity of their hiring and regularity of payments to them would be evaluated against; and
b. study the Municipality's manpower needs and hire JO workers based on specific requests from concerned Department Heads or officials who shall certify that his or her office needs the services of a JO worker for an identified piece of work.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairperson of the Technical Working Group GFPS (TWG-GFPS) to conduct gender analysis through the administration of HGDG tool from the planning and budgeting phase to the implementation, monitoring and evaluation phase, apply the results thereof in the preparation of the GAD Plan and Budget and the accompanying GAD Accomplishment Report and the identification of strategies to address gaps or missing elements, if any, to properly determine and gradually increase the gender-responsiveness of the GAD programs and activities and ensure that gender issues were addressed accordingly.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the
GSO to proceed with the preliminary activities on the One-Time Cleansing of PPE,
particularly the listing and comparison of PPE items between the latest Report of
Physical Count of PPE and PPELCs but excluding the lump-sum PPE balances in the
latter report from the inventory working papers, and henceforth follow the guidelines
provided under COA Circular No. 2020-006 to properly address the unreconciled
balances on PPE records.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
determine the cause/s of the noted differences between the general ledger balances
and PPELCs and make the necessary adjustments to ensure that both records are
reconciled. In addition, make sure to update the PPELCs to include the quantity, unit
cost, and accumulated depreciation of all PPE items.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. update the unit cost of each stock of supplies and materials in the SLCs every
time that there is acquisition/delivery and to record the issuances of the same
based on the SSMI duly supported with RIS to be submitted by the GSO and using
the computed moving average unit cost, pursuant with Section 121 of the MNGAS
for LGUs, Volume I;
b. verify with the GSO the status of the recorded balances of the SEP in the books
of accounts and if there were issuances thereof to the end-users, require the
latter to submit the corresponding RSPI duly supported with ICS to facilitate the
recording of the issuances in the books of accounts; and
c. prepare and maintain the SPLC for the proper recording of the receipts,
issuances/transfers, and disposals, and monitoring of the balances of the semi-
expendable properties by the Accounting Office and to record the
issuances/transfers thereof based on the RSPI duly supported with ICS to be
submitted by the GSO, pursuant with Section 4.7 of the COA Circular No. 2022-
004.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GSO to prepare and
maintain the SPC and RegSPI for the proper recording of the receipts,
issuances/transfers, and disposals, and monitoring of the issued semi-expendable
properties, respectively, by the General Services Office and to prepare and submit
the RSPI together with the ICS to the Municipal Accountant at least weekly to facilitate
the recording of the issued semi-expendable properties in the books of accounts.

Further we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
and GSO to reconcile their respective records on inventory accounts with the
Inventory Report of the Inventory Committee and to undertake necessary actions on
the discrepancies, in accordance with Chapter 5 of the Training Handbook on
Property and Supply Management System.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to record
all check disbursements based on the Report of Checks Issued prepared by the Office
of the Municipal Treasurer and not on the Accountant’s Advice of Local Check
Disbursements, pursuant to Section 43 of the MNGAS, Volume 1, to ensure the fair
presentation of the Cash in Bank – Local Currency, Current Account and liability
accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a) General Services Officer to submit to the Municipal Accountant the copy of the
Deed of Donation and other supporting documents for the three units of motor
vehicle donated to the Municipality with total cost of P3,500,000.00 for recording
in the books of accounts;
b) General Services Officer and Municipal Accountant to check their respective
records pertaining to the two units of motor vehicle with total cost of P478,000.00
recorded in the General Ledger but not included in the Inventory Report in order
to locate the aforesaid assets and/or correct the records, as well as the records
pertaining to the one unit of motor vehicle in which the recorded cost had a noted
discrepancy of P48,803.56 between the Accounting and GSO records;
c) Municipal Accountant to record the appropriate adjusting entry pertaining to the
two units of motor vehicle with total cost of P3,641,000.00 which were recorded to
Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment account and Medical, Dental and
Laboratory Equipment account; and
d) General Services Officer to coordinate with the Donor of the six units of motor
vehicle for the lacking documents required for the transfer of ownership of the
same to the Municipality and with the City of Bacoor for the Deed of Donation,
MOA, or any related document to confirm the ownership or use of the four units
of motor vehicle.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Market Supervisor to:
a) locate the records pertaining to the recorded Operating Lease Receivable balance
of P528,800.00 which remained in the books of accounts since CY 2021 to verify
if those were really unpaid and to demand payment from all the concerned
stallholders, if warranted;
b) verify the net understatement amounting to P91,200.00 and update the existing
records and furnish the Accounting Office with the updated schedule to enable
the latter to record the necessary adjustment in the books of accounts; and
c) regularly reconcile their records of individual balances of all stallholders with the
accounting records for check and balance.

We also reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the Municipal
Mayor direct the:
a) Municipal Accountant to maintain separate financial statements for special
accounts for the proper monitoring of all the properties of the Market and Grand
Central Terminal, and to determine whether the income generated by them were
sufficient to meet their operating costs; and
b) Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reserve or set aside as
capital and management development fund at least 50 percent of the net income
from the market and terminal operations for the maintenance and improvement of
the market and for staff development.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that all municipal officials who
will receive cash advance for purpose other than for official travel have a written
designation order that will clearly set forth their authority to act as disbursing
officers, maximum cash accountability, and the purpose/s of the cash advances that
they are authorized to receive as basis of the application of fidelity bond and grant of
succeeding cash advances, pursuant to the pertinent provisions of Section 4 and 7
of the COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a) Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all
municipal officials who are designated as disbursing officers are properly
bonded in accordance with the pertinent provisions of the Treasury Circular No.
02-2009 dated August 06, 2009;
b) Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting additional cash advance to a
disbursing officer if he/she still has an unliquidated previous cash advance;
c) Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting cash advance to a disbursing
officer with amount that exceeds his/her maximum cash accountability;
d) Municipal Accountant to properly orient all the disbursing officers to report or
liquidate their cash advances as soon as purpose has been served to facilitate the
127
timely verification of the validity of the Report of Disbursement and its supporting
documents and the timely recording of the liquidation of cash advance in the
books of accounts;
e) Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer to evaluate the possibility
of implementation of Automated Payroll System in the payment of honoraria of
the Contract of Service personnel and payment of honoraria of the consultants
thru check disbursement payable directly to the payee to reduce the cash
advances being handled by the disbursing officers and relieved them of huge
cash accountability; and
f) Municipal Accountant to record the payments of honoraria, registration fees and
insurance premiums of motor vehicles, and the payments to PS-DBM for e-wallet
to appropriate accounts to ensure the fair presentation of the financial
statements of the Municipality, pursuant to IPSAS 1 and the Revised Chart of
Accounts for LGUs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a) check the relevant tax records pertaining to the Due to BIR balance of P699,032.62
and if necessary, to make representation with the BIR for the proper settlement of
any balance determined to be overdue unremitted taxes; and
b) record the appropriate adjusting entries in the books of accounts to correct the
understatement and overstatement in the year-end balances of the Due to BIR
account in the General Fund and Trust Fund, respectively, and to submit the copy
of the corresponding Journal of Entry Voucher to the Audit Team for review and
validation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and
Development Coordinator to properly evaluate, in coordination with the other
concerned Municipal officials and stakeholders, the underlying conditions and
circumstances which hindered the full implementation of the aforementioned
projects funded under the 20 percent Development Fund, and to devise and
implement appropriate measures to address the same to enable the Municipality and
intended beneficiaries to enjoy the benefits which could be derived from the
implementation of such projects.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and
Development Coordinator and other concerned Municipal officials to conduct
monitoring and evaluation of the implementation of the projects funded under the 20
percent Development Fund even after their completion to ensure that the desired
socio-economic and environmental development targets and outcomes of the
Municipality are attained.

Further we reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal
Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to conscientiously carry out detailed
engineering activities before awarding the contracts for infrastructure projects to
ensure that issues are properly addressed prior to project implementation to avoid
delays, suspension and/or termination of projects, pursuant to Section 17.6 of the
Revised IRR of RA 9184. Likewise, require the BAC to always check the readiness of
the procurement of infrastructure projects, particularly those costing above
P5,000,000.00, by conducting pre-procurement conference before advertisement of
Invitation to Bid, in compliance with Section 20 of the same IRR.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Officer in Charge – Municipal
Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer to include under separate caption the
unexpended balances of LDRRMF in previous years and the PPAs which will be
charged to the funds as determined and recommended by the Local Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Council (LDRRMC) and approved by the Sangguniang
Bayan in the Annual LDRRMFIP to facilitate the monitoring and evaluation of the
utilization of the funds.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as the Chairperson of the LDRRMC,
ensure that all the subsequent utilization of the unexpended LDRRMF for new PPAs
are in accordance with the approved LDRRMFIP, as indorsed by the LDRMC, and
duly covered by a revised AIP approved by both the sangguniang bayan and by him,
pursuant to Section 6.5 of the NDRRMC, DBM, and DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated
March 25, 2013.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal HRMO to submit
the summary of actual assignment, duties and functions, and accomplishments of
the 49 JO workers designated as garbage sorter and street sweeper in CY 2023 duly
certified by her and by their respective immediate supervisor to enable the Audit
Team to evaluate the necessity of hiring them as JO workers, otherwise the payments
for wages totaling P3,820,920.00 will be suspended in audit.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal HRMO to
provide the correct designation of the JO workers hired by the Municipality in the
Appointment Contracts and Daily Wage Payrolls and to require the concerned JO
workers to prepare a correct Accomplishment Reports and Daily Time Records by
indicating their actual assignment and work done to be certified by their respective
immediate supervisor.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to
deposit all collections intact and daily or on the next banking day with the
Municipality’s depository bank to ensure that the collections are properly
safeguarded against the risk of possible loss or unauthorized use, pursuant to
Section 32 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant or his
authorized representative to make representation with the Cities of Las Pinas and
Navotas to clarify and secure documents that will establish the purpose of the fund
transfers, otherwise instruct the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and
other concerned Municipal officials to study the legality and propriety of transferring
the funds from the Trust Fund to the General Fund for the preparation of or inclusion
thereof in the supplemental budget of the Municipality for CY 2024.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal HRMO to:
a) submit written justification on why the three individuals were hired as
consultants of the Municipality to render services which can be rendered by its
regular plantilla personnel or which were similar with the duties and functions of
vacant plantilla positions, to avoid the issuance of audit disallowance;
b) evaluate the Municipality’s Plantilla of Personnel and to submit
recommendations before hiring an individual under Consultancy Service
Contract to avoid the incurrence of additional cost to the Municipality due to
hiring of consultants for services which could be rendered by its pool of plantilla
personnel, pursuant to Section 2 of Annex “B” of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA
9184; and
c) evaluate the necessity of filling the vacancies, abolishment, and/or creation of
plantilla positions to address the lack of available manpower for services which
are needed by the Municipality and its constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
check the relevant records pertaining to the Due to GSIS balance of P201,343.56 and
if necessary, to make coordination with the GSIS for the proper settlement of any
balance determined to be overdue unremitted premiums.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor
direct the Market Supervisor to:
a. prepare and issue Contracts of Lease to the remaining 106 market stallholders and
17 Grand Central Terminal stallholders to secure the interest of the
Municipality and to categorically inform the stallholders of their rights and
obligations including the revocation of right for those with more than three months
unpaid stall fees;
b. investigate and require the stallholders who sub-leased their stalls to explain their
action and submit the pertinent report thereon to the Audit Team and henceforth,
strictly enforce the provisions of the Market Code on the restriction on sub-leasing
of market stalls and revoke the lease of non-complying stallholders; and
c. advertise and offer to the public all stalls which had been idle and abandoned for
more than 30 days and strictly follow the process of awarding of stalls as stated in
Section 40 of the Revised Municipal Market Code.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to conduct complete and accurate physical inventory of PPE and
prepare the RPCPPE in prescribed format and submit to the COA Audit not later than
January 31 of each year; and
b. Municipal General Services Officer (GSO) and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their
respective property records to come up with the reconciled balances of PPE.
Should reconciliation of the RPCPPE and accounting records appeared to be
improbable, consider availing the one-time cleansing of PPE in accordance with
COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020;

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the Municipal General Services Officer (GSO) and Municipal Accountant to
reconcile their records and to reclassify as semi-expendable property the PPE with
acquisition cost of less than P50,000.00 per unit in compliance with COA Circular
No. 2022-004; and
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the adjusting entries to reclassify the
Semi-Expendable items to appropriate inventory accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the Local Finance Committee to submit a specific, measurable, attainable,
relevant, and time-bound action plan that will outline their recommended fiscal
policies and strategies to address the problem of cash overdraft that the
Municipality had been experiencing over the years;
b. direct the Municipal Budget Officer, Accountant and Treasurer to adopt or
implement measures to eliminate the cash overdraft by exercising diligence,
prudence, and proper control in the disposition of the Municipality’s funds and limit
expenditures within the total income realized and collected during the year and
reduce unnecessary operating expenditures until the cash overdraft is
eliminated; and
c. instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and Treasurer to refrain from using the
Special Education and Trust Funds for purposes other than those for which these
funds are intended, otherwise, necessary charges should be filed against them.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. enjoin the Local Finance Committee and other responsible officials to submit to
the Audit Team the basis used in the computation of the target collectible income
for CY 2023 and explain in writing, for further evaluation of the Audit Team, the
justification for the continuous failure of the Municipality to realize said target;
b. direct the Local Finance Committee, Committee on Finance, Budget and
Appropriations, Municipal Accountant and other responsible officials to submit a
specific, measurable, attainable, relevant, and time-bound action plan that will
outline their recommended fiscal policies and strategies to address the problem of
unrealized income that the Municipality had been experiencing over the years;
c. direct the Committee on Finance, Budget and Appropriations to judiciously and
meticulously review the annual budget of the Municipality in order to prevent the
inclusion of items of income that are not collectible; and
d. direct the Local Finance Committee to strictly set a more realistic budget based on
previous year’s actual performance in terms of revenues and expenses in
accordance with the existing guidelines on budget preparation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to ensure that projects under the
20 percent DF are backed up with available funds to guarantee their timely
implementation;
b. Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Municipal Budget Officer,
Municipal Engineer and other concerned officials to ensure the optimal and timely
utilization of 20 percent DF in the succeeding period by including development
projects that are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready
pursuant to DBM, DOF and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2020-1; and
c. Municipal Engineer and other concerned officials to conduct detailed engineering
and in-depth planning for each project to ensure that the priority development
projects are feasible for implementation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant, BAC and other concerned officials to submit to the COA
Audit Team for evaluation all lacking documents relative to the disbursements for
the Noveleta Information Technology Building at Poblacion, Noveleta, Cavite and
the Purchase of Heavy Equipment (Backhoe), otherwise a Notice of Suspension
will be issued, accordingly. Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant should ensure
that all payments to suppliers and contractors are supported with complete
documentation;
b. BAC to use in their procurements the prescribed Philippine Bidding Documents,
and other standard forms issued by the GPPB whenever necessary; and
c. BAC to explain in writing to the COA Audit Team, for further evaluation the inability
to invite three observers in all stages of the procurement process, and their failure
to conduct pre-bid conference, bid evaluation, and post-qualification as required
under the pertinent provisions of RIRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator,
Municipal Accountant and other responsible officials to explain in writing to the
COA Audit Team, for further evaluation, on why the subject procurement was
charged out of the 20 percent Development Fund, otherwise a Notice of
Disallowance will be issued accordingly; and
b. Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate submission to the COA Audit Team
for evaluation the approved program design containing the rationale, objective and
target beneficiaries, list of recipients with names, addresses and signatures, and
the description and quantity of the items received and pictures during distribution.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GFPS to maximize the
utilization of the GAD funds through the full implementation of GAD-related programs
and projects in order to fully attain the objectives of the appropriated funds.

We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GFPS TWG and other
concerned officials to continue their efforts to fully create and establish the GAD
Monitoring and Evaluation Team and System of the Municipality as required under
Sections 3.4, 5.1 and 5.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circulars
Nos. 2013-01 and 2016-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. enjoin the Municipal Budget Officer, the Municipal Accountant and other
concerned officials to provide valid explanation or justification on the
circumstances behind the expenditures beyond the appropriations amounting to
P4,090,584.58 for evaluation of the Audit Team, otherwise a Notice of
