Disallowance will be issued, accordingly;
b. direct the Municipal Budget Officer to strictly ensure that the actual expenditures
do not exceed the budget for each class/fund and that they are utilized solely for
the purpose for which they were created/intended; and
c. require the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to closely
monitor the balances of appropriations and to advise the concerned officials and
employees to stop the incurrence of obligations when appropriations are no
longer available for the purpose.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant,
Municipal Health Officer, and other responsible officials to:
a. cause the immediate liquidation of the fund to the CHD 4A CALABARZON in
compliance with the pertinent provisions of the Memorandum of Agreement; and
b. submit to the COA Audit Team for evaluation all lacking documents relative to the
total payment of HEA to eligible HCWs and non-HCWs of P3,305,000.00 for the
period May 1, 2022 to October 31, 2022, otherwise a Notice of Suspension will be
issued accordingly.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant or the
authorized representative to submit all the succeeding cash advance vouchers and
supporting documents for Confidential Funds to the Office of the Audit Team Leader
within seven days after the release of the check for timely post-audit thereof and to
ensure strict compliance with the said Circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and other
responsible officials:
a. to explain in writing to the COA Audit Team, for further evaluation, why the
requirement of posting of performance security was not imposed of the subject
procurement; and
b. to ensure that henceforth, the posting of performance security and submission of
the same, if warranted in the procurement activity, is adhered to by the winning
bidders as required under Section 39 of the 2016 RIRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to trace
and determine the details of the Due to BIR account amounting to P822,443.39 and
cause its immediate remittance and/or make the necessary adjustments in the books
of accounts as the case may be. Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal
Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with BIR Memorandum
Circular No. 23-2007 by ensuring that the correct amount of taxes being withheld and
that these taxes are properly remitted within the prescribed period to prevent the
incurrence of surcharges and penalties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and
other concerned officials to review and verify records pertaining to outstanding
balances of Due to GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth and thereafter, remit immediately
all the premiums withheld to avoid the incurrence of penalties and surcharges.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant, Local
Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer and other concerned officials to
submit to the Commission on Audit Audit Team for evaluation all lacking documents
relative to the total disbursements of P562,950.00 charged against LDRRMF,
otherwise a Notice of Suspension will be issued, accordingly. Henceforth, the
Municipal Accountant to ensure that all payments to suppliers are supported with
complete documentation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure
that all CAs and liquidation reports are supported with complete and properly
accomplished documentation, CAs for travel expenses are given directly to the
concerned individual and not to a representative and only duly authorized and bonded
Disbursing Officer of the Municipality receive cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, at the earliest and practicable period,
initiate the one-time cleansing of PPE balances of the Municipality by following the
guidelines and procedures on the six activities prescribed under COA Circular
No. 2020-006 enumerated below, to eliminate any discrepancies or deviations
between accounting and property records and to establish an accurate and
verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability PPE balances:
1. Preliminary activities prior to the conduct of inventory taking;
2. Inventory taking;
3. Reconciliation of inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting
records;
4. Disposition procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs;
5. Procedures in the derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs without available
record of accountability; and
6. Accounting entries and disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the Municipal Accounting Office to submit immediately to the Audit
Team the 5,725 DVs and its supporting documents amounting to
P1,508,802,258.73, otherwise, the transactions shall be disallowed in audit; and
b. henceforth, strictly adhere with the provisions of PD No. 1445, COA Circular No.
2009-006 and the Manual regarding the timely submission of financial
transaction documents and reports to COA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:
a. instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit Team the
corresponding JEVs, approved DV, perfected contract and AIR pertaining to the
recorded Accounts Payable amounting to P148,776,199.27, otherwise, make the
necessary adjusting entries to revert or derecognize the Accounts Payable to
ensure that subject account and other affected accounts are fairly presented in
the financial statements; and
b. refrain from recognizing Accounts Payable unless the required documents to
establish the validity of the claims are complete and henceforth, strictly comply
with the submission of JEV and supporting documentation to the Audit Team
pursuant to Section 31, Chapter 2 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II and COA
Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. submit immediately the following to the COA Audit Team:
i. the JEVs and supporting documents pertaining to the deposits in transit
amounting to P28,166,169.84;
ii. the DVs and supporting documents representing the 174 outstanding
checks totaling P20,919,012.25;
b. prepare the necessary accounting entries to record the bank account amounting
to P10,000.00 and the online collections totaling P3,058,522.98, and submit the
same to the Audit Team for verification and audit; and
c. henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of PD No. 1445, COA Circular Nos.
96-011 and 2009-006, and the Manual regarding the preparation of BRS and the
timely submission of financial transaction documents and reports to COA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to stop the practice of processing
payment of claims under a contract awarded not strictly in accordance with the
procedures prescribed under RA No. 9184 and its 2016 Revised IRR.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. subject all deliveries of all items for acceptance first by the MGSO before
inspection to ensure proper and accurate recording, inventory and monitoring;
and
b. duly notify the COA Audit Team of the time and scheduled delivery date of all
purchases of the Municipality within 24 hours from such acceptance and submit
the corresponding report of inspection thereafter; and henceforth, strictly
comply with the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 92-386 and 95-006 and the
Manual relative to the acceptance and inspection of the purchases of the
Municipality and the notification of delivery and submission of the report of
inspection to COA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. in coordination with the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), submit a written
explanation to this Audit Team, together with supporting documents, regarding
the observations noted especially on the procurement activities undertaken but
not included in the APP aggregating P251,272,722.34, otherwise, payments
relative to the said activities shall be disallowed in audit;
b. ensure that the APP of the Municipality for CY 2024 and succeeding years are in
accordance with the requirements on the preparation of the APP under GPPB
Circular No. 07-2015 and submit the same to the COA Audit Team as
accompanying document to the Annual Budget;
c. instruct the BAC to report all bid results, regardless of modality, in the Report
of Bid Results submitted and uploaded in the FDPP and henceforth, strictly
adhere to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and related
issuances regarding procurement and the preparation of relevant plans and
documents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction Management Council to:
a. ensure that the LDRRMFIP in the succeeding years includes under a separate
caption the PPAs to be charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years,
otherwise any disbursement out of the STF will be suspended or disallowed in
audit; and
b. ensure 100 percent accomplishment of LDRRMF PPAs considering funds are
available thereto, in order to enhance the preparedness and response
capabilities of the Municipal and its constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to:
a. in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer, submit a copy of the portion
of the supplemental budget covering the excess charges, if any; and
b. ensure 100 percent utilization of the GAD Fund considering funds are available
thereto.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. through the HRMO, expedite the hiring and/or promotion of qualified personnel
to reduce, if not totally eliminate, the need to appoint casual workers, thus
ensuring a more efficient and effective government service thru strategic
management of human resources; and
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant and the HRMO to ensure that the figures
reported in the QRHRC submitted and uploaded in the FDPP reconcile with the
figures appearing in the financial statements and related reports.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned personnel to
consider availing the One-Time Cleansing of PPE in accordance with Commission on
Audit Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to address the above-cited
deficiencies in the PPE accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, the
Municipal GSO and other concerned officials, to identify all the land owned by
the Municipality and ensure that all properties owned by the Municipality are
accounted in the books;

b. Municipal Accountant and/or the Municipal GSO to submit to the Audit Team
copies of proof of ownership of one parcel of lot; and

c. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal GSO, to investigate
and/or locate the supporting documents of the portions of lot apparently sold to
the Metrobank, and submit these documents to the Audit Team for proper
derecognition in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all concerned officials/personnel
with issued vehicles from the Municipality of Tanza, in coordination with the General
Service Office (GSO), to:

a. Present their vehicles to the Commission on Audit (COA) Audit Team for
inspection; otherwise, unpresented vehicles will be deemed missing or non-
existing, which may warrant further investigation from the Office of the
Municipal Mayor and the COA Audit Team and the taking of appropriate legal
action against the accountable employees;

b. Attach the plate numbers and/or conduction plate/stickers of all motor vehicles
of the Municipality and mark all government vehicles with the name of the
Municipality for proper identification;

c. Cause the registration of the 49 motor vehicles and the renewal of registration
of the 52 other motor vehicles with the Land Transportation Office in compliance
with Section 5 of Batas Pambansa Bilang 74; and

d. Ensure that all vehicles of the Municipality are insured with the GSIS in
compliance with RA No. 656.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Draw the necessary adjusting entry to record any unused/undistributed cane
sticks and walkers to date in its appropriate inventory account; and

b. Adopt the Perpetual Inventory Method in the recording of supplies and
inventories and to recognize expense only upon issuances thereof to end-users
supported with the submission of necessary supporting documents such as
Summary of Supplies and Materials Inventory (SSMI) from the Municipal General
Services Office (GSO).

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. MSDWO and other concerned Offices to take proactive measures to collect the
outstanding receivables such as sending demand letters or formal notice to
debtors that payments are overdue and legal action may be pursued, if
necessary, otherwise, analyze the effectiveness of the SEA program if non-
payment has become a pattern among these beneficiaries;

b. Municipal Accountant to provide allowance for impairment on expected losses
from uncollectible or long overdue receivables pursuant to Paragraph 67 of
IPSAS 29;

c. Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to communicate with
Postema Gasoline Station and Aqua Fojas Water Refilling Station on the
overpayments made and immediately settle these accounts; and

d. Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entry to correct the error
that resulted to the negative balance amounting to P14,740.65 in the Other
Receivables account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. submit to the Audit Team all necessary documents such as Delivery Receipts,
Sales Invoice, Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) and other pertinent
documents to prove the validity of the recorded Accounts Payable totaling
P1,100,132.29 in CY 2022, otherwise revert all unsupported Accounts Payable to
the General or Funds of the Municipality to fairly present the balances of the
affected accounts in the financial statements; and

b. cause the immediate reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the General
Fund of the long outstanding payables totaling P2,956,745.20 and P17,500.00 for
the GF and SEF, respectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with concerned offices, to send letters to
creditors and/or employees and/or inform them of their collectibles from the
Municipality and if no response is received on the deadline given, immediately
revert these amounts to the unappropriated surplus in accordance with COA
Circular No. 76-45;

b. Municipal Treasurer to obtain explanation from the Accountable Officer (AO)
with overages in collection, and if the overages cannot be satisfactorily
explained by the AO, forfeit the amount in favor of the Municipality by issuing an
Official Receipt and record the overage as “Miscellaneous Income”; and

c. Municipal Treasurer to demand from the AO the immediate settlement of their
unremitted collections, otherwise deduct the amount from their salaries, and
thereafter make sure that overages and/or shortages are settled immediately
once discovered.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. strictly comply with RA Nos. 8291, 7742 and 7875 as amended by RA 9241 by
ensuring that the amounts of all loan payments and/or contributions withheld are
remitted in full to respective government corporations; and

b. investigate the cause of the negative balances of the Due to GSIS and PhilHealth
accounts to facilitate reconciliation and adjustments in the GSIS and PhilHealth
records of the Municipality and individual employees and make necessary
adjusting entries to correct the SLs with negative balances; and

c. establish an effective control system in the recording and monitoring of the Due
to GSIS, Due to Pag-IBIG and Due to PhilHealth accounts to ensure that entries
made thereon are accurate, complete and reconciled with the source documents
and to facilitate timely detection of errors.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal GSO to update all the PAR for all motor vehicles and transfer them to
the personnel who are actually using the service vehicles to establish proper
accountability;

b. Municipal GSO and the concerned officials to observe the allowed number of
vehicles provided in Item 4.2 of DBM Budget Circular 2022-1, unless assignment
of more than one vehicle is justified as necessary in the performance of their
official functions; and

c. Municipal GSO and the concerned Officials to limit the assignment of motor
vehicles to the LGU officials enumerated under Item 4.4 of DBM Budget Circular
2022-1, and consider the provisions of Item 4.5 thereof on the common use of
motor vehicles by other officials and employees in the exercise of their core
function; and

d. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal GSO, to compute the
amount that must be deducted from the Transportation Allowances (TA) of
personnel based on the actual days they use government vehicles.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:

a. Formulate written policies/guidelines and limitations on the granting of mobile
phone and mobile plans to public officials and employees taking into
consideration the necessity of its usage and the reasonableness of the amount
as approved by the local sanggunian;

b. Limit the grant of either the mobile phones or mobile plans to avoid excessive
expenditures and submit written justifications on the availment of mobiles
phones in addition to the mobile plan subscription charged to the Municipality;

c. Analyze the need to provide mobile phones to Officials already granted with
mobile plan subscriptions and ensure that only qualified personnel holding the
position not lower than the Division Chief or Department Heads were provided
with mobile phone/plan, unless otherwise justified as necessary in their official
duty and/or functions; and

d. Require the Municipal General Service Officer (GSO) to record as Municipal
property, the mobile phones included in the plan subscriptions of Officials, with
gadget cash out charged against the Municipality, through Property
Acknowledgement Receipts (PAR) or Inventory Custodian Slips (ICS),
whichever is applicable, since these Officials already have issued mobile
phones in addition to their mobile plans.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. BAC to ensure that the procedural requirements in the hiring of consultants, as
provided under the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184 are strictly complied with;

b. Human Resource and Management Officer and other concerned officials to
analyze and identify the consultants whose services or functions could be
performed by existing plantilla personnel and ensure that in the hiring of
consultants for the completion of a specific project/program/activity, the
services to be rendered are highly technical or special in nature that require
skilled experts in their respective fields to avoid unnecessary payments of
Consultancy Fees; and

c. Municipal Accountant to ensure that complete and proper documentations are
attached to the disbursement vouchers before processing them for payment.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Planning and
Development Coordinator (MPDC) and other concerned officials to ensure that all
issues that may cause delay in the implementation of 20 percent development
projects are reasonably identified and addressed prior to their inclusion in the AIP and
procurement so that the intended benefits from the projects may be timely enjoyed by
the intended users.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management
Officer (MDRRMO) and all other concerned personnel to ensure all changes in
the Annual Budget and AIP be implemented in accordance with Article 417 and
454(b) of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 7160, otherwise,
affected transactions may be disallowed in audit; and

b. Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office to ensure the
implementation of projects, programs, and activities allocated in the LDRRMFIP
to enhance the disaster capabilities of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. BAC Secretariat to prepare the indicative APP for the ensuing budget year using
the APP form approved by the GPPB, which will be submitted to the BAC for the
final recommendation of the appropriate procurement modality, and finally to the
HoPE for his approval; and

b. BAC to submit their APP and PMR to the GPPB as required in the said Circular
and furnish the Office of the Auditor of a copy of each report with proof of
submission to the GPPB.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC Secretariat and the
Municipal GSO to furnish the COA Auditors with copies of all contracts and POs with
supporting documents within five days from their execution pursuant to COA Circular
No. 2009-001, and ensure that POs are properly accomplished containing all the basic
data or information required under COA Circular No. 96-010.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the officer/s responsible for
accepting deliveries to notify the Auditors of incoming deliveries of goods within 24
hours from the scheduled acceptance so that inspection could be timely conducted by
the Auditors, if warranted, and regularity of the deliveries could be effectively
established.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Inventory Committee to conduct annual physical count of all properties of the
Municipality and prepare and submit the RPCPPE to the Audit Team;

b. GSO and the Municipal Accountant to maintain property cards and based on
the RPCPPE and PPELC for each group of PPEs, respectively, to facilitate
the reconciliation and verification of the year-end balances; and

c. GSO and the Municipal Accountant to reconcile their respective property
records to come up with the reconciled balances of PPE. Should
reconciliation of the RPCPPE and accounting records appeared to be
improbable, consider availing the one-time cleansing of PPE in accordance
with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to
prepare and submit an action plan that will outline and discuss in details the legal
measures and/or strategies the Municipal Treasurer’s Office will adopt or
undertake to improve the collection of delinquent RPT and SET of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer and the
Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Chairperson to furnish the COA with copies of
the approved contracts and all the documents forming part thereof within five
days from their execution as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated
February 12, 2009 to facilitate prompt review and evaluation of the contracts and
early communication to Management of the deficiencies, if any.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to
prepare and submit the necessary requirements to enable the Municipality to
include in its automated payroll system the payment of the wages of its Job Order
workers.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. Municipal Accountant and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC)
Secretariat to immediately submit the lacking documentary requirements for
the procurement of heavy equipment subject to verification of the Audit Team,
to avoid audit suspension and/or audit disallowance; and

b. Municipal Accountant to ensure that the provision of warranty security for the
procurement of goods is imposed by retaining an amount equivalent to at least
one percent of the amount paid to the supplier or requiring the latter to submit a
special bank guarantee equivalent to at least one percent of the total contract
price, as required under Section 62.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and the
Municipal Accountant to ensure the timely release of the barangays’ shares from RPT
collections in compliance with Section 271 (d) of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to
increase the amount of fidelity bond of the subject DO to cover the large amount
of her CAs pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 to protect the interest of the
Municipal Government, or otherwise, limit the amount of their cash advances up
to the maximum cash accountability of their fidelity bond.

a. We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
require the concerned officers/employees to immediately liquidate their
outstanding cash advances, especially at year-end and to implement internal
control procedures to prevent the incurrence of the same in the succeeding
years.

b. We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
and Vice Mayor stop the practice of advancing cash except for Confidential Fund
and official travels. Further, direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from
granting CAs to any elected officials except for their official travels.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, during the preparation of the APP,
instruct the BAC to consolidate similar CSE and procure the items in bulk thru
public bidding except when shopping or other alternative mode of procurement
is necessary and warranted under the circumstances set forth under the 2016
Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal DRRM Officer to
ensure that distribution of various supplies and relief goods are supported by
original copy of the list of recipients with their names, addresses, contact
numbers, and signatures, and the description and quantity of the items they had
received to facilitate validation by the COA Audit Team and other stakeholders,
otherwise relevant disbursements may be suspended or disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. require the contractors of the seven completed infrastructure projects to
submit valid financial instruments equivalent to the retention money totaling
P452,274.84 which should have a validity period of one year from the date of
issuance of the Certificates of Completion; and

b. strictly impose the deduction of the 10 percent retention money to the
payments to contractors or require the submission of valid financial
instrument equivalent to the retention money in case the contractor has
requested for its substitution which will be accepted only if the project is on
schedule and satisfactorily undertaken, and to release the retention money to
contractors upon final acceptance of the project, in compliance with Section 6
of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and
Municipal Accountant to coordinate with each other to facilitate the immediate
return to the source agencies or National Treasury of the unexpended fund
transfers totaling P152,012.53.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Planning and
Development Officer and Municipal Engineer to prepare a catch-up plan to fast-
track the completion of all delayed projects and henceforth ensure the optimal
and timely utilization of 20 percent DF in accordance with the approved time-
frame in the AIP.

a. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management (DRRM) Officer and Municipal Accountant to
submit to the Audit Team the Report on Sources and Utilization of the
LDRRMF for the months of January to September of CY 2023. Henceforth,
ensure that the monthly report is submitted on or before the 15th day after the
end of each month to facilitate timely review and verification of the
Municipality’s LDRRMF transactions, thus corrective measures can be
recommended, if necessary.

b. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local DRRM Council to
decide on the use of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF, and review and
update annually the LDRRMF Investment Plan to incorporate the projects,
programs and activities to be funded from the unexpended Quick Response Fund
(QRF) and LDRRMF-Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE) of
the previous years to enable the municipality to further enhance its ability to
address disaster risks, disaster preparedness and response capabilities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipality GAD Focal
Point System and the Technical Working Group to undergo training on HGDG to
build their competence in the conduct of gender analysis of programs and
projects for the purpose of GAD budget attribution.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
remit all taxes withheld within the prescribed period to avoid any liability
equivalent to the surcharges that may be imposed by the BIR against the latter
and other responsible officials in their personal capacity.

We recommended that the City Mayor create an Inventory Committee which shall be reconstituted and responsible for the one-time cleansing of all PPEs owned by the City in order to resolve the recurring issues on the discrepancy between the property records and accounting records and to come up with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability. We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a. Review its PPE records and ascertain the source of discrepancy between the general ledger balances of PPE accounts and the corresponding schedule of PPEs; b. xxx c. Draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the unrecognized depreciation on depreciable PPEs totaling P407,974,555.29 by debiting the Government Equity account and crediting the corresponding Accumulated Depreciation accounts; d. Draw JEV to remove the semi-expendable properties from the PPE accounts by debiting the Government Equity and Accumulated Depreciation accounts of the respective PPE, and crediting the respective PPE accounts.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor has created and reconstituted the Inventory Committee for the one-time cleansing of PPEs through Executive Order No. 23 dated September 8, 2023. The City Accounting Office has made the necessary adjusting entry and the necessary adjusting entry. The City Accounting Office has complied in recording the appropriate liquidations of cash advances based on the date as per the certified Report of Disbursements. On the other hand, the City Accounting Office agreed to provide the depreciation expenses for the Infrastructure Assets accounts with verifiable details, taking into consideration the range of useful life provided in the deferred Government Accounting Manual for Local Government Units.
observations.reason: Lack of available details and property records pertaining to PPEs acquired in CY 2017 and prior years therefrom.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a. Recognize as liability the completed portion only of infrastructure projects as of year-end in order that the Accounts Payable and the corresponding asset or expense accounts will not be overstated; and b. Cause the immediate reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the outstanding payables totaling P7,010,913.18 with no available documents to substantiate the claims in compliance with Section 98 of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant has made the necessary adjustments in the books thru the following JEV Nos: • 100-2023-07-28101 dated July 31, 2023; and • 100-2023-12-30250 to 30254, all dated December 31, 2023. The City Accounting Office has reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the long outstanding payables thru JEV Nos. 100-2023-03-26646 dated March 27, 2023 and 100-2023-07-28101 dated July 31, 2023 amounting to P4,141,530.17 and P2,869,383.01, respectively.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a. Require every City official who will receive cash advance to strictly observe the prescribed due date for the liquidation of his/her cash advance and to ensure that all cash advances except for petty cash are properly liquidated at year-end, in compliance with the pertinent sections of COA Circular No. 97-002 and LTOM 2nd Edition, 2019, Book III, to safeguard cash from the risk of misuse or loss; and b. Record the payments from the cash advances on the actual date that they are made based on the Certified Report of Disbursement and to record the appropriate adjusting entry at month-end and year-end for the accrued expenses to ensure the fair presentation of the Advances account and Payable accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Throughout the year, City Accounting Office staffs have verbally informed several City employees and compliant monitoring was done. The City has complied in recording the appropriate liquidations of cash advances based on the date as per the certified Report of Disbursements.
observations.reason: Notwithstanding the City Accounting Office's constant reminder, several employees were still not able to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period due to the lack of complete supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a. Require the City Treasurer and City Accountant to ensure that: • Designations of all disbursing officers are with prior written approval or authority of the City Mayor and that only one disbursing officer is designated for each specific purpose cash advance unless additional disbursing officers are fully justified by the City Mayor, pursuant to Section 108(A)(6) of the LTOM 2nd Edition, 2019, Book III; • All the designated disbursing officers are properly bonded and that the grant of cash advances is within their explicit authority and does not exceed the maximum cash accountabilities covered by fidelity bond; and b. Direct the City Treasurer to require all the designated disbursing officers to maintain cashbook and in coordination with the City Accountant, conduct periodic reconciliation of the accountable officer's book balance with the accounting records.
observations.managementAction: The City has complied the requirement that all designated disbursing officers are with prior written approval or authority from the City Mayor. The Acting City Treasurer and City Accountant complied in monitoring that all designated accountable officers maintain their respective cashbooks. The City Treasurer has made sure that the bonding of all accountable officers is being monitored. The City Treasurer will update the fidelity bond of the designated disbursing officers and will require the latter to maintain the required cashbook.
observations.reason: Not all designated disbursing officers were bonded as Disbursing Officer; some were bonded as Collecting Officer or Property Custodian. Also, some designated disbursing officers were not yet maintaining cashbooks at the time of audit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to: a. Require the concerned City Officials to submit immediately to the COA Audit Team the pertinent documents that would support the validity of the reported expenses totaling P2,662,712.50, otherwise the subject payments will be suspended in audit; and b. Ensure that every City Official who will receive cash advance is well-oriented on the documentary requirements needed in the liquidation of his/her cash advance, as prescribed in the pertinent sections of COA Circular No. 2012-001, and ensure their completeness and validity prior to liquidation to avoid audit suspension or disallowance.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office committed compliance with the audit recommendations. The City Officials submitted documents where available, and the City is enhancing orientation to accountable officers on documentary requirements.
observations.reason: Not all supporting documents were immediately available at the time of audit; some documents were subsequently submitted by concerned officers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Engineer and concerned contractors to immediately provide the Audit Team with the copies of the pertinent Detailed Breakdown of the ABC and Detailed Breakdown of the Contract Cost, respectively, to facilitate the completion of the conduct of the auditorial review, and the submission of the contracts to the COA Region IV-A Technical Audit Division (TAD) for technical evaluation for the purpose of establishing the validity, correctness and reasonableness of the costs of the projects, otherwise, the transactions will be suspended in audit.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineering Office submitted the copies of the Detailed Breakdown of the ABC and Detailed Breakdown of the Contract Cost.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to: a. Immediately prepare and issue to concerned contractors Resumption Orders to the five stalled projects totaling P76,688,326.00; and b. Formulate and implement an action plan to fast-track the completion of the six projects totaling P84,681,699.79 and thereafter ensure strict compliance with the guidelines on the issuance of Suspension Order and date for the commencement of works provided under the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, Local Government Units Procurement Manual and 2016 PBDs to avoid unnecessary delay in the implementation of infrastructure projects.
observations.managementAction: The City has issued Resumption Orders to projects with a total contract cost of P56,501,098.54. Three projects with a total contract cost of P56,501,098.54 were issued the Resumption Orders. The project with a contract cost of P12,191,495.27 was not issued the Resumption Order as requested by the Office of Registry of Deeds since the office will be demolished as it was situated within the project location. Regarding the last suspended project, its contract will be terminated due to the non-feasibility of its implementation. The City will closely monitor and implement an action plan to fast-track the other projects.
observations.reason: If found out that the cause of delay is made by the contractor, they are being given a notice of work resumption once the suspension is lifted. If the cause of delay is made by the City, they are being given a schedule to resume of work once the suspension is lifted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a. Local Finance Committee to include the estimated receipts from the operations of market and the slaughterhouse in the computation of the appropriation for the LDRRMF as they are considered regular sources of income in compliance with Section 21 of RA No. 10121; and b. City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer and City Accountant to submit a written justification supported with adequate documents, for further evaluation by the Audit Team, on why charges totaling P540,800.00 were made against the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF for CYs 2018 and 2020 for projects which were not included in the City’s LDRRMFIP, otherwise the subject disbursements will be suspended or disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: The Management has submitted to the Audit Team the appropriate LDRRMFIP for CY 2023. The City gave assurance that they will abide with the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the legal opinion and/or assistance of the City Legal Officer in the review of the proposed tax ordinances with pertinent provisions of RA No. 7160 in order to prevent misinterpretations and misapplications of laws which could result in revenue loss to the City.
observations.managementAction: The Management sought the legal assistance of the City Legal Officer and agreed with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the OIC – City Assessor to prepare the updated Schedule of Fair Market Values for enactment into ordinance by the Sangguniang Panlungsod and undertake the general revision of real property assessments and property classification in compliance with the pertinent provisions of the IRR of RA No. 7160 and the Manual on Real Property Appraisal and Assessment Operations.
observations.managementAction: Submitted the proposed updated Schedule of Fair Market Values (SFMV) for the year 2025 in the Office of the City Assessor and recommendation was forwarded to BLGF - Region IV-A for review and approval. The City is still waiting for the approval and/or recommendation of BLGF - Region IV-A.
observations.reason: Waiting for the BLGF Region IV-A review and approval of the submitted SFMV.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require all the JO workers of the City to register in the biometric attendance monitoring system for the generation of the Daily Time Records and strictly implement the preparation of detailed monthly Accomplishment Report, in compliance with the pertinent section of COA Circular No. 2012-001.
observations.managementAction: The Administrator’s Office has submitted the Accomplishment Reports of the Job Order workers. Most of the Job Order workers were registered in the biometric attendance monitoring system, except those assigned in the field.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer and other concerned officials to prepare and submit the necessary requirements to enable the City to include in its automated payroll system the payment of the wages of its Job Order workers.
observations.managementAction: The City has started payment of the wages of the Job Order workers thru Automated Teller Machine (ATM) starting on June 2023.

Management’s Comment:
Due to time constraints, the City was not able to complete the one-time cleansing of PPE as of December 31, 2023. However, the assigned Committee continued working on the reconciliation of the inventory count per RPCPPE with the property and accounting records.

The City Mayor instructed the concerned departments to complete the one-time cleansing of PPE balances following the procedural guidelines on the six activities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to conduct preliminary activities such as the updating and comparison of their respective records of PPE using the available latest Report of Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) and other property records prior to the preparation of Physical Inventory Plan (PIP). After which, it is recommended that the Inventory Committee and other concerned officials adhere to the guidelines provided under COA Circular No. 2020-006, specifically to: (i) prepare the IIRUP and submit the same to the Disposal/Appraisal Committee for appraisal, particularly of properties the cost of which could not anymore be established despite diligent efforts, and for the latter to proceed with the disposal of said properties pursuant to the guidelines provided under the afore-cited issuances.
observations.managementAction:
Management’s Comment:
The City Mayor instructed the CGSO to adhere with the recommendations of COA so that the Accounting Office can prepare the required ledgers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and the City Accountant to immediately remit the unexpended balance of the transferred funds totaling P568,963.51 to their respective source agencies, in accordance with COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.
observations.managementAction:
Management commented the following:
• The P150,584.00 credited to the bank account last December 20, 2022 pertains to amount transferred from The Clubhouse Network, Inc. This was the assistance given to the Computer Center.
• The amount of P100,000.00 financial assistance from the League of Cities will be used for a project to be completed in the year 2024.
• The unexpended balance of P33,000.00 for the DSWD’s Social Pension Program fund was already remitted to the Bureau of Treasury last February 28, 2024.
• The amount of P465,000.00 received from DILG and CENRO as Awards from Manila Bay Clean Up Drive and Environmental Management Bureau (EMB) has a project proposal already entitled “Capacity Building and Study Tours of General Trias Save Manila Bay Task Force and Inspection, Inventory, Monitoring and Validation Team (IIMT).” This will be implemented before the end of the semester of this year.

The City Mayor instructed all concerned departments to implement the intended projects for the amount received from the source agencies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineering Office to conduct preliminary inspection and submit punch-lists to contractors on infrastructure projects when they reach an accomplishment of 95 percent of the total contract amount to determine defects and thus, make necessary corrections, before proper turnover and acceptance of the projects to the City.
observations.managementAction:
Management’s Comment:
The City Engineer was able to conduct preliminary inspection at 90 to 95 percent completion of the infrastructure projects and prepare and submit punch-lists, if any, for any defects found therein, prior to the City’s turnover and acceptance of the projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Planning and Development Coordinator, Officer-In-Charge - City Engineer and other concerned officials to conduct comprehensive variation orders feasibility and preliminary engineering studies prior to implementation of projects, including those pertaining to the timing/phasing of related project activities, such as engineering design and acquisition of right-of-way or location as well as the conformance to land use and zoning guidelines prescribed by existing laws, among others, to ensure that the projects are implemented and completed on time.
observations.managementAction:
Management’s Comment:
The non-implementation of the four projects to be funded by the 20 percent DF as of December 31, 2023 was due to issues on adjacent projects, accessibility, alteration and ownership of locations as explained by the concerned departments. However, the City believes that addressing these issues requires effective governance, transparency and accountability to ensure the proper utilization of resources for development initiatives.

Nonetheless, the City Mayor directed the concerned departments to immediately address the issues and proceed to implementation of the projects for the benefit of the constituents in a timely manner. In addition, they were instructed to review carefully all development projects to be funded by 20 percent DF and to study the same before its inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer and other concerned officials to prepare and submit the necessary requirements to enable the City to include in its automated payroll system the payment of the wages of its Job Order workers.
observations.managementAction:
Management’s Comment:
The City Treasurer and other concerned officials have submitted the necessary requirements to the banks so that the City will be able to include in its automated payroll system the payment of the wages of its Job Order workers. As of this writing, the City's submitted requirements with the Land Bank of the Philippines and the Development Bank of the Philippines, both of which cater to the City's current accounts, are being processed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and the OIC - City Public Market Administration Division to: execute formal COLs with the qualified market stallholders, making sure that they are prepared in accordance with the provisions of the 2015 Market Code of General Trias; update and complete the names reported in the list submitted by the City Treasurer’s Office, and reconcile them with those of the City Public Market Administration Office, as well as with the registered business owners per BPLO reports; require all market stallholders to obtain business or Mayor’s permits from the BPLO, and inform them that non-compliance may result in imposition of penalties and closure of their operations under relevant City ordinances; send demand letters to stallholders with unsettled accounts; and ensure the strict enforcement of the 2015 Market Code of General Trias provisions.
observations.managementAction:
Management’s Comment:
• The COLs of stallholders is being reviewed by the City Legal Office, and amendments to the contract are being proposed which will be mutually-beneficial to the City Government and stallholders.
• The BPLO, City Treasurer’s Office, Market Supervisor’s Office, with the assistance of the Internal Audit Office will again conduct inspections of stalls in order to validate records.
• A request to the Sangguniang Panlungsod have been made to amend the 2015 Market Code to align it to the proposed changes in the lease contract. A legislative measure to waive the penalties and interest of delinquent market stall holders, as well as reasonable payment terms are also being proposed.
• The City is looking for private investors from the retail industry for possible privatization of market operations.

Due to the complexity of the situation, careful planning for a long-term solution is needed in order to transform the public market into an income generating asset.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the GFPS to maximize the utilization of the GAD funds through the full implementation of GAD-related programs and projects in order to fully attain the objectives of the appropriated funds. We likewise recommended that the City Mayor direct the Chairperson of the Technical Working Group-GFPS to conduct gender analysis through the administration of HGDG tool from the planning and budgeting phase to the implementation, monitoring and evaluation phase, apply the results thereof in the preparation of the GAD Plan and Budget and the accompanying GAD Accomplishment Report, and the identification of strategies to address gaps or missing elements, if any.
observations.managementAction:
Management’s Comment:
The City acknowledges and appreciate the importance of integrating gender analysis from the planning and budgeting stages to implementation, monitoring and evaluation. It is committed to ensuring that these insights are effectively incorporated into its GPB and GAD AR.

For fiscal year (FY) 2024, all responsible offices have already utilized the HGDG tool during their planning and budgeting processes, and have adhered to the prescribed guidelines throughout the implementation phase. They will continue to apply the HGDG tool during their monitoring and evaluation phase to ensure comprehensive compliance and effective gender mainstreaming.

They will compile all relevant documentation and it will be included in their 2024 GAD AR, which will be submitted to the Audit Team at the end of the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to compute and journalize the depreciation expenses of the PPE accounts totaling P986,068,575.49, to ensure that the relevant accounts are presented fairly in the financial statements of the City, in compliance with Section 262 of GAM for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the City Accountant already computed the depreciation expenses and made the necessary adjusting entries per Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) No. 221-2023-12-1027 dated December 31, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to compute and make the necessary journal entries to adjust the Government Equity and Accumulated Depreciation - Motor Vehicle accounts, and henceforth, ensure that all Property, Plant and Equipment purchased by the City, and the corresponding depreciation, if applicable, are recognized in the manner provided under Sections 4 and 120 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant already computed the depreciation expenses and made the necessary adjusting entries per JEV No. 100-23-02-894 dated February 20, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to cause the immediate reversion of all long outstanding payables aged over two years to the unappropriated surplus in the General Fund.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the City Accountant already made the necessary reversion of all the long outstanding payables to the unappropriated surplus in the General Fund per JEV No. 200-23-02-0910 dated February 20, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor, being the Chairman of the Board of Trustees (BoT) of ONI, require the: a) Head of the Accounting/Finance Section of ONI to immediately reconstruct and submit to the BoT the accounting reports and financial statements of the ONI during the last two or three years to enable evaluation of its financial performance and condition over the years, and as basis for introduction of new or modification of existing policies and strategies to eventually capacitate the ONI to be self-reliant or less dependent from the City for financing its operations; b) Head of the Accounting/Finance Section of ONI to immediately start setting up the books of accounts of the ONI, and thereafter, prepare and submit regularly to the City Accounting Office and the Commission on Audit the hospital’s complete quarterly and annual financial statements for CY 2023; and c) Chief of Hospital of the ONI to prepare and submit to the Sanggunian Panlungsod for approval, the Annual Operating Budget for ONI for CY 2024, using historical data and estimates of the hospital’s own revenues and expenses as basis.
observations.managementAction: The Head of Accounting Section of ONI had submitted the quarterly and annual financial statements for CY 2023. The Head of Accounting/Finance Section of ONI has prepared the quarterly and annual accounting reports and financial statements of the ONI for the last two or three years. The Chief of Hospital has prepared the Annual Operating Budget for ONI for CY 2024 but has yet to submit it to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for approval.
observations.reason: The Chief of Hospital has yet to submit the prepared Annual Operating Budget for ONI for CY 2024 to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for approval.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a) Direct the City Accountant to record the appropriate Receivable account to recognize the overpayment made to the NFA amounting to P275,000.00 with corresponding credit to Government Equity; b) Enjoin the CDRRMO and City Treasurer to write a letter to the NFA to explain the circumstances behind the overpayment of P275,000.00 and request for the refund of the amount; and c) Demand from the responsible City official to pay the P275,000.00 to the City should recovery from the NFA be unsuccessful, otherwise, the amount will be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant already recorded the appropriate Receivable account to recognize the overpayment made to the NFA per JEV No. 100-23-02-893 dated February 20, 2023. The CDRRMO and City Treasurer already made a series of communications with NFA through e-mail and social media accounts on February 8, 2023 as justification submitted by City Treasurer on February 27, 2023. The NFA already refunded the amount of P275,000.00 to the City per Official Receipt No. 2264730 dated April 12, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Engineer to submit immediately an explanation or justification on the excessive amounts of several work items in the contract cost of the infrastructure project, otherwise, the amount of P26,578,692.38 will be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: Management has submitted justification on the excessive amounts of several work items in the infrastructure project. Evaluation of the Audit Team found the justification satisfactory.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a) Through the City Legal Officer, demand from the supplier of the e-trikes, the concerned GSO personnel and/or other former City officials who certified or signed the Acceptance and Inspection Receipt (AIR) attesting to the completeness of the delivery of the 77 e-trikes to the City of Imus, Cavite on November 10, 2022, to submit immediately to the COA Audit Team a written explanation why the amount of P40,656,000.00 should not be refunded or disallowed in audit in view of the findings of the COA Audit Team of irregular disbursements; b) Through the City Legal Officer, demand from the former GSO Head and former City Mayor to submit to the COA Audit Team a written explanation why the project implementation of e-vehicles was not implemented as planned in accordance with the 2013 City Ordinance No. 03-125, also known as the ELM Program which supported the City’s application for loans to the Development Bank of the Philippines (DBP); c) Instruct the present City GSO to conduct investigation on the withdrawal of 24 e-trikes on June 30, 2022 and removal of batteries of the remaining 53 e-trikes and impose appropriate sanctions against personnel who may be found liable for releasing the batteries without proper authority and documentation; d) File appropriate civil and/or criminal charges and initiate blacklisting proceedings against the supplier, if warranted; and e) Require the present City GSO to conduct investigation on the withdrawal of 24 e-trikes and proceed with other necessary administrative and disposal actions.
observations.managementAction: The supplier of the e-trikes already submitted a written explanation. The City Legal Office already made a demand letter dated November 24, 2022 to the former GSO Head and former City Mayor to submit explanation. The City has made evaluation; imposition of sanctions and filing of charges pending issuance of Notice of Finality of Decision of the ND.
observations.reason: Though justifications were submitted by the responsible persons and some communications/actions were taken, imposition of appropriate sanctions and final legal/administrative disposition pending Notice of Finality of Decision of the issued Notice of Disallowance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the: a) Schools Division Superintendent, BAC and the BAC-TWG to provide valid justification in writing on the excessive ABC and unit price of the noise cancelling headsets; and b) GSO personnel concerned to submit a valid explanation or justification in writing on why the subject items were accepted despite non-conformance with specifications in the Purchase Order for evaluation of the Audit Team, otherwise, the amount of P891,662.30 will be disallowed in audit, and filing of appropriate charges against accountable officials will be recommended.
observations.managementAction: The Schools Division Superintendent, BAC and the BAC-TWG submitted written justification. The Inspection Committee submitted its written justification and informed the Audit Team that it actually examined and inspected the product when it was officially accepted by the LGU and found that the headsets matched their specifications.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a) Require the City Engineer and the City Accountant to compute liquidated damages equivalent to the delays of completion of the concerned Contractors, and demand payment thereof to avoid audit disallowance; and b) Require the City Engineer and the City Accountant to strictly impose and deduct liquidated damages equivalent to the delays of the Contractors before final payment is made.
observations.managementAction: The City Engineer has submitted written justification dated March 8, 2023 which has been accepted and found satisfactory by the Audit Team. The City Engineer and City Accountant have computed the liquidated damages and demanded payment from the contractor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a) Direct the City Administrator to require all JOs to submit all requirements for Automated Teller Machine (ATM) Card application in its Authorized Government Depository Bank (AGDB) and enroll them in the automated payroll system; and b) Ensure that the payment of wages to JOs shall be through the automated payroll system to provide greater control and protection in handling cash and avoid manual cash advances.
observations.managementAction: The City Administrator issued Memorandum Order dated March 24, 2023 requiring all JOs to submit all requirements to HRMO for ATM Card application in AGDB. The Municipality has started the transition to automated payroll with JOs to be enrolled.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the LDRRMO and other concerned officials to ensure that distributions of various welfare goods are supported by original copy of the List of Recipients with their names, addresses, contact numbers, and signatures, and the description and quantity of the items they had received to facilitate validation by the COA Audit Team and other stakeholders; otherwise, relevant disbursements may be suspended or disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMO submitted the copy of List of Recipients with their names, addresses, signatures and the description and quantity of the items they had received on March 2, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the LDRRM Council to identify and incorporate under a separate caption in the LDRRMFIP the projects and activities to be charged out of the unexpended LDRRMF, and ensure that the object of expenditure of each PPA in LDRRMFIP is also presented therein.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMO identified and has incorporated under a separate caption in the LDRRMFIP the projects and activities to be charged out of the unexpended LDRRMF, and ensured that the object of expenditure of each PPA was presented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the CPDC and other concerned officials to thoroughly plan the start and target completion dates and timetable of the procurement of all development projects, and integrate them in the AIP and APP to facilitate timely completion of the projects.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-City Planning and Development Coordinator and other concerned officials have planned the start and target completion dates and timetables for the projects; achieving a utilization rate of 92.65 percent.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor enjoin the LDRRM Council to identify and incorporate under a separate caption in the LDRRMFIP the projects and activities to be charged out of the unexpended LDRRMF, and ensure that the object of expenditure of each PPA in LDRRMFIP is also presented therein. (Duplicate/related recommendation)
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMO identified and incorporated the projects and activities under a separate caption in the LDRRMFIP.

observations.recommendation: See earlier related recommendations on LDRRMFIP.
Various remedial actions recommended including submission of documents to COA, justification, and establishment of proper procurement and planning processes.
observations.managementAction: Actions taken vary per item; some implemented, some ongoing.

observations.recommendation: Submit justifications, supporting documents, and await COA evaluation; consider disallowance where necessary.
observations.managementAction: Some justifications submitted; COA issued Notices of Disallowance for certain procurements.
observations.reason: Pending COA evaluation or full compliance with recommendations.

observations.recommendation: Submit cash advance vouchers and supporting documents for confidential funds to the Office of the Audit Team Leader within seven days after release of check for timely post-audit.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality submitted outstanding documents albeit delayed and committed to comply with timelines.
observations.reason: Some documents were submitted late; delays of 87 and 69 days noted.

observations.recommendation: Provide required documentation including contractor bills, route details, cubic meters hauled, accomplishment reports, and ensure compliance with procurement and documentary requirements.
observations.managementAction: All required documents were submitted to the Audit Team; Municipal Accountant to ensure future compliance.

observations.recommendation: Terminate time deposits under SEF and make funds readily available to support programs and projects under SEF.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer submitted a report regarding terminated time deposits under SEF and terminated the deposits.

observations.recommendation: Coordinate with implementing offices (e.g., City Engineer) to facilitate full settlement and proper liquidation of funds and submit required documents to source agencies and COA.
observations.managementAction: City Engineer submitted certificate of completion and supporting documents for project completion for evaluation. Coordination ongoing.
observations.reason: Liquidation pending submission/acceptance of complete supporting documents; some documents submitted.

observations.recommendation: Compute and collect liquidated damages, enforce contract terms and take appropriate actions to avoid disallowances.
observations.managementAction: Computed liquidated damages and demanded payment; justifications submitted by contractors where applicable; Audit Team found satisfactory after evaluation.

observations.recommendation: Review, verify and remit outstanding withholdings and contributions to avoid surcharges and penalties; coordinate with national agencies and ensure timely remittances.
observations.managementAction: Remittances were made in early January and subsequent months for most balances; coordination with NGAs ongoing to resolve timing differences.
observations.reason: Timing differences with NGAs' electronic billing systems caused delays; remedial remittances were made in subsequent months.

observations.recommendation: Pursue settlement of disallowances, submit documents for appeal where applicable and coordinate with COA for resolution.
observations.managementAction: Disallowances have pending appeal; no unsettled audit suspensions and charges as of December 31, 2023.
observations.reason: Pending appeal processes and coordination with COA.

observations.recommendation: Ensure compliance with RA 9184 IRR, GPPB requirements and COA Circulars; submit required procurement documents and justifications for Audit Team evaluation; apply sanctions as appropriate.
observations.managementAction: Various procurement justifications and documents were submitted; BAC and concerned offices committed to adhere to procurement rules; some actions pending COA evaluation.
observations.reason: Some procurements lacked complete documentation or procedures; COA issued notices for certain transactions.

observations.recommendation: Reclassify items with acquisition cost below P50,000.00 as semi-expendable property in compliance with COA Circular No. 2022-004, reconcile records, and prepare adjusting entries.
observations.managementAction: Management committed to comply; Municipal GSO and Accountant to reconcile records and effect reclassification; some adjustments were made and further reconciliation ongoing.
observations.reason: Reclassification and reconciliation in progress; some semi-expendable items already adjusted, others pending reconciliation.

observations.recommendation: Ensure trust fund accounting, prepare reconciliations, transfer unspent amounts properly and submit supporting documents for audit.
observations.managementAction: Transfers and reconciliations were undertaken; some trust fund balances were adjusted and supporting documents submitted.
observations.reason: Ongoing reconciliation and submission of documents for certain trust liabilities and transfers.

observations.recommendation: Conduct full inventory, reconcile Property Cards and ledger cards, prepare lapsing schedules for depreciation, and update accounting records accordingly.
observations.managementAction: Inventory activities initiated; lapsing schedules and reconciliations being prepared; some lapsing schedules submitted to Audit Team.
observations.reason: One-time cleansing and inventory taking not fully completed; some records still being reconciled.

observations.recommendation: Implement corrective actions as recommended in the CY 2022 AAR for each specific observation, ensure documentation and compliance.
observations.managementAction: Management provided various responses and implemented several recommendations; others remain in progress.
observations.reason: Various reasons including pending documentation, ongoing reconciliations, timing differences with NGAs, and required legal processes.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the CGSO to study and consider availing of the provisions of the one-time cleansing of the PPE balances by complying with the guidelines provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006, amongst which are as follows: a. For the City Accountant and CGSO to conduct the preliminary activities such as the updating and comparison of their respective records of PPE using the available latest RPCPPE and other property records prior to the preparation of the Physical Inventory Plan (PIP); b. Prepare and submit the PIP for approval of the City Mayor and submit a copy thereof to the COA at least 10 calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory-taking activities; c. Prepare the consolidated RPCPPE classified according to type/category for reconciliation with the PPE records provided by the City Accounting Office; d. Conduct disposition and de-recognition procedures for missing or unaccounted PPE, if any; and e. Conduct other procedures as may be necessary to complete and finish the one-time cleansing of PPE.
observations.managementAction: The City General Services Office and Accounting Office have again reviewed the submitted RPCPPE and found that there were pages or sub-totals that were not included in the total amount of the said report and, likewise noted typographical error in the summary list of PPE in the General Fund. Also, they have started discussing the activities to be undertaken for the one-time cleansing procedures. The Management submitted to the COA the PIP approved by the City Mayor. The Management prepared and RPCPPE classified according to type/category. The Management prepared the consolidated RPCPPE and submitted the RPCPPE classified according to type/category. The City General Services Office and Accounting Office have again reviewed the submitted RPCPPE and found that there were pages or sub-totals that were not included in the total amount of the said report and, likewise noted typographical error in the summary list of PPE in the General Fund. Also, they have started discussing the activities to be undertaken for the one-time cleansing procedures.
observations.reason:
The Inventory Committee only completed the first three out of six steps of the One-Time Cleansing of PPE specifically, (1) the preliminary activities prior to the conduct of the inventory taking, (2) inventory taking and (3) reconciliation of inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records. Currently, the Inventory Committee is undertaking the fourth step of the one-time cleansing of PPE which is the disposition procedures for non-existing/missing PPEs. Demand letters have already been issued to the concerned offices with missing PPEs and replies have already been received. Investigations are already on-going for missing items, which were not produced upon demand, to determine the last known location/office where the missing PPE items were issued/installed, person accountable, circumstances of the loss, and the persons responsible for the loss. If the accountability over the missing PPEs could still not be pinpointed after the investigation, the city will request for authority for derecognition from COA in order to complete the fifth step of the one-time PPE cleansing. On the other hand, the sixth step, which is taking effect the derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs will be done upon the approval of request for derecognition from the respective COA office. All the necessary disclosures in the notes to the financial statements will also be included the Financial Statements for CY2024.
Reiterated with modification in Part II, Audit Observation No. 3, Page Nos. 45 to 48. For preparation of Recommendation Tracking Sheet (RTS) for the above recommendation to be considered closed having been revised with modification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the: a. Head of the Land Tax Division to prepare and submit to the City Accountant the updated certified list showing the names of taxpayers with the amount of taxes due and collectible based on the RPT Account Register/Taxpayer's Index Cards, and direct the City Accountant to use the list as basis in setting up the receivables at the beginning of the year; b. Head of the Land Tax Division to submit to the City Accountant the Summary of Real Property Accounts Delinquencies as of December 31, 2022 for the latter to make necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct balance of delinquent RPT and SET Receivables as of Year-End; and c. City Accountant to record the total amount of collections, including share of Barangay, as a reduction to the RPT Receivables.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office obtained the list from the service provider of the City’s RPT computerized system to enable the generation of the RPT Receivable due for 2023 to facilitate the setting-up of the RPT Receivable for Calendar Year (CY) 2023. The Accounting Office recorded the collections of the RPT from the Trust Fund through the generation of quarterly collections. The Accounting Office recorded the total collections including the share of barangay as a reduction to the RPT Receivables. The Accounting Office recorded the necessary adjusting entries under JEV No. 2023-01-70431 dated January 1, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to conduct an analysis of the CIP and Accounts Payable accounts and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the entries corresponding to the unaccomplished portion of the infrastructure projects. We further recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to closely work with the City Engineer for additional information/status and request documents to ascertain the status and proper recording of all government PPAs and the completion status of projects to reclassify completed projects to appropriate PPE accounts.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant coordinated with the City Engineering Office regarding the status of the projects and made necessary adjusting entries under JEV Nos. 2023-04-07228 and 2023-04-70507 both dated April 11, 2023 for the correction of entries corresponding to the unaccomplished portion of the infrastructure projects. The City Accountant coordinated with the City Engineering Office regarding the status of the projects and made necessary adjusting entries under JEV No. 2023-04-70515 dated April 11, 2023 for the reclassification of the completed projects to the appropriate PPE accounts. The City Engineer ensured that all government PPAs are reported in the Report of Government PPAs and furnished the City Accountant. This report has already been forwarded to the City Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We further recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer to facilitate and expedite the titling of the parcels of land under the name of the City to confirm its legal ownership over the properties and safeguard them from possible multiple-party claims. We also recommended the City Accountant to capitalize the appraisal fee and other related costs necessary to bring the asset to condition for its intended use pursuant to IPSAS.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office coordinated with the City Treasurer regarding the status of the parcels and made the necessary adjusting Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) No. 2023-01-70431. The City Treasurer facilitated the titling process and submitted an Abstract detailing the current status of the 21 lots and had designated additional personnel to expedite the process of registering the parcels of land in the name of the City.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct all Department Heads and their respective Supply Officers to conduct the complete physical count of all unissued, undistributed, or unconsumed supplies and materials in their custody as of year-end and correspondingly prepare the RPCI and submit the same to the CGSO for consolidation and to the City Accountant for adjustment of the inventory account in the books of account, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: The City General Services Office and Accounting Office have submitted the RPCI as of year-end and the Accounting Office agreed to the audit recommendation to adjust the inventory accounts based on the consolidated RPCI, if needed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Health Office to submit promptly all necessary documents to the City Accountant to facilitate the timely recording of the related receipts and utilization of donations in kind; and the City Accountant to record all receipts of donations in kind and its subsequent utilization in the books of accounts pursuant to pertinent provisions of the MNGAS for LGUs.
observations.managementAction: The City Health Office, Ospital ng Tagaytay and other offices concerned were already directed to have the AIR received by COA office within 24 hours upon receipt of the medicines and to attach the same, together with the respective CPR, to the Disbursement Vouchers. The AIR stamped received and the CPR are for checking of the Accounting Office upon processing of the disbursement vouchers. The City Accountant recorded all receipts and donations in kind and their subsequent utilization in the books of accounts pursuant to pertinent provisions of the MNGAS for LGUs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Treasurer and the City Accountant to increase the amount of fidelity bond of the subject AOs and subject the AOs to sufficiently cover their cash accountabilities pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 so as to protect the interest of the City Government, otherwise, limit the amount of their cash accountability to the maximum cash accountability of their fidelity bond.
observations.managementAction: The City made adjustments and increased the fidelity bond of the accountable officers to sufficiently cover their cash accountabilities. The City Treasury and Accounting Office implemented measures to limit cash accountability where necessary.
