observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor revoke the lease agreement/certificate of award of stallholders who subleased the market stalls and cause the execution of lease agreement between the City Government and the present occupants. Henceforth, enjoin the Market Administrator to enforce the provisions in the contract and keep an updated list of market stall lessees. Also, regarding subleased market stalls, they will conduct a meeting with the registered lessees to inform them of their violation and imminent revocation of their contract. Likewise, the City will review the submitted applications for new lessees to maximize income opportunities.
observations.managementAction: The Management committed to keep an updated list of stall lessees and conduct periodic inspections. The City Mayor will instruct the City Administrator and Market Administrator to enforce the provisions in the contract and keep an updated list of market stall lessees. The City Management committed to review the lease requests of prospective lessees in order to maximize income opportunities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended various corrective actions including recording, reconciliation and submission of supporting documents to correct the accounts as detailed in the CY 2022 AAR.
observations.managementAction: Management undertook the necessary reconciliations and adjustments as recommended and submitted supporting documents where available. The Accounting Office processed required Journal Entry Vouchers and coordinated with concerned offices to complete records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management review procurement processes, ensure complete documentation, and make proper accounting adjustments where necessary.
observations.managementAction: Management reviewed procurement and payment records, made necessary adjustments and strengthened monitoring to ensure completeness of documentation and compliance with procurement rules.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CDRRMC to ensure that projects and programs in the LDRRMFIP are implemented and monitored.
observations.managementAction: The CDRRMC was directed to implement projects; however some activities were affected by the COVID-19 response requirements and prohibitions on face-to-face activities. The City has continued to plan and prioritize PPAs in the LDRRMFIP and will implement them as conditions permit.
observations.reason: With the insurgence of COVID-19 and rise of cases in the City, the use of the mitigation fund was halted with the purpose of possibly using it for COVID response. Additional amount of P12,472,800.00 was utilized in 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to record inventories the goods procured for consumption upon receipt and to recognize expense only when the goods are issued to end-users or beneficiaries, in accordance with IPSAS 12, Section 114 of the MNGAS for LGUs and COA Circular No. 2015-009.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented The Municipal Accounting Office adopted the Direct Expense Method of accounting for the COVID-19 pandemic amounting to P2,545,700.00 instead of the Perpetual Inventory System.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accounting Office recorded as inventories the goods procured for receipt. Implemented in CY 2023. (Note: entry indicates the action taken and prior non-compliance addressed)

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Municipal Health Office to submit all necessary documents of receipts and utilization of donations in-kind to the Municipal Accountant to facilitate their recording; and b. Office of the Municipal Accountant to record all receipts of donations including vaccines in-kind in the books of accounts in accordance with Item 4A of Section 36, Chapter 3 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I and Paragraph 16 of IPSAS 12.
observations.managementAction: Fully Implemented There was nothing to record since there were no COVID-19 vaccines received during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineering Office to conduct preliminary inspection and submit punch-lists to contractors on accomplishment of 95 percent of the total contract amount to determine defects and thus, make necessary corrections, before proper turnover and acceptance of the projects to the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Fully Implemented The Engineering Office was able to conduct preliminary inspection at 90 to 95 per cent completion of the infrastructure projects and prepare and submit punch-lists, if any, for defects found therein, prior to the Municipality’s turnover and acceptance of the projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairperson of the Technical Working Group - GAD Focal Point System (TWG-GFPS), with the assistance of the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, to establish and maintain a GAD database containing statistics and sex-disaggregated data systematically gathered and regularly updated to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation and subjected to gender analysis, in compliance with Section 4.1.B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
observations.managementAction: Fully Implemented The GAD database was established and maintained during the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Municipal Health Officer to submit promptly all necessary documents to the Municipal Accounting Office to facilitate the timely recording of the receipts and utilization of donations in kind; b. Municipal Accounting Office to record all receipts of donations in kind and its subsequent utilization in the books of accounts pursuant to pertinent provisions of the MNGAS for LGUs.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Health Officer submitted the necessary documents to the Accounting Office; and the donated vaccines and other supplies were properly recorded in the books during the year end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. make the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to fairly present the balances of the affected accounts in the books for the year; and b. refrain from recording Accounts Payable merely on the basis of proof of obligations and/or proof of deliveries of services and/or goods and instead ensure the validity of the claim before recognizing Accounts Payable in the books of accounts of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Accounts Payable for the year were supported with documents for the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to revert the unexpended balance of CYs 2014 to 2017 LDRRMF to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund.
observations.managementAction: As of December 31, 2023, unexpended payables aged over two years were already reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to meticulously and judiciously review the compliance of their respective office with Section 79 of the GAM for LGUs, Volume I in order to prevent errors, thereafter, the Municipal Accountant to prepare the Journal Voucher (JV) for the restoration of cash equivalent of the unreleased checks and recognition of the appropriate payable/liability accounts at the end of the year and in addition, reverse the entries made in the ensuing year to fairly present the balances of the affected accounts in the SFPo.
observations.managementAction: All unreleased checks as of December 31, 2023, were reverted to their respective Payables accounts. As of December 31, 2023, the journal entries for the restoration were made and the payable/liability accounts were presented in the SFPo.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate reversion of all long outstanding payables aged over two years to the unappropriated surplus in the General Fund.
observations.managementAction: As of December 31, 2023, payables aged more than two years were already reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat Head to furnish the COA with copies of the approved contracts and all the documents forming part thereof within five days from their execution as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 to facilitate prompt review and evaluation of the contracts and early communication to Management of the deficiencies, if any.
observations.managementAction: Copies of contracts for the procurement of projects were submitted to the COA Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineering Office to conduct preliminary inspection and submit punch-lists to contractors when they reach an accomplishment of 95 percent of the total contract amount to determine defects and thus, make necessary corrections, before proper turnover and acceptance of the projects to the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineering Office conducts inspection at 95% completion as evidenced by the Inspection Report attached as supporting document to disbursement vouchers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the OIC- Municipal Treasurer and other concerned officials to prepare and submit the necessary requirements to enable the Municipality to include in its automated payroll system the payment of the wages of Job Order workers.
observations.managementAction: Wages of job order workers of the Municipality were already paid through automated payrolls.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office (MDRRMO) to closely coordinate with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC), on the PPAs to be programmed in the LDRRMFIP before the preparation of the investment plan; b. Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (MDRRMC) to see to it that the PPAs are implemented according to the approved schedule of implementation; c. reprogram the unimplemented PPAs that are not directly related to disaster management activities to new programs, projects and activities; and d. ensure that the PPAs to be included in the LDRRMFIP are in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.
observations.managementAction: As of December 31, 2023, unutilized funds were due for implementation during the year, yet were not implemented. The MDRRMO will comply with the recommendations.
observations.reason: There were unutilized funds that were due for implementation during the year, yet were not implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Municipal Engineer, BAC Chairman and other concerned officials to thoroughly plan the start and target completion dates and timetable of the procurement of all development projects and ensure that all issues that may affect the implementation of a project are discussed and resolved prior to the projects’ inclusion in the Annual Procurement Plan and Annual Investment Plan (AIP).
observations.managementAction: Office of the Municipal Engineer and other partner Offices followed through and coordinated with the barangays regarding their project implementation, however, some chose to put on hold their projects until they have sufficient counterpart budget to finish the project; some projects were cancelled or put on hold due to the implementations of other government agency’s on-going projects; another factor was the BSK election which affected the projects to be implemented. The Office will do their best to immediately implement these PPAs in CY 2024.
observations.reason: There were unutilized funds that were due for implementation during the year, yet were not implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. submit to the Audit Team all the noted lacking documents on the grant and liquidation of CAs; and b. ensure that all CAs and related travelling liquidation reports are supported with complete documentations.
observations.managementAction: All noted lacking documents on the grant and liquidation of CAs were submitted to the Audit Team. The Municipality will ensure that all CAs and related travelling liquidation reports are properly supported with complete documentations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. analyze and identify the consultants whose services or functions could be performed by existing plantilla personnel to minimize the cost of Consultancy Fees incurred by the Municipality; and ensure that in the hiring of consultants for the completion of a specific project/program/activity, services to be rendered are highly technical or special in nature that require skilled experts in their respective fields to avoid unnecessary expenditures; b. direct the Municipal Accountant or any concerned Office to make sure that accomplishments of the consultants match with the services/deliverables required from them based on their contracts, otherwise, payments should not be made if there are unmet or unaccomplished deliverables/services; c. direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that complete and proper documentations were attached to the disbursement vouchers before processing them for payment; and d. direct other concerned officials to submit a written justification on payments made to consultants on months where the NOA, NTP, and contracts for their services were not yet executed.
observations.managementAction: The areas of concern has been based on the sound judgement of the Municipal Mayor considering the mission, vision, priority projects, essential services and additional level of expertise and attention needed beyond the optimum in-house capacity of the Municipality. The Municipality will be analytical and diligent on planning and procurement as early as possible for future needed consultancy services.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) require the Municipal Accountant, as the one certifying the completeness and propriety of supporting documents, to submit written explanation on why there are erasures/alterations on the above documents; and thereafter ensure that all attachments to the disbursement vouchers are properly accomplished, without erasures, to avoid doubts on their regularity; and b) require the Municipal Engineer to make sure that the NOA are properly received by the supplier with dates of receipt written in the document itself.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Engineer commented that erasures are treated as simple human error and that the Municipality will ensure to perfect supporting documents without erasures or seek the essential countersignature for any alterations and remind suppliers to write dates of receipt on documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and other concerned officials to prepare and submit the necessary requirements to enable the Municipality to include in its automated payroll system the payment of the wages of its Job Order workers.
observations.managementAction: The management processed the requirements of the bank and the Job Worker’s wages were paid through automated payroll system.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to ensure that necessary detailed engineering activities are conducted prior to procurement of infrastructure projects, otherwise any delay in the completion of project due to non-conduct thereof shall result to imposition of liquidated damages.
observations.managementAction: Necessary detailed engineering activities are already being conducted by the Municipal Engineering Office prior to its procurement of infrastructure projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Market Supervisor and Municipal Legal Officer to facilitate the execution of contracts between the Municipality and existing Binakayan Public Market stallholders, as implied by the pertinent provisions of the Comprehensive Market Code of the Municipality of Kawit.
observations.managementAction: Contracts between the Municipality and existing Binakayan Public Market stallholders were executed through the Municipal Market Supervisor and Municipal Legal Officer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairperson of the Technical Working Group - GAD Focal Point System (TWG-GFPS), with the assistance of the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, to establish and maintain a GAD database containing statistics and sex-disaggregated data systematically gathered and regularly updated to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation and subjected to gender analysis, in compliance with Section 4.1.B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
observations.reason: No COVID-19 vaccines were received in CY 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineering Office to conduct preliminary inspection and submit punch-lists to contractors to determine defects and thus make necessary corrections, before proper turnover and acceptance of the projects.
observations.reason: The Municipal Engineering Office was able to conduct preliminary inspection at 90-95 per cent of projects and prepare and submit punch-lists, if any, for necessary corrections, prior to the City’s turnover and acceptance of projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairperson of the Technical Working Group - GAD Focal Point System (TWG-GFPS), with the assistance of the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, to establish and maintain a GAD database containing statistics and sex-disaggregated data systematically gathered and regularly updated to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation and subjected to gender analysis, in compliance with Section 4.1.B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
observations.reason: The GAD database was established and maintained during the year.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their records to arrive at a correct cash balance every recording period; monthly.
b. Municipal Treasurer and all concerned personnel to refrain from using the cash balances of the 20 percent DF, LDRRMF and Trust Funds in the settlement of other expenses of the Municipality and to use these funds only for the purpose for which they were created as this may amount to technical malversation of funds; and
c. Moreover, correct the bank balances of the 20 percent DF, DRRMF, and Trust Funds to reflect the funds’ unexpended or correct balances, pursuant to existing budgeting rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction:
Reconciliation of cash balances between the Accounting Office and Treasurer’s Office were being performed every recording monthly.
The 20 percent DF, LDRRMF, and Trust Funds for CY 2023 were utilized for expenditures/purposes allowed by the relevant guidelines.
Appropriate adjustment was not yet made.
observations.reason: Not Reconciliation and verification of the discrepancies is still on-going.

observations.managementAction:
The GSO ensured that they will comply with all the recommendations of the Audit Team as to the necessary documents for the handling of semi-expendable properties of the Municipality.

Likewise, the Municipal Accountant commented that the noted observations and recommendations were highly appreciated and that they will do their best to comply. She also added that instructions were also given to those in charge to update the unit cost of each stock and to maintain updated ledger cards.
observations.reason: Not Preparation/ updating of SLCs is still on-going.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Health Office to submit all necessary documents of receipts and utilization of donations in-kind to the Office of the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Office to facilitate its recording and reporting; and
b. Municipal Accountant to record all receipts of donations/transfers in kind and its utilization in the books of accounts in accordance with Item 4A of Section 36, Chapter 3 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I and Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1, as follows: Appropriate asset account (e.g. Drugs and Medicines Inventory) and Grants and Donations in Kind.
observations.managementAction: The concerned RHU informed the Audit Team that all documents pertaining to the receipts and utilization of COVID-19 vaccines received through donation were already submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to make sure that all supporting documents that will prove that there are actual deliveries of goods or work accomplishments for services rendered are attached to the claims before recording these transactions as Accounts Payable. Also, make an adjustment for the transactions that were erroneously recorded in the Accounts Payable and revert the unsupported Accounts Payable to the General or Funds of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: All entries in the Accounts Payable account were accruals made for CY 2023 and were duly supported with AIR and other related documents as evidence that goods/services were incurred as of December 31, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and submit to him an action plan that will outline and discuss the legal measures and/or strategies the Municipal Treasurer’s Office will adopt or undertake to improve the collection of delinquent Real Property Tax (RPT) and Special Education Tax (SET) of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction:
The BPLO notified the concerned suppliers to provide written explanation why they did not fully declare their gross sales.
The Accounting Office had already furnished the BPLO with the List of Suppliers with business address in Naic, Cavite and the total payments of barangays in the Municipality to them for CY 2023. Moreover, the BPLO had required the taxpayers to submit documents to support the gross sales declared by them.
The Municipal Treasurer conducted a tax campaign and posted/distributed the list of delinquent tax payers in all barangays last April 2023.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Business Permit and Licensing Office (BPLO) to require concerned suppliers to provide written explanation, copy furnished the COA Audit Team, why they did not fully report their gross sales for purpose of computation of their business taxes and to institute appropriate action as such as refrain from transacting with these suppliers or blacklisting them from participating in procurement activities of the Municipality, if warranted;
b. BPLO, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, to use the information gathered from the list of payments made to contractors/suppliers who transacted with the Municipality and barangays in the preceding calendar year and other available evidences or documents, if any, to serve as one of the bases in the determination of income levels of various business establishments for purpose of calculating local business tax; and
c. Municipal Treasurer to enforce the collection of taxpayers underpayments of business taxes totaling P270,766.39 with penalties and surcharges, if applicable, pursuant to the rates provided in the Municipal Revenue Code, otherwise Notice of Charges will be issued by the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction:
The BPLO notified the concerned suppliers to provide written explanation why they did not fully declare their gross sales.
The Accounting Office had already furnished the BPLO with the List of Suppliers with business address in Naic, Cavite and the total payments of barangays in the Municipality to them for CY 2023. Moreover, the BPLO had required the taxpayers to submit documents to support the gross sales declared by them.
The Municipal Treasurer enforced collection and some of the concerned taxpayers had already settled their accountabilities to the Municipality.
observations.reason: Not Out of the total amount of Notices of Charge issued of P270,766.39, P108,284.90 were not yet settled as of March 31, 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the Market Supervisor to:
a. prepare and issue Contracts of Lease to the remaining 106 market stallholders and 17 Grand Central Terminal stallholders to secure the interest of the Municipality and to categorically inform the stallholders of their rights and obligations including the revocation of right for those with more than three months unpaid stall fees;
b. investigate and require the stallholders who sub-leased their stalls to explain their action and submit the pertinent report thereon to the Audit Team and henceforth, strictly enforce the provisions of the Market Code on the restriction on sub-leasing of market stalls and revoke the lease of non-complying stallholders; and
c. advertise and offer to the public all stalls which had been idle and abandoned for more than 30 days and strictly follow the process of awarding of stalls as stated in Section 40 of the Revised Municipal Market Code.
observations.managementAction:
The newly-designated Market Supervisor had already initiated the preparation of contract of lease for the lease of stalls in the Public Market and Grand Central Terminal starting in December 31, 2023. However, there were still 106 stalls in the Public Market and 17 stalls in the Grand Central Terminal being occupied but still were not covered by Contracts of Lease as the concerned stallholders had not yet visited their office for the contract signing and explanation of the contents thereof.

There was prior advertisement but the same was already removed due to apparent "expiration" of offer.

The said market rules were discussed to the concerned stallholders designated by the newly-designated Market Supervisor. The said amnesty were partially collected which was prescribed in Section 6.2 of Municipal Ordinance No. 05, or the Implementing Guidelines in the Operation of Grand Central Terminal.

The Market Supervisor did not grant any amnesty for unpaid stall fees since the effectivity of her designation.
observations.reason: Not all concerned stallholders had visited the Office of Market Supervisor for the contract signing; some stalls remained unadjudicated despite advertisement.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer to:
a) require the Contractor to submit a revised detailed “catch-up” program with weekly physical targets together with the required additional input resources in the order to fast track the completion of the project and avoid rescindment of the contract; and
b) intensify on-site supervision and evaluation of the project performance to at least once a week and prepare contingency plans for a possible termination of the contract or take-over of the work by administration or contract, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineering Office issued a Notice to the Contractor regarding the negative slippage and delays in the implementation of the project. Liquidated damage was imposed and paid by the Contractor. Appropriate measures were implemented by the concerned Office to minimize or avoid the same issue.

For CY 2023, the bidding documents already contained the GCG and SCC in accordance with the Philippine Bidding Documents issued by the GPPB.
For CY 2023, the As-Built Plans were already signed by the contractors.
For CY 2023, the submitted required forms of the contractors already contained the mandatory provisions stated in Item 6.0 of GPPB Circular No. 04-2020.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and other concerned officials to:
a) ensure that PPAs charged against the 20 percent Development Plan are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready by conducting extensive Preliminary Detailed Engineering (PDE) prior to its inclusion in the AIP to facilitate the timely implementation and procurement of the projects; and
b) coordinate with the DPWH and other government agencies prior to finalizing the Annual Investment Plan to make sure that the projects to be incorporated therein are not among those projects to be implemented by the said agencies.
observations.managementAction:
The recommendation was applied in the planning of projects to be implemented in CY 2024.
A statement: For CY 2023, five projects included in the original AIP to be funded under 20 percent DF for CY 2023 with total approved budget of P24,000,000.00 were re-programmed during the year.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a) Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) and other concerned officials to prepare and submit the necessary requirements to the Land Bank of the Philippines (LBP) to include the payments of wages of all Job Order workers and casual employees of the Municipality to the automated payroll system; and
b) also pay all allowances and other personnel benefits, such as Representation Allowance and Transportation Allowance (RATA) and overtime pay, of all elected and regular employees of the Municipality thru the automated payroll system to minimize the grant of cash advances to disbursing officers.
observations.managementAction:
Wages of all JO workers, except for Contact Tracers, were already being paid thru Automated Payroll System. Allowances and other personnel benefits of all elected and regular employees of the Municipality were already being paid thru Automated Payroll System.
The Municipal Treasurer had requested to the Land Bank of the Philippines, the Municipality’s depository bank, for the twice a week pick-up of deposits, which explained the low deposit frequency rate of the Municipality in CY 2023.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the Municipal Mayor direct the Market Supervisor to:
a. prepare and issue Contracts of Lease to the remaining 106 market stallholders and 17 Grand Central Terminal stallholders to secure the interest of the Municipality and to categorically inform the stallholders of their rights and obligations including the revocation of right for those with more than three months unpaid stall fees;
b. investigate and require the stallholders who sub-leased their stalls to explain their action and submit the pertinent report thereon to the Audit Team and henceforth, strictly enforce the provisions of the Market Code on the restriction on sub-leasing of market stalls and revoke the lease of non-complying stallholders; and
c. advertise and offer to the public all stalls which had been idle and abandoned for more than 30 days and strictly follow the process of awarding of stalls as stated in Section 40 of the Revised Municipal Market Code.
observations.managementAction: The Acting Market Supervisor commented that they will comply with the recommendations of the Audit Team. The newly-designated Market Supervisor had initiated preparation of Contracts of Lease and discussed relevant rules with concerned stallholders; some prior amnesty were partially collected.
observations.reason: Some stallholders had not yet executed contracts; some stalls remain sub-leased or idle. Implementation is ongoing.

observations.recommendation: a. direct the Municipal GSO and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records and to reclassify as semi-expendable property the PPE with acquisition cost of less than P50,000.00 per unit in compliance with COA Circular No. 2022-004; and b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the adjusting entries to reclassify the Semi-Expendable items to appropriate inventory accounts.
observations.managementAction: Management will reconcile the records with the GSO to reclassify as semi-expendable properties in the PPE with acquisition cost of less than P50,000.00. Adjusting entries will also be prepared to reclassify semi-expendable items to appropriate inventory accounts.

observations.recommendation: a. enjoin the Municipal Budget Officer, the Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to provide valid explanation or justification on the circumstances behind the expenditures beyond the appropriations amounting to P4,090,584.58 for evaluation of the Audit Team, otherwise a Notice of Disallowance will be issued, accordingly; b. direct the Municipal Budget Officer to strictly ensure that the actual expenditures do not exceed the budget for each class/fund and that they are utilized solely for the purpose for which they were created/intended; and c. require the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to closely monitor the balances of appropriations and to advise the concerned officials and employees to stop the incurrence of obligations when appropriations are no longer available for the purpose.
observations.managementAction: Management will provide valid explanation or justification on the circumstances behind the expenditures beyond the appropriations amounting to P4,090,584.58. The Municipal Budget Officer will ensure that the actual expenditures do not exceed the budget for each class/fund and that they are utilized solely for the purpose for which they were created/intended. Lastly, the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant will closely monitor the balances of appropriations.

observations.recommendation: a. cause the immediate liquidation of the fund to the CHD 4A CALABARZON in compliance with the pertinent provisions of the Memorandum of Agreement; and b. submit to the COA Audit Team for evaluation all lacking documents relative to the total payment of HEA to eligible HCWs and non-HCWs of P3,305,000.00 for the period May 1, 2022 to October 31, 2022, otherwise a Notice of Suspension will be issued accordingly.
observations.managementAction: Management will submit immediately the liquidation of HEA to CHD 4A CALABARZON to comply with the Memorandum Agreement. The Municipal Health Officer already submitted a letter request to DOH RO IV-A last May 23, 2024 to provide us all lacking documents for submission to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: a. to explain in writing to the COA Audit Team, for further evaluation, why the requirement of posting of performance security was not imposed of the subject procurement; and b. to ensure that henceforth, the posting of performance security and submission of the same, if warranted in the procurement activity, is adhered to by the winning bidders as required under Section 39 of the 2016 RIRR of RA 9184.
observations.managementAction: Management acknowledged that the performance bond was posted after the signing of the contract due to an administrative oversight and not an intentional deviation from the procurement process. Management has implemented a more rigorous review process to ensure that all necessary documents, including performance securities, are all submitted and verified before contract signing. Management will ensure that the posting of performance security and submission of the same, if warranted in the procurement activity, is strictly adhered to by the winning bidders as required under Section 39 of the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184.

observations.recommendation: Instruct the Municipal Accountant, Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer and other concerned officials to submit to the Commission on Audit Audit Team for evaluation all lacking documents relative to the total disbursements of P562,950.00 charged against LDRRMF, otherwise a Notice of Suspension will be issued, accordingly. Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all payments to suppliers are supported with complete documentation.
observations.managementAction: Management will submit to the COA Audit Team for evaluation all lacking documents relative to the total disbursements of P562,950.00 charged against LDRRMF. The Municipal Accountant will ensure that all payments to suppliers are supported with complete documentation.

observations.recommendation: Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all CAs and liquidation reports are supported with complete and properly accomplished documentation, CAs for travel expenses are given directly to the concerned individual and not to a representative and only duly authorized and bonded Disbursing Officer of the Municipality receive cash advances.
observations.managementAction: Management will ensure that all CAs and liquidation reports are supported with complete and properly accomplished documentation, CAs for travel expenses are given directly to the concerned individual and not to a representative and only duly authorized and bonded Disbursing Officer of the Municipality receive cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. instruct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare the Deferred Real Property Tax and Accounts Receivable (RPTAR) or certified list of taxpayers and the amounts collectible from them and to submit it to the Municipal Accountant who shall use it as her basis to set-up RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning of every year; and b. require the Municipal Accountant to adjust or correct the balances of RPT and SET balances of Receivables, Deferred Real Property Tax and the Other Deferred Credits by excluding the uncollected Fines and Penalties.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer prepared the RPTAR and certified list of taxpayers and submitted it to the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned BAC-TWG to provide written explanation on why the involved contractor was not disqualified despite non-submission of a valid business permit and henceforth, direct the Technical Working Group (TWG) to strictly adhere to the requirements, rules and guidelines on the post-qualification evaluation of bidders as provided under Section 34 of the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184, otherwise the relevant procurement and payments to the bidder, who was supposedly disqualified, will be disallowed in audit, with the participating BAC members included as liable persons.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant forwarded the valid business permit of the contractor to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the BAC to: a. submit immediately the lacking supporting documents for the procurement of the three infrastructure projects, subject to verification of the Audit Team, to avoid audit suspension and/or audit disallowance; and b. strictly comply with the documentary requirements enumerated in Items 9.1 and 9.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and Section 17.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 in the conduct of public biddings and processing of payments of infrastructure projects.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has submitted the five missing supporting documents and the photocopied DVs and the remaining copies previously submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the BAC to: a. submit immediately the lacking supporting documents for the procurement of the three infrastructure projects, subject to verification of the Audit Team, to avoid audit suspension and/or audit disallowance; and b. strictly comply with the documentary requirements enumerated in Items 9.1 and 9.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and Section 17.1 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 in the conduct of public biddings and processing of payments of infrastructure projects.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality has complied with the audit recommendations relative to the payments totaling P19,214,072.40.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. instruct the Municipal Treasurer and Information Technology Department Head to immediately coordinate with the computerized system service provider to review the system and recommend necessary upgrade or improvement thereon that will address the issue on the generation of detailed collection reports of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office Tellers; and b. require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to institute measures, controls and review process that will give assurance that the collections of the Tellers are recorded and classified correctly in accordance with existing accounting rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The generated reports of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office Tellers now provide information per individual OR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC - Municipal Engineer and other concerned officials to ensure that the required project signboards and/or public notices are displayed at the project location and the information therein such as start date of the construction, target date of completion, cost incurred, percentage of completion as of given date are complete and up-to-date.
observations.managementAction: The Management has ensured the required signboard/s and/or public notices.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer, in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant, to closely monitor the Implementation of projects with funding from the capital outlays for LDRRM or consider a thorough review thereof during the budget preparation to determine whether there is a need to revert and/ or reprogram the funds, subject to the approval of the Sangguniang Bayan upon the Mayor’s recommendation and compliance with other pertinent rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The LDRRMO coordinated with the MPDC and Budget Officer; the LDRRMO will ensure that the PPAs to be charged in the Continuing Appropriation and Special Trust Fund will be included in the LDRRMFIP.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Municipal General Services Officer (GSO) and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their respective property records to come up with the reconciled balances of PPE. Should reconciliation of the net inventory report and accounting records appeared to be improbable, consider availing the one-time cleansing of the PPE accounts in accordance with Commission on Audit (COA) Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; and b. Municipal GSO to use and properly accomplish the prescribed format of Report on Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) in the conduct and reporting of the annual physical count of PPE provided in Section 57 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: Reiterated in Observation No. 1, pages 33 to 35.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal Assessor, the Municipal GSO and other concerned officials, to identify all the land owned by the Municipality and ensure that all properties owned by the Municipality are accounted in the books; b. Municipal Accountant and/or the Municipal GSO to submit to the Audit Team copies of proof of ownership of one parcel of lot; and c. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal GSO, to investigate and/or locate the supporting documents of the portions of lot apparently sold to the Metrobank, and submit these documents to the Audit Team for proper derecognition in the books.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: The Municipal Mayor directed the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Assessor, and Municipal GSO to investigate and identify all land owned by the Municipality to ensure that all properties owned by the Municipality are accounted in the Municipality’s book of accounts, including the derecognition of land already sold. Moreover, the Municipal GSO already requested copies of cancelled titles in the name of the Municipality from the Registry of Deeds, including its supporting documents. Additionally, copies of the Deed of Sale on the acquisition of land in Bagtas, Tanza, Cavite were submitted to the Audit Team during the exit conference.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require all concerned officials/personnel with issued vehicles from the Municipality of Tanza, in coordination with the General Services Office (GSO), to: a. Present their vehicles to the Commission on Audit (COA) Audit Team for inspection; otherwise, unpresented vehicles will be deemed missing or non-existing, which may warrant further investigation from the Office of the Municipal Mayor and the COA Audit Team and the taking of appropriate legal action against the accountable employees; b. Attach the plate numbers and/or conduction plate/stickers of all motor vehicles of the Municipality and mark all government vehicles with the name of the Municipality for proper identification; c. Cause the registration of the 49 motor vehicles and the renewal of registration of the 52 other motor vehicles with the Land Transportation Office in compliance with Section 5 of Batas Pambansa Bilang 74; and d. Ensure that all vehicles of the Municipality are insured with the GSIS in compliance with RA No. 656.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: The Municipal Mayor directed the Municipal GSO to ensure that all government vehicles owned by the Municipality have plate numbers and/or conduction plates/stickers. Additionally, ensure that the vehicles were duly registered with the LTO and insured through the GSIS. Furthermore, the Municipal GSO was directed to coordinate with various offices to present to the COA the 29 vehicles that were not inspected during their ocular inspection.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to: a. Draw the necessary adjusting entry to record any unused/undistributed cane sticks and walkers to date in its appropriate inventory account; and b. Adopt the Perpetual Inventory Method in the recording of supplies and inventories and to recognize expense only upon issuances thereof to end-users supported with the submission of necessary supporting documents such as Summary of Supplies and Materials Inventory (SSMI) from the Municipal General Services Office (GSO).
observations.managementAction: Fully Implemented
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant commented that they Perpetual Inventory method in the recording of supplies and inventories was being applied. However, for this specific transaction, outright recording of supplies as expense was applied, therefore an adjusting entry was posted on May 23, 2024 to record the unused cane sticks and walkers in the Inventory account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. MSWDO and other concerned Offices to take proactive measures to collect the outstanding receivables such as sending demand letters or formal notice to debtors that payments are overdue and legal action may be pursued, if necessary, otherwise, analyze the effectiveness of the SEA program if non-payment has become a pattern among these beneficiaries; b. Municipal Accountant to provide allowance for impairment on expected losses from uncollectible or long overdue receivables pursuant to Paragraph 67 of IPSAS 29; c. Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to communicate with Postema Gasoline Station and Aqua Fojas Water Refilling Station on the overpayments made and immediately settle these accounts; and d. Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entry to correct the error that resulted to the negative balance amounting to P14,740.65 in the Other Receivables account.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: The Municipal Mayor directed the MSWDO to send demand letters to the debtors/beneficiaries of SEA, informing them that payments were already overdue. Moreover, the Municipal Accountant was instructed to communicate with the suppliers regarding the overpayments made, investigate and make necessary adjusting entries to correct errors in the books. Additionally, provide an allowance for impairment to the account for expected losses from uncollectible or long overdue receivables in the Other Receivables account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to: a. submit to the Audit Team all necessary documents such as Delivery Receipts, Sales Invoice, Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) and other pertinent documents to prove the validity of the recorded Accounts Payable totaling P1,100,132.29 in CY 2022, otherwise revert all unsupported Accounts Payable to the General or Funds of the Municipality to fairly present the balances of the affected accounts in the financial statements; and b. cause the immediate reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund of the long outstanding payables totaling P2,956,745.20 and P17,500.00 for the GF and SEF, respectively.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: The Municipal Mayor directed the Municipal Accountant to present to the Audit Team all supporting documents to validate the recorded Accounts Payable, and revert all unsupported Accounts Payable to its appropriate accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with concerned offices, to send letters to creditors and/or employees and/or inform them of their collectibles from the Municipality and if no response is received on the deadline given, immediately revert these amounts to the unappropriated surplus in accordance with COA Circular No. 76-45; b. Municipal Treasurer to obtain explanation from the Accountable Officer (AO) with overages in collection, and if the overages cannot be satisfactorily explained by the AO, forfeit the amount in favor of the Municipality by issuing an Official Receipt and record the overage as “Miscellaneous Income”; and c. Municipal Treasurer to demand from the AO the immediate settlement of their unremitted collections, otherwise deduct the amount from their salaries, and thereafter make sure that overages and/or shortages are settled immediately once discovered.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: The Municipal Mayor directed the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to send letters to the creditors and/or employees to inform them of their collection. As of May 28, 2024, the following Accountable Officers have settled their unremitted collections with the following details: a. Leilani Torres – OR No. 8735499 dated May 21, 2024 amounting to P400.23 b. Aubrey Tamio – OR No. 8735488 dated May 21, 2024 amounting to P10.00 c. Juanita Cerrero – OR No. 8735489 dated May 21, 2024 amounting to P0.55

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. strictly comply with RA Nos. 8291, 7742 and 7875 as amended by RA 9241 by ensuring that the amounts of all loan payments and/or contributions withheld are remitted in full to respective government corporations; and b. investigate the cause of the negative balances of the Due to GSIS and PhilHealth accounts to facilitate reconciliation and adjustments in the GSIS and PhilHealth records of the Municipality and individual employees and make necessary adjusting entries to correct the SLs with negative balances; and c. establish an effective control system in the recording and monitoring of the Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-IBIG and Due to PhilHealth accounts to ensure that entries made thereon are accurate, complete and reconciled with the source documents and to facilitate timely detection of errors.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: The Municipal Mayor directed the Municipal Accountant to investigate and reconcile the balances of Due to GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth accounts. Furthermore, establish an effective control system in the recording and monitoring of the said accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor require the: a. Municipal GSO to update all the Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) for all motor vehicles and transfer them to the personnel who are actually using the service vehicles to establish proper accountability; b. Municipal GSO and the concerned officials to observe the allowed number of vehicles provided in Item 4.2 of DBM Budget Circular 2022-1, unless assignment of more than one vehicle is justified as necessary in the performance of their official functions; c. Municipal GSO and the concerned Officials to limit the assignment of motor vehicles to the LGU officials enumerated under Item 4.4 of DBM Budget Circular 2022-1, and consider the provisions of Item 4.5 thereof on the common use of motor vehicles by other officials and employees in the exercise of their core function; and d. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the Municipal GSO, to compute the amount that must be deducted from the Transportation Allowances (TA) of personnel based on the actual days they use government vehicles.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: The Municipal directed the Office of the Municipal GSO to update all PAR and RPCPPE for motor vehicles to ensure proper accountability over these vehicles. Moreover, the motor vehicles assigned to various offices were for the common use of personnel in the exercise of their core functions. These were not exclusively for the use of the personnel under that office but for the use of personnel assigned to other offices as well. Furthermore, the Municipal Mayor instructed the Office of the Municipal GSO to draft guidelines and procedures on the Common Use of Motor Vehicles.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to: a. Formulate written policies/guidelines and limitations on the granting of mobile phone and mobile plans to public officials and employees taking into consideration the necessity of its usage and the reasonableness of the amount as approved by the local sanggunian; b. Limit the grant of either the mobile phones or mobile plans to avoid excessive expenditures and submit written justifications on the availment of mobiles phones in addition to the mobile plan subscription charged to the Municipality; c. Analyze the need to provide mobile phones to Officials already granted with mobile plan subscriptions and ensure that only qualified personnel holding the position not lower than the Division Chief or Department Heads were provided with mobile phone/plan, unless otherwise justified as necessary in their official duty and/or functions; and d. Require the Municipal GSO to record as Municipal property, the mobile phones included in the plan subscriptions of Officials, with gadget cash out charged against the Municipality, through PAR or Inventory Custodian Slips (ICS), whichever is applicable, since these Officials already have issued mobile phones in addition to their mobile plans.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented
observations.reason: The Municipality will formulate written policies/guidelines on the provision and use of official mobile phones and postpaid lines. Moreover, the Municipal GSO and the Municipal Accountant will record as government properties the mobile phones in the plan subscriptions of officials with gadget cash out charged against the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. BAC to ensure that the procedural requirements in the hiring of consultants, as provided under the 2016 RIRR of RA No. 9184 are strictly complied with; b. Human Resource and Management Officer and other concerned officials to analyze and identify the consultants whose services or functions could be performed by existing plantilla personnel and ensure that in the hiring of consultants for the completion of a specific project/program/activity, the services to be rendered are highly technical or special in nature that require skilled experts in their respective fields to avoid unnecessary payments of Consultancy Fees; and c. Municipal Accountant to ensure that complete and proper documentations are attached to the disbursement vouchers before processing them for payment.
observations.managementAction: Fully Implemented
observations.reason: The Municipal Mayor directed the BAC to ensure strict compliance with the procurement process and requirements in hiring consultants. Furthermore, the Office of the Human Resource and Management Officer was instructed to first analyze and identify the consultants whose services and functions could be performed by existing plantilla personnel. Moreover, the Office of the Municipal Accountant was instructed to ensure complete and proper documentations before payment of consultancy fee.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. create an Inventory Committee and supervise the conduct of complete physical count of all PPEs and prepare and submit the RPCPPE to ascertain the accuracy, validity, existence, and confirm the conditions of the recorded PPEs not later than January 31 of each year; b. require the Municipal Accountant and the General Service Officer to maintain an updated and complete Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards (PPELCs) and Property Cards (PCs), respectively, for all PPEs of the Municipality; c. require the Accounting Office and GSO to undertake the reconciliation of their respective records and make the adjustments where needed to arrive at the correct balance of the PPEs accounts; and d. enjoin the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary journal entries for the unrecorded depreciation expenses of PPEs using the straight-line method, depreciate it using the table of estimated useful life provided under Commission on Audit (COA) Circular 2017-004, and submit a copy of the journal entry voucher to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: The Management committed to comply with the Audit Team’s recommendation.
observations.reason:
No Inventory Committee was created nor physical count was undertaken. Hence, no RCPPE was submitted to the Audit Team for CY 2023.
Of the two accounts, only one was provided with depreciation. Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 1, Page Nos. 26-31.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the following: a. direct the Municipal Health Office to submit all necessary documents of receipts and utilization of donations in-kind to the Municipal Accountant to facilitate its recording; and, b. direct the Municipal Accountant to record all receipts of donations in kind and its utilization in the books of accounts in accordance with Item 4A of Section 36, Chapter 3 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I and Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.
observations.managementAction: The necessary documents were forwarded to the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the recording; and, The Municipal Accountant had recorded the receipts and utilization of the donated vaccines in the books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with responsible Municipal officials to facilitate the immediate submission of lacking documents to the Audit Team to facilitate the review of the validity and propriety of the disbursements.
observations.managementAction: The necessary documents were forwarded to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat to submit the lacking documentary requirements for procurement of various infrastructure projects charged to LDF, SEF and TF to establish the regularity, propriety and validity of the disbursements.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Secretariat had submitted the lacking documents to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the BAC Secretariat to submit the lacking documentary requirements for procurement of various supplies, materials and equipment charged to LDRRMF, GF and LDF to establish the regularity, propriety and validity of the disbursements.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Secretariat had submitted the lacking documents to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the BAC Secretariat to submit lacking documents pertaining to the subject expenditures charged to LDRRMF to establish the propriety, regularity, and validity of the disbursements.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Secretariat had submitted the lacking documents to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor order the BAC to ensure that the procedural requirements in the hiring of consultants as provided under the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184, particularly the responsibility of the BAC to negotiate with the consultants, validate their qualification and recommend their hiring to the Municipal Mayor, are strictly complied with.
observations.managementAction: The BAC adhered to the procedural requirements in the hiring of consultants as required under the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to coordinate with each other to facilitate the immediate return to the source agencies or National Treasury of the unexpended fund transfers totaling P825,131.55.
observations.managementAction: The unexpended fund transfers totaling P825,131.55 were returned to the source agencies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor demand the liquidation of outstanding cash advances amounting to P298,268.09 and file appropriate charges against the accountable officers if they refused to settle their cash advances; and that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to refrain from processing cash advances of elected officials for purposes other than his traveling expenses to avoid loss and/or misusage of government fund.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant had sent demand letters to those employees with outstanding cash advances. The Municipal Mayor was still granting cash advances for purposes other than his traveling expenses.
observations.reason: Of the total amount of P298,268.09, P27,924.11 or 9% were liquidated as of December 31, 2023. Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 8, Page Nos. 44-46.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to immediately remit the amount of P302,329.18 to the BIR and henceforth ensure that all taxes withheld be remitted to the BIR within the prescribed period to prevent incurrence of penalties and surcharges that may be imposed due to late remittances.
observations.managementAction: The amount of P302,329.18 were remitted to the BIR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineering Office to conduct preliminary inspection and submit punch-lists to contractors when they reach an on infrastructure accomplishment of ninety-five percent of the total contract amount to determine defects and thus, make necessary corrections, before proper turnover and acceptance of the projects to the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Preliminary inspection and preparation of punch-lists were conducted by the Municipal Engineering Office when they reach an accomplishment of ninety-five percent of the total contract amount.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: a. Inventory Committee to prepare a Physical Inventory Plan and accordingly conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of all PPE of the Province and prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) following the guidelines set forth in said COA Circular; b. General Services Officer to update the GSO’s property records based on the RPCPPE; and c. General Services Officer and Provincial Accountant to reconcile their respective property records to come up with the reconciled balances of PPE accounts to be considered as the correct balance of the PPE.
observations.managementAction: The Provincial Accountant and the GSO explained that the Cavite Provincial Inventory Plan, prepared by the Provincial Inventory Committee was approved by the Provincial Governor on January 31, 2022. A Provincial Government-wide orientation and briefing on the conduct of provincial inventory commenced on February 7, 2022. To date, the completion rate was roughly at 25%. Once completed, the committee will move forward with the one-time cleansing of PPE records provided under COA Circular 2020-006.
observations.reason: The Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) was only approved by the Provincial Governor in January 2022. A Provincial Government-wide orientation and briefing on the conduct of provincial inventory was conducted on Feb. 1, 2022. According to the plan, the physical count of inventory will commence on February 7, 2022. To date, the completion rate is roughly at 25 per cent. Once completed, the committee will move forward with the one-time cleansing of PPE records provided under COA Circular 2020-006.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Provincial Governor instruct the
Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of all PPE in accordance with the
PIP submitted by the latter and thereafter comply with the other guidelines on the one-time cleansing of
PPE records provided under COA Circular No. 2020-006, specifically on the
updating and reconciliation of the property and accounting records with the results
of the actual count of PPE.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the Head-General Service
Office (GSO) to present to the COA Audit Team the 36 laptops and other IT gadgets
which were identified by the latter to be still under the names of former officials and employees
for validation of their existence and to update the pertinent accountability reports of his
office, otherwise ensure that appropriate courses of action such as filing of request for
relief from property accountability to the COA or recoupment of the book value of the
properties are taken by the Province.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Provincial Governor
instruct the Provincial Accountant to complete the necessary documents and
comply with the guidelines prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005 to
facilitate the submission to the COA of the request for authority to write-off the
dormant receivables from the three municipalities amounting to P1,207,844.53.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the Provincial Accountant
to formally communicate with the concerned supplier to verify the claim and to
request for submission of proof of validity thereof, otherwise cause the immediate
reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund of the subject accounts
payable.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the OIC-Provincial
Treasurer and other concerned officials to prepare and submit the necessary
requirements to enable the Province to include in its automated payroll system the
PhilHealth shares of hospital officials and employees and other employees
benefits.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the Provincial Budget Officer,
the Provincial Accountant and the General Service Officer to coordinate with each
other so that all procurement of supplies are properly and uniformly identified,
classified and recorded in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts
provided under COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We also recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the Provincial
Accountant to record as inventories the goods procured for distribution/ consumption upon receipt and
to recognize expense only when the goods are distributed/issued to end-users or
beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor require the Provincial Planning and
Development Coordinator, Provincial Engineer, BAC-Infrastructure Projects and
other concerned officials to prepare a catch-up plan to fast-track the completion of
all delayed projects and thereafter consider the conduct of early procurement
activities and adhere to a specific schedule to effectively manage the
implementation of infrastructure projects.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the Provincial Accountant
and other concerned officials to make representation with the donors of the idle
funds to request their formal instructions or arrangement on what to do with the
subject funds.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Provincial Governor require the Provincial
Budget Officer, the Provincial Accountant and other concerned officials to ensure
that relevant records and reports on donations are prepared and submitted to him
or his authorized representative for reference and effective monitoring of their
receipt, utilization and balances.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the Provincial Accountant
and the Bids and Awards Committee-Goods and Services to submit to the Audit
Team, for further evaluation, the lacking documents pertaining to the subject
expenditures to avoid suspension and/or disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the Provincial Accountant to
immediately coordinate with concerned Provincial offices and employees to settle
their arrearages with the GSIS, including the penalties and/or interests incurred due
to late payments of premiums and loan amortizations.

Henceforth, we reiterated our previous years recommendation that the Provincial
Governor instruct the Provincial Accountant, in coordination with concerned
Provincial Heads of Offices, to strictly comply with the IRR of RA No. 8291 by
ensuring that the amount withheld from employees for their GSIS contributions and
loan amortizations are remitted in full to the GSIS on or before the 10th calendar day
of the month following the month to which the payments are due and applicable.

We recommended that the City Mayor create an Inventory Committee composed of adequate number of members to be able to complete the physical inventory in three months or less, and who shall be temporarily relieved of all their regular duties to devote their full time in the conduct of the physical inventory taking until the same is completed, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020. After which, it is recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. Inventory Committee to prepare a Physical Inventory Plan and accordingly conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of all PPE of the City and prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) following the guidelines set forth in said COA Circular;

b. General Services Officer to update the GSO’s property records based on the RPCPPE; and

c. City Accountant to submit a plan or proposal, including the logistics support her Office needs, to locate the source documents and old records of the PPE purchased prior to CY 2001 to facilitate the preparation of complete subsidiary records of all PPE and eventually, the reconciliation of the latter with the RPCPPE and Property Cards of the GSO.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the procurement of computer software, hardware and other services to the appropriate PPE, Intangible Asset - Computer and Government Equity accounts and to submit copies of the Journal Entry Vouchers to the Audit Team for review and validation.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to fast-track the installation of the RPT software procured by the City to facilitate the production of more reliable reports on RPT and SET collectibles from the City’s taxpayers.

We further recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to review and evaluate the SLs for the noted variance of P2,015,877.86 between the balances of said receivables in the SL and financial statements and coordinate with the City Treasurer to reconcile their records and effect the necessary adjustment in the appropriate report/record, if warranted.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to coordinate with the GSO, City Treasurer, CHO and CSWDO to validate the discrepancies noted in the audit and prepare the necessary adjusting entries based on the RSMI, RIS and pertinent supporting documents and submit copies thereof to the Audit Team.

We also recommended that the City Mayor require the GSO to conduct inventory on a quarterly basis and all department or office heads to prepare and submit to the GSO, and the latter to the Accounting Office, the RSMI supported with RIS for the issued inventories on a monthly basis and the RPCI for reconciliation with the reported inventory balance in the books.
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to immediately prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries for debit memos determined during the process of bank reconciliation.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:

a. City Accountant to submit to the City Treasurer a list of payments made to contractors/suppliers who transacted with the City Government in the preceding calendar year to serve as one of the reference or one of the bases in the assessment of business taxes in the current year; and

b. City Treasurer to collect additional business taxes of ₽35,444.89 plus penalties and surcharges, if provided under the City’s Revised Revenue Code, otherwise corresponding Notice of Charge will be issued by the COA Audit Team.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CGSO to immediately dispose of all unserviceable PPEs in accordance with applicable rules and regulations and thereafter submit the duly accomplished IIRUP together with the WMR and other pertinent documents to the City Accountant as a basis for dropping the property from the books of accounts and recording of proceeds from sale.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to prepare and submit to him an action plan that will outline and discuss in detail the legal measures and strategies the City Treasurer’s Office will adopt or undertake to improve the collection efficiency of the City, especially on delinquent taxes.
Henceforth, ensure that the City adopts the measures prescribed in RA No. 7160 and its IRR in the distraint of property.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Planning and Development Coordinator and other concerned officials to prepare a catch-up plan on projects that had been stalled due to Covid-19 related lockdowns in the country and henceforth, observe proper scheduling and institute necessary measures to mitigate the impact of the pandemic, weather disruptions and other risks in the implementation of on-going and future projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the CDRRMO and the City Planning and Development Coordinator to submit a catch-up plan for unimplemented and partially implemented LDRRMF projects to enhance the City’s capacity to prevent, detect and respond to disasters.

We also recommended that the City Mayor instruct said officials to specify under a separate caption in the LDRRMFIP the programs and activities to be funded by the unexpended prior years’ balances of the LDRRMF.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City GAD Focal Point System and the Technical Working Group to undergo trainings on HGDG to build their competence in the conduct of gender analysis of programs and projects for purpose of GAD budget attribution.

We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct all Provincial Department Heads to ensure that their respective offices conduct a physical count of all the unissued, undistributed or unconsumed supplies and materials in their custody as of year-end and correspondingly prepare the RPCI to facilitate the adjustment of the inventory records of both the GSO and the Provincial Accounting Office.
observations.managementAction: Provincial offices conducted physical count of supplies and materials in their custody as of year-end and prepared RPCI.

observations.recommendation: We further recommended that the Provincial Governor instruct the Provincial Accountant to complete the required documents and ensure that the conditions for write-off are already met to facilitate the submission to the COA of the request for authority to write-off of long dormant receivables amounting to P1,370,774.39, together with the necessary documents prescribed under COA Circular No. 2016-005, for evaluation of the COA.
observations.managementAction: The Provincial Accountant stated that their office was still in the process of completing the required documents. One employee in the Accounting Office was assigned to monitor the reconciliation of the subject reciprocal accounts. The Provincial Accountant requested all Accountants in the Province to furnish him with copies of SLs on a monthly basis. The Provincial Accountant has been coordinating and reconciling the due from/to LGUs accounts.
observations.reason: To date, the Provincial Accountant have not completed the necessary documents to facilitate the submission to the COA of the request for authority to write-off the long dormant receivables.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Provincial Governor direct the Acting Provincial Treasurer and the Provincial Accountant to study further and present recommendations on the various options available to the Province that will ensure that idle funds are placed into low-risk but high-earning investments in government depository banks and/or utilized to pre-terminate outstanding loans payables.
observations.managementAction: As of December 31, 2021, only P498,699.68 was retained under the Local Currency-Current Account (LCCA)-General Fund while the updated amount of P153,000,000.00 was transferred to the Time Deposit account-General Fund, in the same bank.
